title: Arithmetic function
snippet: An arithmetic function is a complex-valued function whose domain is the positive integers.

title: Bernoulli numbers
snippet: The Bernoulli numbers are the rational numbers $B_n$ that appear as coefficients of the formal power series \[ \frac{T}{e^T-1}=\sum_{n\ge 0}B_n\frac{T^n}{n!}, \] which has radius of convergence $2\pi$.

title: Divisor function
snippet: A **divisor function** is a multiplicative arithmetic function of the form \[ \sigma_\tau(n)=\sum_{d\mid n}d^\tau, \] for some fixed $\tau\in\mathbb{C}$.

title: Multiplicative arithmetic function
snippet: An arithmetic function $f:\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\to \C$ is multiplicative if $f(mn)=f(m)f(n)$ for all coprime integers $m,n>0$, and is not the zero-function (in particular, $f(1)=1$).

title: Abelian surface
snippet: An **abelian surface** is an abelian variety of dimension 2. Examples are given by the product of two elliptic curves, or the Jacobian of a curve of genus 2.

title: Abelian variety
snippet: An **abelian variety** defined over the field $K$ is a smooth connected projective variety equipped with the structure of an algebraic group. The group law is automatically commutative. An abelian variety of dimension 1 is the same as an elliptic curve.

title: Affine space
snippet: **Affine space** $\mathbb{A}^n(K)$ of dimension $n$ over a field $K$ is the set $K^n$. If $P=(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ is a point in $\mathbb{A}^n(K)$, the $x_i$ are called the *affine coordinates* of $P$. Thus $$ \mathbb{A}^n(K) = \{(x_1,\dots,x_n)\mid x_1,\dots,x_n\in K\}. $$

title: Algebraic group
snippet: TBA

title: Arithmetic genus
snippet: The **arithmetic genus** of a projective scheme $X$ is $p_a(X) = (-1)^{\dim X}(\chi(X)-1)$ where $\chi(X)$ is the holomorphic Euler characteristic.

title: Automorphism group
snippet: An **automorphism** of an algebraic variety is an isomorphism from the variety to itself. The set of automorphisms of an algebraic variety $X$ form a group $\mathrm{Aut}(X)$ under composition; this is the **automorphism group** of the algebraic variety.

title: Prime of bad reduction
snippet: For an abelian variety $A$ of dimension $g$ over a number field $K$, a **prime of bad reduction** (for $A$) is a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ of $K$ for which the reduction of $A$ modulo $\mathfrak{p}$ is not an abelian variety of dimension $g$ over the residue field at $\mathfrak{p}$. The number of primes of bad reduction is always finite; these are precisely the primes that divide the conductor of $A$.

title: Base change
snippet: Let $V$ be an algebraic variety defined over a field $K$. If $L/K$ is a field extension, then any set of equations that define $V$ over $K$ can be used to define an algebraic variety over $L$, the **base change** of $V$ from $K$ to $L$ (typically denoted $V_L$). An algebraic variety over a field $L$ is said to be a **base change** if it is the base change of an algebraic variety defined over a proper subfield of $L$, equivalently, if its base field is not a minimal field of definition.

title: Base field
snippet: The **base field**, of an algebraic variety is the field over which it is defined; it necessarily contains the coefficients of a set of defining equations for the variety, but it is not necessarily a minimal field of definition.

title: Bielliptic curve
snippet: A smooth projective algebraic curve $X$ defined over a field $k$ of genus $g\ge 2$ is a **bielliptic curve** if and only if it satisfies one of the following conditions: - $X$ admits a 2-to-1 map $X\to E$ defined over the algebraic closure $\bar k$ onto an elliptic curve $E$. - There exists an involution $w \in \Aut(X)$ defined over $k$ such that $X/\langle w \rangle$ has genus $1$ and $X/\langle w \rangle (k) \ne \empty$. - There exists an involution $w \in \Aut(X)$ defined over $k$ having exactly $2g -2$ fixed points. The involution above is called the **bielliptic involution**.

title: Canonical height of an abelian variety
snippet: Let $A$ be a principally polarised abelian variety defined over a number field $L$. The **canonical height** on $A$ is a function $$ \hat{h}: A(L) \to \R_{ {}\ge0} $$ defined on the Mordell-Weil group $A(L)$ which induces a positive definite quadratic form on $A(L)\otimes\R$. One definition of $\hat{h}(P)$ is $\hat h(P)= h_{\mathrm{Kum}}(\sigma(P))$, where $\sigma \colon A \to K$ is the natural map from $A$ to its Kummer surface $K : = A/\{\pm 1\}$, and $h_\mathrm{kum} \colon K(L) \to \R$ the canonical height pairing associated to a theta divisor $\Theta$ on $K$, which can be defined by $$…

title: Canonical model
snippet: The **canonical map** of a curve $C$ of genus $g$ is the map to $\mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ defined by the canonical class. For non-hyperelliptic curves of genus larger than 1, this map is an embedding, and its image is a normal curve called the **canonical model** of $C$.

title: Cluster picture
snippet: Let $H$ be an elliptic or hyperelliptic curve over $\Q$ given by an equation $y^2 = f(x)$, and let $p$ be an odd prime number. Then the **cluster picture** of $H$ at $p$ associated to this particular equation for $H$, is a graphical representation of the $p$-adic distances between the roots of $f$. The **size** of a cluster is the number of roots of $f(x)$ that it contains. The **depth** of a cluster, is the valuation of the radius of a minimal $p$-adic disk containing all the roots in that particular cluster.

title: Cohen-Macaulay type
snippet: The **Cohen-Macaulay** type of an order $R$, is the minimal number of generators (as an $R$-module) of a canonical module of $R$. Note that the trace dual ideal $R^t =\{ a : \mathrm{Tr}(aR) \subseteq \mathbb{Z} \}$ is a canonical module for $R$. In particular, the Cohen-Macaulay type equals $\max \{ \dim_{R/\mathfrak{l}} \frac{R^t}{\mathfrak{l}R^t} \}_\mathfrak{l}$, where $\mathfrak{l}$ runs over the maximal ideals of $R$. The order is called Gorenstein if the Cohen-Macaulay type equals $1$. This is the case precisely when every fractional $R$-ideal $I$ with $(I:I)$ is invertible.

title: Complex multiplication
snippet: A simple abelian variety of dimension $g$ is said to have **complex multiplication** (CM) if its endomorphism algebra is a CM field of degree $2g$, or equivalently, if its endomorphism ring is an order in a CM field of degree $2g$.

title: Conductor of an abelian variety
snippet: The **conductor** of an abelian variety $A$ over $\Q$ is a positive integer $N$ whose prime factors are the primes $p$ where $A$ has bad reduction. The power to which $p$ divides $N$ depends on the type of bad reduction; it can be expressed in terms of ramification in the $\ell$-adic representation associated to $A$ for any prime $\ell \ne p$. The conductor of an abelian variety over a number field is defined similarly; it is an ideal that is a product of positive powers of the prime ideals where $A$ has bad reduction.

title: Algebraic curve
snippet: An **algebraic curve** is an algebraic variety of dimension $1.$

title: Genus of a smooth curve
snippet: The **genus** of a smooth projective geometrically integral curve $C$ defined over a field $k$ is the dimension of the $k$-vector space of regular differentials $H^0(C, \omega_C)$. When $k=\C$ this coincides with the topological genus of the corresponding Riemann surface. The quantity defined above is sometimes also called the **algebraic genus** or the **geometric genus** of $C$. Because of our assumption on the smoothness of $C$, it coincides with the **arithmetic genus** $H^1(C,\mathcal{O}_C)$.

title: Smoothness of an algebraic curve
snippet: Let $C$ be an algebraic curve over a perfect field $k$. Then $C$ is called **smooth** if the extension of $C$ to the algebraic closure of $k$ is non-singular at all of its points.

title: Cyclic trigonal curve
snippet: An algebraic curve $X$ is a **cyclic trigonal curve** if there is an order $3$ subgroup $H$ of its automorphism group such that the quotient curve $X/H$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{P}^1$. A generator of such a subgroup $H$ is called a **trigonal automorphism**.

title: Dimension of an algebraic variety
snippet: The **dimension** of an algebraic variety $V$ is the maximal length $d$ of a chain $$ V_0 \subset V_1 \subset \cdots \subset V_d $$ of distinct irreducible subvarieties of $V$.

title: Endomorphism algebra
snippet: The **endomorphism algebra** of an abelian variety $A$ is the $\Q$-algebra $\textrm{End}(A)\otimes\Q$, where $\textrm{End}(A)$ is the endomorphism ring of $A$.

title: Endomorphism ring
snippet: An **endomorphism** of an abelian variety $A$ over a field $k$ is a homomorphism $\varphi \colon A \to A$ defined over $k$. The set of endomorphisms of an abelian variety $A$ can be given the structure of a ring in which addition is defined pointwise (using the group operation of $A$) and multiplication is composition; this ring is called the **endomorphism ring** of $A$, denoted $\textrm{End}(A)$. For endomorphisms defined over an extension of $k$, we instead speak about the geometric endomorphism ring.

title: Point counts of a variety
snippet: If $X$ is a variety defined over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$, one can show that it will have finitely many points that are defined over that field. Upon extending the base field to $\mathbb{F}_{q^2}$, $\mathbb{F}_{q^3}$, $\mathbb{F}_{q^4}$, etc. the number of points of $X$ defined over those fields remains finite, as long as we consider only field extensions of finite degree over $\mathbb{F}_q$.

title: Geometric component
snippet: A **geometric component** of a smooth scheme $X$ of finite type over a field $k$ is an irreducible component of the base change $X_{\overline{k}}$. (Because $X_{\overline{k}}$ is smooth, irreducible components are the same as connected components.) The set $\pi_0(X_{\overline{k}})$ of geometric components is a finite set with an action of the absolute Galois group $\Gal_k$.

title: Geometric endomorphism algebra
snippet: For an abelian variety $A$ over a field $F$, the **geometric endomorphism algebra** of $A$ is the $\Q$-algebra $\End(A_{\overline{F}}) \otimes \Q$, where $\End(A_{\overline{F}})$ is the geometric endomorphism ring of $A$.

title: Geometric endomorphism ring
snippet: For an abelian variety $A$ over a field $F$, the **geometric endomorphism ring** of $A$ is $\End(A_{\overline{F}})$, the endomorphism ring of the base change of $A$ to an algebraic closure $\overline{F}$ of $F$.

title: Geometrically simple
snippet: An abelian variety over a field $k$ is **geometrically (or absolutely) simple** if it is simple when viewed as a variety over $\bar k$.

title: Gonality of a curve
snippet: The **gonality** (or **$k$-gonality**) of an integral algebraic curve $X$ over a field $k$ is the minimal degree of a dominant $k$-morphism $X \to \mathbb P^1_k$. Equivalently, it is the minimum of $[k(X):k(t)]$ as $t$ ranges over (transcendental) elements of the function field $k(X)$ of $X$. The base extension of any dominant morphism $X \to \mathbb{P}^1_k$ is a dominant morphism $X_{\overline{k}} \to \mathbb{P}^1_{\overline{k}}$, so the geometric gonality of $X$ is less than or equal to the $k$-gonality of $X$.

title: Geometric gonality
snippet: Let $X$ be a geometrically integral algebraic curve over a field $k$. Let $\overline{k}$ be an algebraic closure of $k$. Let $X_{\overline{k}}$ be the base extension. The **geometric gonality** (or **$\overline{k}$-gonality**) of $X$ is the gonality of $X_{\overline{k}}$, that is, the minimal degree of a dominant morphism $X_{\overline{k}} \to \mathbb P^1_{\overline{k}}$.

title: Good and bad reduction
snippet: A smooth proper variety $X$ over a number field $K$ is said to have **good reduction** at a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ if it has a model over $\mathcal{O}_{K, \mathfrak{p}}$ whose reduction modulo $\mathfrak{p}$ is a smooth variety over the residue field; more precisely, $X$ has good reduction if it is the generic fiber of a smooth proper scheme over $\mathcal{O}_{K, \mathfrak{p}}$. Otherwise, $\mathfrak{p}$ is said to be a prime of **bad reduction**. The set of primes at which a given $X$ has bad reduction is finite.

title: Holomorphic Euler characteristic
snippet: The **holomorphic Euler characteristic** of a projective scheme $X$ is the alternating sum $\chi(X)=\chi(\mathcal{O}_X) = \sum_{i=0}^{\dim X} (-1)^i \dim H^i(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$. See also the arithmetic genus.

title: Hyperelliptic curve
snippet: A **hyperelliptic curve** $X$ over a field $k$ is a smooth projective algebraic curve of genus $g\ge 2$ that admits a 2-to-1 map $X\to \mathbb{P}^1$ defined over the algebraic closure $\bar k$. If $X$ is a hyperelliptic curve over $k$, then the canonical map $X \to \mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ is a 2-to-1 map onto a smooth genus 0 curve $Y$. The curve $Y$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{P}^1$ if and only if $Y$ has a $k$-rational point.

title: Irreducible variety
snippet: A variety defined over a field $F$ is **irreducible** if it is nonempty and cannot be decomposed as the union of two strictly smaller varieties over $F$. It is **geometrically irreducible** if it remains irreducible when seen as a variety over the algebraic closure of $F$.

title: Isolated point
snippet: A closed point $x$ on a curve $C$ is isolated if it is not contained in a family of effective degree $d$ divisors parametrized by $\mathbb P^1$ or a positive rank abelian variety.

title: Jacobian of a curve
snippet: The **Jacobian** of a (smooth, projective, geometrically integral) curve $X$ of genus $g$ over a field $k$ is a $g$-dimensional principally polarized abelian variety $J$ that is canonically associated to $X$. If $X$ has a $k$-rational point, then $J(k)$ is isomorphic to the group of degree zero divisors on $X$ modulo linear equivalence. A choice of rational point on $X$ determines a morphism $X \to J$ called an Abel-Jacobi map; it is an embedding if and only if $g \ge 1$, and an isomorphism if and only if $g=1$.

title: Kodaira dimension
snippet: Let $X$ be a nonsingular complex algebraic variety with canonical divisor $K$. For each $d \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$, the $d^\text{th}$ plurigenus of $X$ is $P_d \colonequals \dim H^0(X, K^{\otimes d})$. The global sections of $K^{\otimes d}$ define a rational map $$ \varphi_d: X \to \mathbb{P}(H^0(X,K^{\otimes d})) \cong \mathbb{P}^{P_d-1} \, . $$ The Kodaira dimension $\kappa(X)$ is defined to be $-1$ if $P_d = 0$ for all $d$, and $$ \kappa(X) \colonequals \sup_d \, \dim(\varphi_d(X)) $$ otherwise.

title: Minimal field of definition
snippet: Let $V/k$ be an algebraic variety defined over a field $k$ and let $S$ be the set of subfields $k_0\subseteq k$ for which there exists an algebraic variety $V_0/k_0$ whose base change to $k$ is isomorphic to $V$. Any field $k_0\in S$ that contains no other elements of $S$ is a **minimal field of definition** for $V$. In general, an algebraic variety may have more than one minimal field of definition; this does not occur for elliptic curves but it does occur for curves of genus 2.

title: Modular curve $X_0(N)$
snippet: Let $N \ge 1$. The *modular curve* $X_0(N)$ is an algebraic curve defined over $\Q$ (or even $\Z[1/N]$) whose points over any algebraically closed field of characteristic not dividing $N$ correspond to isomorphism classes of pairs $(E,\phi)$, where $E$ is an elliptic curve and $\phi$ is an isogeny with domain $E$ and kernel a cyclic group of order $N$, with the exception of particular points called *cusps* which correspond to degenerate such pairs. The complex manifold $X_0(N)(\C)$ is biholomorphic to the quotient $\Gamma_0(N) \backslash \mathcal{H}^*$ of the completed upper half plane.

title: Modular curve $X_1(N)$
snippet: Let $N \ge 1$. The *modular curve* $X_1(N)$ is an algebraic curve defined over $\Q$ (or even $\Z[1/N]$) whose points over any algebraically closed field $k$ of characteristic not dividing $N$ correspond to isomorphism classes of pairs $(E,P)$, where $E$ is an elliptic curve over $k$ and $P \in E(k)$ is a torsion point of order exactly $N$, with the exception of particular points called *cusps* which correspond to degenerate such pairs. If $N \ge 4$, the same description of points is valid even if $k$ is not algebraically closed.

title: Modular curve $X_{\text{ns}}^{+}(N)$
snippet: Let $N \ge 1$. The curve $X_{\text{ns}}^{+}(N)$ is the modular curve $X(\Gamma_{\text{ns}}^{+}(N))$ where $\Gamma_{\text{ns}}^{+}(N)$ is the normalizer of the non-split Cartan subgroup $\Gamma_{\text{ns}}(N)$.

title: Mordell-Weil group of an abelian variety
snippet: The **Mordell-Weil group** of an abelian variety $A$ over a number field $K$ is its group of $K$-rational points $A(K)$. Weil, building on Mordell's theorem for elliptic curves over $\Q$, proved that the abelian group $A(K)$ is finitely generated. Thus \[ A(K)\simeq \Z^r \oplus T, \] where $r$ is a nonnegative integer called the **Mordell-Weil rank** of $A$, and $T$ is a finite abelian group called the **torsion subgroup**. The torsion subgroup $T$ is the product of at most $2g$ cyclic groups, where $g$ is the dimension of $A$.

title: Plane model
snippet: A **plane model** of a smooth projective curve $X$ is a closed curve $f(x,y,z)=0$ in $\mathbb{P}^2$ that is birational to $X$. Such a model is **singular** if the zero locus of $f$ and its partial derivatives is non-empty, otherwise it is **smooth**.

title: Potential good reduction
snippet: A smooth proper variety $X$ over a number field $K$ is said to have **potential good reduction** at a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ if there is a finite extension $L \supset K$ such that the base change $X_L$ has good reduction at all primes $\mathfrak{q}$ of $L$ above $\mathfrak{p}$.

title: Primitive algebraic variety
snippet: An algebraic variety is **primitive** if it is not a base-change of an algebraic variety defined over a subfield.

title: Projective space
snippet: **Projective space** $\mathbb{P}^n(K)$ of dimension $n$ over a field $K$ is the set $(K^{n+1}\setminus\{0\})/{}\sim{}$, where $$ (x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n) \sim (y_0,y_1,\dots,y_n) \iff x_0=\lambda y_0, \dots, x_n=\lambda y_n\quad\text{for some}\ \lambda\in K^*. $$ This is also known as the projectivization of the vector space $K^{n+1}$. The equivalence class of $(x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n)$ in $\mathbb{P}^n(K)$ is denoted by $(x_0:x_1:\dots:x_n)$, and the $x_i$ are called **homogeneous coordinates**. Thus $$ \mathbb{P}^n(K) = \{(x_0:\dots:x_n)\mid x_0,\dots,x_n\in K,\ \text{not all zero}\}. $$

title: Quotient curve
snippet: Let $X$ be an algebraic curve and let $H$ be a finite subgroup of its automorphism group. The **quotient curve** $X/H$ is the algebraic curve obtained by identifying points of $X$ that lie in the same $H$-orbit (equations defining $X/H$ as an algebraic variety of dimension one can be constructed from the equations defining $X$ and the automorphisms in $H$). The natural projection $X\to X/H$ that sends each point on $X$ to its $H$-orbit is a surjective morphism

title: Real endomorphism algebra
snippet: The **real endomorphism algebra** of an abelian variety $A$ is the $\R$-algebra $\textrm{End}(A)\otimes\R$, where $\textrm{End}(A)$ is the endomorphism ring of $A$.

title: Real geometric endomorphism algebra
snippet: The **real geometric endomorphism algebra** of an abelian variety $A$ over a field $F$ is the $\R$-algebra $\textrm{End}(A_{\overline{F}})\otimes\R$, where $\textrm{End}(A_{\overline{F}})$ is the geometric endomorphism ring of $A$.

title: Real multiplication
snippet: An abelian variety is said to have **real multiplication** (RM) if its endomorphism algebra is a totally real (number) field. (In most situations, we exclude the trivial case of RM by $\Q$.)

title: Regulator of an abelian variety
snippet: The **regulator** of an abelian variety $A$ defined over a number field $K,$ denoted $\operatorname{Reg}(A/K)$, is the volume of $A(K)/A(K)_{tor}$ with respect to the **height pairing** $\langle -,-\rangle$ associated to the canonical height $\hat{h}$, i.e. $\langle P,Q\rangle = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{h}(P+Q)-\hat{h}(P)-\hat{h}(Q))$. If the Mordell-Weil group $A(K)$ has rank $r$ and $P_1, \ldots, P_r \in E(K)$ generate $A(K)/A(K)_{\text{tors}}$, then \[ \operatorname{Reg}(A/K) = \left|\det (\langle P_i, P_j \rangle )_{1\leq i,j \leq r}\right|, \] which is independent of the choice of generators.

title: Riemann surface
snippet: A **Riemann surface** is a connected complex manifold of dimension one. Compact Riemann surfaces can be identified with smooth projective curves over $\C$.

title: Sato-Tate group (of a motive)
snippet: The Sato-Tate group of a motive over a number field is a compact (but often disconnected) Lie group which conjecturally governs the variation of Euler factors of the L-function associated to this motive. For example, for (the 1-motive of) a generic abelian variety of dimension $g$, this group is $\mathrm{USp}(2g)$. For an elliptic curve $E$ over a number field $K$, the Sato-Tate group equals $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ if $E$ does not have complex multiplication.

title: Selmer group of an abelian variety
snippet: Let $A$ be an abelian variety over a number field $K$. Let $n \ge 1$. Let $G_K$ be the absolute Galois group of $K$. There is a connecting homomorphism $A(K)/nA(K) \to H^1(G_K,A[n])$, and likewise a homomorphism $A(K_v)/nA(K_v) \to H^1(G_{K_v},A[n])$ for each place $v$ of $K$. The **$n$-Selmer group** $\operatorname{Sel}_n A$ is the set of classes in $H^1(G_K, A[n])$ whose image in $H^1(G_{K_v},A[n])$ lies in the image of $A(K_v)/nA(K_v) \to H^1(G_{K_v},A[n])$ for every place $v$, including the archimedean places.

title: Shimura variety
snippet: There are a number of different ways to define a Shimura variety. One definition starts with connected complex manifolds such that for every point on the manifold there is an involution fixing only that point. If this manifold is isomorphic to a bounded open subset of $\C^n$ for some $n$, it is called a Hermitian symmetric domain (or space). Then, **(connected) Shimura varieties** are quotients of Hermitian symmetric domains by torsion-free subgroups of the group of automorphisms of the domain.

title: Simple
snippet: An abelian variety is **simple** if it is nonzero and not isogenous to a product of abelian varieties of lower dimension.

title: Non-singular point (definition)
snippet: Let $V$ be a variety over a perfect field $F$. A point $P$ of $V$ is **non-singular** if the module of differentials of $V$ is locally free at $P$. According to the Jacobian criterion, if $V$ is defined in a neighborhood of $P$ by affine polynomial equations $f_1(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \ldots =f_r(X_1, \ldots, X_n)=0$, then $V$ is non-singular at $P$ if the Jacobian matrix $\left( \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial X_j} \right)_{ij}$ has the same rank as the codimension of $V$ in $\mathbb A^n$.

title: Splitting field
snippet: The **splitting field** of an algebraic torus $T$ is the minimal Galois extension of its base field over which $T$ becomes isomorphic to a power of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{G}_m$.

title: Tate-Shafarevich group
snippet: The **Tate-Shafarevich group** of an abelian variety $A$ over a number field $K$ is $$ Ш(A) = \ker\Bigl(H^1(G_K,A) \to \prod_v H^1(G_{K_v},A_{K_v})\Bigr), $$ where $G_K$ is the absolute Galois group of $K$, and $v$ ranges over all places of $K$, including the archimedean places. It classifies locally solvable principal homogeneous spaces of $A$. It is a torsion abelian group that is conjectured to be finite.

title: Principal homogeneous space
snippet: A **principal homogeneous space** (or **torsor**) of an abelian variety $A$ over a field $k$ is a variety $X$ equipped with an $A$-action such that ($X_{\overline{k}}$ with the induced $A_{\overline{k}}$-action) is isomorphic to ($A_{\overline{k}}$ with $A_{\overline{k}}$-action given by translation).

title: Algebraic variety
snippet: There are two main kinds of **algebraic varieties**, *affine varieties* and *projective varieties*. Both are defined as the set of common zeros of a collection of polynomials. Let $K$ be a field with algebraic closure $\overline{K}$. An **affine algebraic set** is a subset of affine space $\mathbb A^n(\overline K)$ of the form $$ V(I) = \{P \in \mathbb A^n(\overline K) : f(P) = 0\text{ for all }f \in I\} $$ where $I \subseteq \overline{K}[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is an ideal.

title: Bézout matrix
snippet: Given two polynomials $f$ and $g$ of degree at most $n$, their **degree $n$ Bézout matrix** is the $n\times n$ matrix $(c_{ij})$ where \[ \frac{f(x)g(y)-f(y)g(x)}{x-y} = \sum_{i,j=1}^n c_{ij} x^i y^{j}.\] The determinant of this matrix is the resultant of the two polynomials.

title: Binary operation
snippet: A **binary operation**, on a set $S$ is a function $S\times S\to S$. If the operation is denoted by $*$, then the output of this function applied to $(s_1,s_2)$ is typically denoted $s_1*s_2$.

title: Associative binary operation
snippet: If $*$ is a binary operation on a set $A$, then $*$ is **associative** on $A$ if for all $a,b,c\in A$, $$ a*(b*c)=(a*b)*c.$$/div>

title: Commutative binary operation
snippet: If $*$ is a binary operation on a set $A$, then $*$ is **commutative** on $A$ if for all $a,b\in A$, $$ a*b=b*a.$$

title: Identity for a binary operation
snippet: If $*$ is a binary operation on a set $A$, then $A$ has an **identity** element with respect to $*$ if there exists $e\in A$ such that for all $a\in A$, $$ a*e = e*a = a.$$ Such an identity element $e$, if it exists, is unique and is thus called **the identity element** of $A$ with respect to $*$.

title: Inverse for a binary operation
snippet: If $*$ is a binary operation on a set $A$ having identity element $e\in A$, then an element $a\in A$ has an inverse in $A$ with respect to $*$ if there exists $a'\in A$ such that $$ a*a' = a'*a = e.$$

title: Skew-symmetric matrix
snippet: A matrix $M$ is skew-symmetric if its transpose equals its negation, i.e. if $M^T = -M$. In particular, a skew-symmetric matrix must be square.

title: Symmetric bilinear form
snippet: For a vector space $V$ over a field $F$, a **symmetric bilinear form** is a function $B \colon V^2 \to F$ satisfying 1. $B(x+w,y) = B(x,y) + B(w,y)$ for all $x,y,w \in V$; 2. $B(\alpha x, y) = \alpha B(x,y)$ for all $x \in V$, and all $\alpha \in F$; 3. $B(x,y) = B(y,x)$ for all $x,y \in V$. Conditions (1) and (2) imply that $B(\cdot,y)$ is a linear map in the first variable when $y$ is fixed. Including condition (3) then implies that $B(x,\cdot)$ is also a linear map in the second variable when $x$ is fixed.

title: Symplectic isomorphism
snippet: Let $N \ge 1$. Let $\mu_N$ be the group of $N$th roots of unity in some algebraically closed field of characteristic not dividing $N$. Let $M$ be a free rank $2$ $\Z/N\Z$-module together with an isomorphism $\alpha \colon \bigwedge^2 M \stackrel{\sim}\to \mu_N$, or equivalently with a nondegenerate alternating pairing $M \times M \to \mu_N$. For example, $M$ could be $E[N]$ for an elliptic curve $E$, together with the Weil pairing. Or $M$ could be $\Z/N\Z \times \mu_N$ with the "determinant" pairing $(a,\gamma),(b,\delta) \mapsto \delta^a/\gamma^b$.

title: Artin representation (definition)
snippet: An **Artin representation** is a continuous homomorphism $\rho:\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to\GL(V)$ from the absolute Galois group of $\Q$ to the automorphism group of a finite-dimensional $\C$-vector space $V$. Here continuity means that $\rho$ factors through the Galois group of some finite extension $K/\Q$. The smallest such $K$ is called the Artin field of $\rho$.

title: Conductor of an Artin representation
snippet: The **conductor** of an Artin representation is a positive integer that measures its ramification. It can be expressed as a product of local conductors. Let $K/\Q$ be a Galois extension and $\rho:\Gal(K/\Q)\to\GL(V)$ an Artin representation. Then the conductor of $\rho$ is $ \prod_p p^{f(\rho,p)} $ for non-negative integers $f(\rho,p)$, where the product is taken over prime numbers $p$. To define the exponents $f(\rho,p)$, fix a prime $\mathfrak{p}$ of $K$ above $p$ and consider the corresponding extension of local fields $K_{\mathfrak{p}}/\Q_p$ with Galois group $G$.

title: Determinant of an Artin representation
snippet: Given an Artin representation $\rho:\Gal(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to\GL(V)$, where $V$ is a finite dimensional vector space over $\C$, we can compose with the $\det:\GL(V)\to \C^\times$ to get a one-dimensional Artin representation. This one-dimensional representation is called the **determinant** of $\rho$.

title: Dimension of an Artin representation
snippet: The **dimension** of an Artin representation $\rho:\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})\to\mathrm{GL}(V)$ is the dimension of the associated vector space $V$.

title: Dirichlet character associated with a 1-dimensional Artin representation
snippet: A one-dimensional Artin representation $\rho:\Gal(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to \GL_1(\C)\cong \C^\times$. Since the image is abelian and every Artin representation factors through the Galois group of a number field, we can view $\rho$ as a map $\Gal(K/\Q)\to \C^\times$ for an abelian number field $K$. By the Kronecker-Weber theorem, $K$ is contained in a cyclotomic extension $\Q(\zeta_n)$ for some positive integer $n$. Using the canonical isomorphism $\Gal(\Q(\zeta_n)/\Q)\cong(\Z/n\Z)^\times$, the Artin representation corresponds to a homomorphism $(\Z/n\Z)^\times\to \C^\times$.

title: Frobenius-Schur indicator of a representation
snippet: If $G$ is a finite group and $\chi$ is the character of an irreducible complex representation of $G$, then its **Frobenius-Schur indicator** is given by \[ \frac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G} \chi(g^2).\] It is $0$, $1$, or $-1$ depending on whether the type of the representation is complex type, real, or quaternionic (symplectic) respectively.

title: Galois orbit of an Artin representation
snippet: The absolute Galois group $\Gal(\overline\Q/\Q)$ acts on the set of Artin representations for an extension by pre-composition, so we consider two representations to be Galois conjugate if they differ by such an automorphism. The action, for a given representation, in fact factors through a finite degree abelian extension. The **Galois orbit** of a representation is its orbit under this action.

title: Image of the representation
snippet: An Artin representation $\rho:\textrm{Gal}(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to \GL(V)$ factors through the Galois group of a finite extension of $\Q$. Thus its image is a finite group $G \subset \GL(V)$ which is canonically isomorphic to the Galois group of a finite extension $K/\Q$ where $K$ is the fixed field of $\ker(\rho)$ and is called the Artin field of $\rho$. We sometimes say that $\Gal(K/\Q)$ is the **image of the Artin representation** $\rho$.

title: Label for an Artin representation
snippet: The label for an Artin representation takes the form A.B.C.DcE where * A is the dimension of the representation; * B is the conductor of the representation, the format for which is explained below; * C is the smallest permutation representation for which this representation is a factor. If the degree is small enough, we give the degree d and t-number j in the form dtj; otherwise we use just the degree d; * D is an index to distinguish cases where non-Galois conjugate representations have the same dimension, conductor, and smallest permutation…

title: Artin L-function
snippet: The L-function associated to an Artin representation $\rho:\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb{Q}}/\mathbb{Q})\to\mathrm{GL}(V)$ is given by an Euler product of the form $\prod_p\det(I-\rho(\mathrm{Frob}(p))p^{-s})^{-1}$, where $\mathrm{Frob}(p)$ denotes the conjugacy class of Frobenius elements above $p$. When $\rho$ is ramified at $p$, $\rho(\mathrm{Frob}(p))$ should be interpreted as acting on the invariant subspace $V^{I_p}$ of the inertia group at $p$.

title: Number field associated to an Artin representation
snippet: The **Artin field** is a number field associated to an Artin representation $\rho:\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to\GL(V)$ by being the smallest Galois extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ such that $\rho$ factors through $\mathrm{Gal}(K/\Q)$.

title: Parity of a representation
snippet: An Artin representation $\rho:\Gal(\overline\Q/\Q)\to \GL(V)$ is **even** or **odd** if $\det(\rho(c))$ equals $1$ or $-1$, respectively, where $c$ is a complex conjugation.

title: Containing permutation representation
snippet: If $\rho:G\to \GL_n(\C)$ is a representation of a finite group $G$, then $\rho$ is equivalent to a direct sum of irreducible representations. We say that $\rho$ **contains** each of these representations. Given an irreducible representation, we can then ask which permutation representations contain it. There is at least one because the regular representation of $G$ contains all irreducible representations of $G$. To determine the smallest permutation representation, we pick one of smallest degree.

title: Projective Artin field
snippet: Given a projective Artin representation $\rho:\Gal(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to \PGL_n(\C)$, the fixed field of the kernel of $\rho$ is the **projective Artin field** of the representation.

title: Projective image of an Artin representation
snippet: Composing an Artin representation $\Gal(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to\GL_n(\C)$ with the natural projection $\GL_n(\C)\to\PGL_n(\C)$ gives a continuous homomorphism $\rho:\Gal(\overline{\Q}/\Q)\to\PGL_n(\C)$ with finite image. The image of $\rho$ is the **projective image** of the Artin representation.

title: Projective image type
snippet: The projective image of an Artin representation is a finite subgroup of $\PGL_n(\C)$. Possible subgroups have been classified for small $n$. The case which has garnered the most interest is when $n=2$, in which case the image is isomorphic to either $A_4$, $S_4$, $A_5$, or $D_n$ for some $n\geq 2$ (where $D_2$ is the Klein group).

title: Projective stem field of an Artin representation
snippet: An **projective stem field** is a stem field for the projective field of an Artin representation.

title: Ramified prime of an Artin representation
snippet: If $\rho:\Gal(\overline\Q/\Q)\to\GL_n(\C)$ is an Artin representation with Artin field $K$, then a prime $p$ is **ramified** if it is ramified in $K/\Q$. Equivalently, a prime is ramified if the inertia subgroup for a prime above $p$ is not contained in the kernel of $\rho$.

title: Root number of an Artin representation
snippet: The **root number** of an Artin representation $\rho$, also called the **sign**, is the constant $W(\rho)$ appearing in the functional equation of the associated L-function, i.e. the complex number $W(\rho)$ of absolute value $1$ such that $\Lambda(s,\rho)=W(\rho)\Lambda(1-s,\overline{\rho})$. It can be expressed as a product of local root numbers.

title: Find an Artin representation
snippet: To find a specific Artin representation, enter its label.

title: Artin stem field
snippet: An **Artin stem field** is a stem field for the Artin field of an Artin representation.

title: Trace of complex conjugation
snippet: For an Artin Representation, complex conjugation gives a well-defined conjugacy class $c$ in the Galois group, so it has a well-defined character value.

title: Unramified prime of an Artin representation
snippet: If $\rho:\Gal(\overline\Q/\Q)\to\GL_n(\C)$ is an Artin representation, a prime $p$ is **unramified** if it is not ramified. Equivalently, a prime is unramified if the inertia subgroup for a prime above $p$ in the Artin field of $\rho$ is contained in the kernel of $\rho$.

title: Automorphism group
snippet: An **automorphism** of an algebraic variety is an isomorphism from the variety to itself. The set of automorphisms of an algebraic variety $X$ form a group $\mathrm{Aut}(X)$ under composition; this is the **automorphism group** of the algebraic variety.

title: Decomposition into isogeny factors
snippet: Any abelian variety $A$ is isogenous to a product of simple abelian varieties $B_i$, called the **isogeny factors** of $A$: $$A \sim B_1 \times \cdots \times B_n.$$ We say that $A$ decomposes up to isogeny into the product of the $B_i$. Note that two elements of this product might be isogenous; in other words, elements of the decomposition may appear with multiplicity.

title: Deligne module
snippet: A **Deligne module** is a finitely generated free $\mathbb{Z}$-module $M$ together with a $\mathbb{Z}$-linear endomorphism $F$ inducing an action of the Frobenius order $\mathbb{Z}[F,V]$ of some isogeny class determined by a Weil polynomial $h(x)$ such that the action of $F$ on $M$ has characteristic polynomial $h(x)$. If the characteristic polynomial $h(x)$ is squarefree, than $M$ can be identified with a fractional $\mathbb{Z}[F,V]$-ideal in in the étale $\mathbb{Q}$-algebra $\mathbb{Q}[F]$.

title: Endomorphism field
snippet: The **endomorphism field** of an abelian variety $A$ over a field $k$ is the minimal extension $L \supset k$ in $\overline{k}$ such that $\End A_L = \End A_{\overline{k}}$. It is a finite Galois extension of $k$.

title: Conductor
snippet: The conductor $\mathfrak{f}_R$ of an order $R$ is the colon ideal $(R:\mathcal{O})$, where $\mathcal{O}$ is the maximal order. Recall that a maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}$ of $R$ is non-invertible if and only if $\mathfrak{f}_R\subseteq \mathfrak{m}$.

title: Isogeny class data
snippet: This knowl can display information about a particular isogeny class of abelian varieties over $\F_q$ specified by parameters.

title: (In)decomposable polarized abelian variety
snippet: A polarized abelian variety $(A,\lambda)$ is **decomposable** if there are polarized abelian varieties $(A_1,\lambda_1)$ and $(A_2,\lambda_2)$ and an isomorphism \[ (A,\lambda) \simeq (A_1,\lambda_1) \times (A_2,\lambda_2). \] If this is not the case, we say that $(A,\lambda)$ is **indecomposable**.

title: Angle rank of a set
snippet: For any set $\{\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_s\}$ of non-zero complex numbers, the **angle rank** of the set is the quantity $$\delta = \dim_{\Q}\left({\rm Span}_{\Q}\left\{ \frac{ {\rm Arg}( \alpha_i)}{2\pi}\right\} \cup \{1\} \right)-1.$$ The angle rank of a polynomial is the angle rank of the set of its non-zero roots, and the angle rank of an abelian variety $A$ over a finite field is the angle rank of its Weil polynomial. If $A$ has dimension $g$ then $0 \le \delta \le g$, and $\delta = 0$ if and only if $A$ is supersingular. We define the **angle corank** of $A$ to be $g - \delta$.

title: Bass Frobenius order
snippet: The Frobenius order of an isogeny class of abelian varieties over a finite field is **Bass** if all over-orders for the order $\mathbb{Z}[F,V]$ are Gorenstein, where $F$ is the Frobenius endomorphism and $V$ is the Verschiebung endomorphism.

title: Function fields of class number 1
snippet: There are exactly 8 isomorphism classes of curves over finite fields with trivial class group. Of these, 7 were identified by Madan and Queen in 1972 \cite{doi:10.4064/aa-20-4-423-432, MR:0306160}; it was claimed by Leitzel, Madan, and Queen in 1975 \cite{doi:0.1016/0022-314X(75)90004-9,MR:0369326} that this list is complete.

title: Conductor of an order
snippet: The **conductor** $\mathfrak{f}_R$ of an order $R$ in an étale algebra $K$ over $\Q$ with maximal order $\mathcal{O}_K$ is the biggest $\mathcal{O}_K$-ideal contained in $R$, that is, \[ \mathfrak{f}_R = (R:\mathcal{O}_K) = \{ x \in K :\ x\mathcal{O}_K \subseteq R \}. \] If $R\subseteq S$ the **(relative) conductor** of $R$ in $S$ is the biggest $S$-ideal contained in $R$, that is, \[ (R:S) = \{ x \in K :\ xS \subseteq R \}. \]

title: Conjugate stable order
snippet: The étale algebra $K=\Q[F]$ generated by the Frobenius endomorphism of an abelian variety over $\F_q$ comes equipped with an algebra involution induced by $F\mapsto V=q/F$. This is the **CM-involution** of $K$ and it is denoted by $\overline{\cdot}$. An order $R$ in $K$ is **conjugate stable** if $R = \overline{R}$.

title: Point counts of the curve
snippet: If an abelian variety $A$ defined over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$ is the Jacobian of a curve $X$, then from the data for $A$ we can produce a count of the (finite) number of points of $X$ defined over $\mathbb{F}_q$ and its extensions $\mathbb{F}_{q^2}$, $\mathbb{F}_{q^3}$, $\mathbb{F}_{q^4}$, etc. Even if $A$ is not known to be isogenous to a Jacobian, we can still perform this computation. In that case, we refer to the virtual curve associated to $A$, since we do not know a priori if the curve whose points we are counting exists.

title: Cyclic group of points
snippet: We say that an isogeny class of abelian varieties over a finite field $\F_q$ is **cyclic** if the group of points $A(\F_q)$ is cyclic for every abelian variety $A$ in the isogeny class.

title: Endomorphism ring notation
snippet: In the graph, each vertex correspond to an endomorphism of abelian varieties in the isogeny class and each edge correspond to an inclusion. For each endomorphism ring $S$, we use the following **notation** $[\mathrm{index}]^{\#\mathrm{weak}\cdot \#\mathrm{Pic}}_i$ : - **$\mathrm{index}$** is the index of the inclusion (of additive groups) $S\subseteq \mathcal{O}$, where $\mathcal{O}$ is the maximal order of the étale $\mathbb{Q}$-algebra $\mathbb{Q}[F]$; - **$i$** is a counter that uniquely identifies the order $S$ among all the overorders of $\mathbb{Z}[F,V]$ with index in $\mathcal{O}$…

title: Frobenius angles
snippet: Given an isogeny class of abelian varieties defined over a finite field $\F_q$, its Weil polynomial is such that all of its roots $\alpha_1,\alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_{2g}$ have absolute value $\sqrt{q}$. Therefore all of the information on the roots is contained in the quantities $${\rm Arg}(\alpha_i),$$ which we call the **Frobenius angles** of the Weil polynomial.

title: Correctness of Frobenius angles
snippet: The angle rank is computed by applying the LLL algorithm to the Frobenius angles computed to 500 bits of precision. While this provides a high degree of confidence, it does not constitute a rigorous upper or lower bound on the angle rank.

title: Frobenius Order
snippet: The subring $\Z[F,V]$ of the endomorphism ring generated by the Frobenius and Verschiebung endomorphisms of an isogeny class of abelian varieties defined over $\F_q$. The Frobenius order is an example of order in the étale algebra $K=\Q[F]$.

title: Galois group of an isogeny class of abelian varieties
snippet: The Galois group attached to an isogeny class of abelian varieties over a finite field is the Galois group of the splitting field of its Weil polynomial over $\Q$.

title: Group of points
snippet: The set of **rational $k$-points** $A(k)$ of an abelian variety $A$ defined over a field $k$ is an abelian group. If $k\subseteq K$ is a field extension, then we have an inlcusion of abelian groups $A(k)\subseteq A(K)$.

title: Honda-Tate theorem
snippet: The Honda-Tate theorem states that abelian varieties $A$ and $B$ over $\F_q$ are isogenous if and only if they have the same characteristic polynomial, and it characterizes the polynomials that can arise as characteristic polynomials of abelian varieties over $\F_q$ as being the Weil polynomials satisfying a condition on the multiplicities of factors. The condition is given explicitly in Waterhouse, "Abelian varieties over finite fields" \cite{MR:265369,doi:10.24033/asens.1183}. Moreover, $A$ is isogenous to an abelian subvariety of $B$ if and only $P_A(t)$ divides $P_B(t)$.

title: Index of an order
snippet: Let $R$ be an order in an étale algebra $K$ with maximal order $\mathcal{O}_K$. Then the **index** $[\mathcal{O}_K:R]$ is a finite positive integer.

title: Initial coefficients of the L-polynomial
snippet: The LMFDB allows one to search for isogeny classes of abelian varieties over finite fields by specifying the first few coefficients of the L-polynomial of the isogeny class. The coefficients are ordered in increasing order of the degree of the term, and the initial coefficients **do not include** the leading constant term 1.

title: $\Z$-conductor sum
snippet: Whether an order $R$ in an étale algebra $K$ can be written as $ R = \Z + \mathfrak{f}_R$, where $\mathfrak{f}_R$ is the conductor of $R$ in the maximal order $\mathcal{O}_K$ of $K$.

title: $\Z[F,V]$-conductor sum
snippet: Whether an overorder $R$ of the Frobenius order $\Z[F,V]$ can be written as $ R = \Z[F,V] + \mathfrak{f}_R$, where $\mathfrak{f}_R$ is the conductor of $R$ in the maximal order $\mathcal{O}_K$ of the étale algebra $K=\Q[F]$.

title: Isomorphism classes within an isogeny class
snippet: Each isogeny class of abelian varieties is divided into isomorphism classes. Since some of these abelian varieties might not be principally polarizable, isomorphism means isomorphism of *unpolarized* abelian varieties.

title: L-polynomial of an abelian variety
snippet: Let $A$ be an abelian variety of dimension $g$ defined over $\F_q$. Let $F_q$ be the inverse of the field automorphism $x \mapsto x^q$ in $\Gal(\overline{\F}_q/\F_q)$, which acts on $\ell$-adic \'etale cohomology. The **L-polynomial** of $A$ is $$L_A(t) = \det(1-t F_q|H^1(A_{\overline{\F}_q}, \Q_\ell)).$$ This is a polynomial of degree $2g$ with integer coefficients that are independent of $\ell$. Its constant term is $1$. The L-polynomial $L_A(t)$ is the reverse of the characteristic polynomial $P_A(t)$, which is a Weil $q$-polynomial.

title: Labels for isogeny classes of abelian varieties over finite fields
snippet: The LMFDB uses a systematic system to label isogeny classes, endomorphism ring, weak equivalence classes, Deligne module, and polarizations defined over finite fields. The **label** format for an isogeny class defined over a finite field is **g.q.isog**, where - **$g$** is the dimension of the abelian varieties contained in the isogeny class, - **$q$** is the cardinality of the field over which the abelian varieties and the isogenies are defined, and - **isog** specifies the isogeny class.

title: Maximum Cohen-Macaulay
snippet: The maximum Cohen-Macaulay type of the overorders of the Frobenius order of the isogeny class.

title: Maximal Frobenius order
snippet: The Frobenius order of the abelian variety is **maximal** if it equals $\mathcal{O}_K$.

title: Newton polygon elevation
snippet: The Newton polygon $P$ of an abelian variety of dimension $g$ over a finite field is a piecewise linear function satisfying the following conditions: * Its left endpoint is $(0,0)$ and its right endpoint is $(2g, g)$. * Its vertices are nonnegative integer lattice points. * Its vertices are symmetric: $(i,j)$ is a vertex if and only if $(2g-i, g-j)$ is. We call the set of polygons satisfying these conditions **eligible**. The **elevation** of such a polygon is the number of nonnegative lattice points $(x,y)$ with $0 \le x \le g$ lying strictly below $P$.

title: Non-cyclic primes
snippet: We say that an isogeny class of abelian varieties over a finite field $\F_q$ is **non-cyclic at $\ell$** if the $\ell$-primary part of the group of points $A(\F_q)$ is non-cyclic for some abelian variety $A$ in the isogeny class. If an isogeny class is not cyclic then there exists at least one prime $\ell$ so that it is non-cyclic at $\ell$.

title: Number field of an isogeny class of abelian varieties
snippet: If the Weil polynomial of an isogeny class of abelian varieties defined over a finite field is irreducible, then the **number field** attached to this isogeny class is the finite extension of $\Q$ defined by this irreducible polynomial.

title: Abelian varieties with one rational point
snippet: The classification (up to isogeny) of simple abelian varieties over finite fields with only one rational point has been given by Madan-Pal \cite{MR:0439848,ZBL:0339.14028,doi:10.1515/crll.1977.291.78}. It consists of an infinite family of examples over $\F_2$, together with six sporadic examples over $\F_2$ and one each over $\F_3$ and $\F_4$.

title: Order in an étale algebra
snippet: An **étale algebra** over $\Q$ is a finite product of number fields. An **order** $R$ in an étale algebra $K$ over $\Q$ is a subring $R$ of $K$ whose underlying additive group is free of maximal rank, that is with rank equal to $\dim_\Q K$. If $R$ and $S$ are orders satisfying $R \subseteq S$, we say that $S$ is an **overorder** of $R$. The product $\mathcal{O}_K$ of the ring of integers of the number fields that are the components of $K$ is called the **maximal order** of $K$. It contains every order in $K$. The Frobenius order of an isogeny class is an example of order.

title: Ordinary
snippet: An abelian variety of dimension $g$ defined over a finite field $\F_q$ with $q = p^r$ is **ordinary** if it has $p$-rank equal to $g$.

title: p-rank
snippet: Let $A$ be a $g$-dimensional abelian variety over $\F_q$ where $q=p^r$. The **$p$-rank** of $A$ is the dimension of the geometric $p$-torsion as an $\F_p$-vector space: $$p\operatorname{-rank}(A) = \dim_{\F_p}( A(\overline{\F}_p)[p] ).$$ The $p$-rank is at most $g$, with equality if and only if $A$ is ordinary. The difference between $g$ and the $p$-rank is the **$p$-corank** of $A$.

title: Picard Group
snippet: Let $S$ be an order in an étale algebra $K$ over $\Q$. The **Picard group** $\Pic(S)$ of $S$ is the group of invertible fractional $S$-ideals modulo $S$-linear isomorphism. The group operation is ideal multiplication. The Picard group $\Pic(\mathcal{O}_K)$ of the ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$ of a number field $K$ is also referred to as the ideal class group of $K$. Its size is the class number of $K$.

title: Find an isogeny class of abelian varieties
snippet: To find a specific isogeny class of abelian varieties over a finite field, enter its label, Weil polynomial, or L-polynomial.

title: Dimension of a fractional ideal at residue fields
snippet: Let $R$ be an order in an étale algebra $K$ and $I$ a fractional $R$-ideal. For every prime $\mathfrak{P}$ of $R$, the quotient $I/\mathfrak{P} I$ is a finite dimensional vector space over the finite field $R/\mathfrak{P}$. This dimension is $1$ if and only if the localization $I_\mathfrak{P}$ of $I$ at $\mathfrak{P}$ is a principal $R_\mathfrak{P}$-ideal. The ideal $I$ is **invertible** if and only if it is **locally principal** at every prime of $R$. If the ideal $I$ is not locally principal at $\mathfrak{P}$ then $\mathfrak{P}$ is a singular prime of $R$.

title: Singular primes and singular support
snippet: A maximal ideal, or prime, $\mathfrak{P}$ of an order $R$ in an étale algebra over $\Q$ is a **singular primes** if it divides the conductor $\mathfrak{f}_R=(R:\mathcal{O}_K)$ of $R$. The **singular support** of an overorder $S$ of the Frobenius order $\Z[F,V]$ is the set of singular primes of $\Z[F,V]$ that contain the relative conductor $(\Z[F,V]:S)$.

title: Supersingular abelian variety
snippet: An abelian variety defined over a finite field is **supersingular** if all of the slopes of the Newton polygon of its associated Weil polynomial have value $1/2$.

title: Weak equivalence class
snippet: Two fractional $S$-ideals $I$ and $J$ are in the same **weak equivalence class** if $I_\mathfrak{l}=I\otimes_S S_\mathfrak{l}$ and $J_\mathfrak{l}=J\otimes_S S_\mathfrak{l}$ are isomorphic as $S_\mathfrak{l}$-modules for every maximal ideal $\mathfrak{l}$ of $S$. In the literature, weak equivalence classes are also called local isomorphism classes of genera.

title: Characteristic polynomial of an abelian variety over a finite field
snippet: Let $A$ be an abelian variety of dimension $g$ defined over $\F_q$. Fix a prime $\ell \nmid q$. The $q$-power Frobenius endomorphism $F$ of $A$ induces an endomorphism of the Tate module $T_\ell A$. The **characteristic polynomial** (or **Weil polynomial**) of $A$ is the characteristic polynomial of the latter endomorphism: $$P_A(t) = \det(t-F|T_\ell A).$$ It is a monic polynomial of degree $2g$ with integer coefficients that are independent of $\ell$. It is the reverse of the $L$-polynomial. By a theorem of Weil, $P_A(t)$ is a Weil $q$-polynomial.

title: Index of maximal order
snippet: The **index** of the Frobenius order is the index of the ring $\mathbb{Z}[F,V]$ in $\mathcal{O}_K$, where $F$ is the Frobenius endomorphism and $V$ is the Verschiebung endomorphism.

title: Index of Real Order
snippet: The **real index** is the index of $\mathbb{Z}[F+V]$ in the totally real ring $\mathcal{O}_{K^+}$, where $F$ is the Frobenius endomorphism, $V$ is the Verschiebung endomorphism, and $K^+=\mathbb{Q}[F+V]$ is the totally real subfield of $K=\mathbb{Q}[F,V]$.

title: Relative discriminant norm
snippet: The **relative discriminant** norm is $N_{K/\mathbb{Q}}(F-V)$, where $F$ is the Frobenius endomorphism, $V$ is the Verschiebung endomorphism, and $K=\mathbb{Q}[F,V]$. For this value, we have $$|N_{K/\mathbb{Q}}(F-V)|=|\Delta_R|/\Delta_{R^+}^2,$$ where $R=\mathbb{Q}[F,V]$ is the Frobenius order and $R^+=\mathbb{Z}[F+V]$ is the real order.

title: Galois representations attached to an abelian variety
snippet: If $A$ is an abelian variety defined over a field $K$ and $m$ is a positive integer, then the **mod-$m$ Galois representation** attached to $A$ is the continuous homomorphism \[ \overline\rho_{A,m}: \Gal(\overline{K}/K) \to \Aut(A[m]) \] describing the action of the absolute Galois group of $K$ on the $m$-torsion subgroup $A[m]$. When the characteristic of $K$ does not divide $m > 1$, we may identify the finite abelian group $A[m]$ with $(\Z/m\Z)^{2g}$.

title: Geometrically simple
snippet: An abelian variety is **geometrically simple** if its base change to an algebraic closure of the ground field is simple.

title: Geometrically squarefree abelian variety
snippet: An abelian variety is **geometrically squarefree** if its base change to an algebraic closure of the ground field is squarefree.

title: Number of hyperelliptic Jacobians
snippet: The number of Jacobians of hyperelliptic curves in each isogeny class (up to isomorphism of principally polarized abelian varieties) has been computed for $g \le 2$ and $q \le 211$; and for $g = 3$ and $q \le 13$ that are either prime or odd.

title: Product abelian variety
snippet: Given two abelian variety $A_1$ and $A_2$ over the same field, one can construct the **product abelian variety** $A_1\times A_2$.

title: Isogeny of abelian varieties
snippet: An **isogeny** of abelian varieties is a surjective algebraic group homomorphism with finite kernel. Two abelian varieties are **isogenous** if there is an isogeny between them. This defines an equivalence relation on the set of isomorphism classes. Equivalence classes are called isogeny classes.

title: Isogeny class of an abelian variety
snippet: The **isogeny class** of an abelian variety $A$ over a field $K$ is the set of isomorphism classes of abelian varieties over $K$ that are isogenous to $A$ (over $K$). When $K$ is a number field, LMFDB entries for isogeny classes typically list only those elements of the isogeny class that are present in the LMFDB; for abelian varieties of dimension greater than 1, this means that the list of elements of the isogeny class will often be incomplete.

title: Number of Jacobians
snippet: The number of Jacobians in each isogeny class (up to isomorphism of principally polarized abelian varieties) has been computed for $g \le 2$ and $q \le 211$; and for $g = 3$ and $q \le 5$.

title: Polarization
snippet: A **polarization** of an abelian variety $A$ is an isogeny from $A$ to its dual abelian variety that is symmetric with respect to double-duality for abelian varieties and for which the pullback of the Poincar\'{e} bundle along the associated graph morphism is ample. The **degree** (resp. **kernel**) of a polarization is the degree (resp. kernel) of this isogeny. Any abelian variety admits at least one polarization. In fact, over $\C$ the existence of a polarization distinguishes abelian varieties among complex tori.

title: Potential toric rank
snippet: Let $A$ be an abelian variety over a number field $K$, and let $\mathfrak{p}$ be a prime of $K$. Then after a finite extension $K \subset L$, there is an integral model of $A$ whose reduction is a semiabelian variety, i.e. the extension of an abelian variety by a torus. The **potential toric rank** of $A$ at $\mathfrak{p}$ is the dimension of that torus. As a special case, $A$ has potential good reduction if and only if its potential toric rank is 0.

title: Principally polarizable
snippet: An abelian variety is **principally polarizable** if it admits a polarization that is an isomorphism. For example, the Jacobian of a curve admits a canonical principal polarization; by the Torelli theorem, the curve is uniquely determined by its Jacobian together with this polarization. An isogeny class of abelian varieties is principally polarizable if one of its members admits a principal polarization.

title: Semiabelian variety
snippet: A **semiabelian variety** is a commutative group variety which is an extension of an abelian variety by a torus.

title: Simple abelian variety
snippet: An abelian variety is **simple** if it is nonzero and not isogenous to a product of abelian varieties of lower dimension.

title: Squarefree abelian variety
snippet: An abelian variety is **squarefree** if, in its isogeny decomposition, no simple factor occurs up to isogeny with exponent larger than 1.

title: Tate module of an abelian variety
snippet: Let $p \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ be a prime and $A$ an abelian variety of dimension $g$ defined over a field $K$. The **$p$-adic Tate module** of $A$ is the inverse limit \[ T_p(A) = \lim_{\xleftarrow[n \in \mathbb{N}]{}} A[p^n]. \] Here for $m\in\mathbb{Z}_{> 0}$, $A[m]$ denotes the $m$-torsion subgroup of $A$, which is the kernel of the multiplication-by-$m$ isogeny of $A$. If $K$ has characteristic not equal to $p$, then $T_p(A)$ is a free $\Z_p$-module of rank $2g$. It carries an action of the absolute Galois group of $K$, and thus has an associated Galois representation.

title: Theta divisor on a principally polarized abelian variety
snippet: Let $A$ be a principally polarized abelian variety of dimension $g$ over a field $k$. A **theta divisor** is an effective divisor $\Theta$ on $A_{\overline{k}}$ whose associated homomorphism $A_{\overline{k}} \to A^\vee_{\overline{k}}$ is the principal polarization. It is determined up to translation on $A_{\overline{k}}$. Any theta divisor is ample. The divisor $[-1]^* \Theta$ is another theta divisor, hence a translate of $\Theta$. If $\operatorname{char} k \ne 2$, then there are exactly $2^{2g}$ translates of $\Theta$ that are **symmetric**, meaning that $[-1]^* \Theta = \Theta$.

title: Twist of an abelian variety
snippet: A **twist** of an abelian variety $A$ is an abelian variety $A'$ over the same field that becomes isomorphic to $A$ upon base change to an algebraic closure.

title: weak equivalence
snippet: Let $R$ be an order in an étale $\mathbf{Q}$-algebra. Two fractional $R$-ideals $I$ and $J$ are **weakly equivalent** if $I_\mathfrak{p}\simeq J_\mathfrak{p}$ as $R_\mathfrak{p}$-modules for every maximal ideal $\mathfrak{p}$ of $R$. If $I$ and $J$ are weakly equivalent then $(I:I)=(J:J)$. Such ideals are also said to be locally isomorphic or in the same genus.

title: abc triple for Belyi maps
snippet: The **$(a,b,c)$ triple** for a Belyi map is obtained by taking the orders of the elements in the corresponding transitive permutation triple.

title: Base field of a Belyi map
snippet: The **base field** of a Belyi map $\phi:X\to\mathbb{P}^1$ is the field $X$ and $\phi$ are defined over. (In the database, we take this base field to be the field of moduli whenever possible.)

title: Degree of a Belyi map
snippet: The **degree** of a Belyi map $\phi:X\to\mathbb{P}^1$ is the degree of $\phi$ as a finite map of curves.

title: Belyi map
snippet: A **Belyi map** over a field $K$ of characteristic zero is a finite morphism $\phi\colon X\to\mathbb{P}^1$ of (smooth, projective, geometrically irreducible) curves over $K$ that is unramified outside $\{0,1,\infty\}$.

title: Genus of a Belyi map
snippet: The **genus** of a Belyi map $\phi:X\to\mathbb{P}^1$ is the genus of the source $X$.

title: Geometry type of a Belyi map
snippet: A Belyi map corresponds to a finite index subgroup of a triangle group $\Delta(a,b,c)$. The **geometry type** of a Belyi map is spherical, Euclidean, or hyperbolic according to $\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}$ being $>1$, $=1$, or $<1$, respectively.

title: Monodromy group of a Belyi map
snippet: The **monodromy group** of a Belyi map $\phi \colon X \to \mathbb{P}^1$ of degree $d$ is the transitive subgroup of $S_d$ generated by its associated transitive permutation triple $\sigma$. This group is the geometric monodromy group of the branched cover $\phi$, or equivalently it is the geometric Galois group of the corresponding extension of function fields $K(X) \supseteq K(\phi) \simeq K(\mathbb{P}^1)$.

title: Belyi map label
snippet: The **label** of a Belyi map $\phi$ has the form $d$T$G$-$\lambda_0\_\lambda_1\_\lambda_\infty$-m encoding the following data. - $d$T$G$ is the transitive group label of the monodromy group of $\phi$. Here $d$ is the degree of $\phi$. - $\lambda_i$ are partitions of $d$ specifying the ramification type of $\phi$, each written using `.` as a separator between the parts of the partition. - m is the Galois orbit label, a non-negative integer written in base 26 using the 26 symbols a, b, ..., z.

title: Number of Galois orbits
snippet: A passport decomposes as a disjoint union of $k$ Galois orbits. We denote the number $k$ by **number of Galois orbits**.

title: Orbit size
snippet: The **orbit size** is the number of isomorphism classes of Belyi maps contained in a given Galois orbit.

title: Belyi permutation triple orders
snippet: A less restrictive version of abc_orders where only some of the orders are specified.

title: Passport size of a Belyi map
snippet: The number of isomorphism classes of Belyi maps belonging to a given passport.

title: Passport
snippet: A **passport** is the data of a transitive subgroup of the symmetric group $S_d$ along with a triple of conjugacy classes $(C_0, C_1, C_\infty)$ of $S_d$, (equivalently, a triple of partitions $(\lambda_0, \lambda_1, \lambda_\infty)$ of $d$).

title: Transitive permutation triple
snippet: A **permutation triple** is a triple of permutations $\sigma=(\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\sigma_\infty) \in S_d^3$ such that $\sigma_\infty\sigma_1\sigma_0=1$. A permutation triple is **transitive** if the subgroup $\langle \sigma \rangle$ is a transitive subgroup of the symmetric group $S_d$.

title: Primitive Belyi map
snippet: A Belyi map is primitive if its monodromy group is primitive as a transitive subgroup of $S_d$.

title: Primitivization of a Belyi map
snippet: Given a Belyi map $\phi$ the primitivization of $\phi$ is the subcover of minimal degree with primitive monodromy group, which is unique up to isomorphism.

title: Ramification type
snippet: The **ramification type** of a Belyi map $\phi \colon X \to \mathbb{P}^1$ is the triple of partitions $\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_\infty$ giving the ramification orders of the preimages of $0,1,\infty$, respectively.

title: Find a Belyi map
snippet: Enter the label of a Belyi map or passport.

title: Completeness of the Shimura curve database
Dirichlet character
snippet: A Dirichlet character is a function \(\chi: \Z\to \C\) together with a positive integer $q$ called the **modulus** such that $\chi$ is completely multiplicative, i.e. $\chi(mn)=\chi(m)\chi(n)$ for all integers $m$ and $n$, and $\chi$ is periodic modulo $q$, i.e. $\chi(n+q)=\chi(n)$ for all $n$. If $(n,q)>1$ then $\chi(n)=0$, whereas if $(n,q)=1$, then $\chi(n)$ is a root of unity. The character $\chi$ is primitive if its conductor is equal to its modulus.

title: Basic properties of Dirichlet characters
snippet: Basic properties of a Dirichlet character are - its modulus - its conductor - its order - its parity: odd/even - it is either real/complex - it is either primitive/non-primitive

title: Conductor of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The conductor of a Dirichlet character $\chi$ modulo $q$ is the least positive integer $q_1$ dividing $q$ for which $\chi(n+kq_1)=\chi(n)$ for all $n$ and $n+kq_1$ coprime to $q$.

title: Conrey label of Dirichlet characters
snippet: We use the notation $\chi_{q}(n,\cdot)$ to identify Dirichlet characters $\Z\to \C$, where $q$ is the modulus, and $n$ is the index, a positive integer coprime to $q$ that identifies a Dirichlet character of modulus $q$ as described below. The LMFDB label $\texttt{q.n}$, with $1\le n < \max(q,2)$ uniquely identifies $\chi_{q}(n,\cdot)$.

title: Conductor of a Dirichlet character from its Conrey label
snippet: Let $$\chi_q(n,\cdot)=\prod_{p|q}\chi_{p^e}(n,\cdot)$$ be the unique factorization of the Dirichlet character $\chi_q(n,\cdot)$ into characters of prime power modulus $p^e$ under the Conrey labeling system. The conductor of $\chi_q(n,\cdot)$ is the product of the conductors of the $\chi_{p^e}(n,\cdot)$. For odd primes $p$ and $1\leq f\leq e$, the isomorphism between $(\Z/p^f\Z)^\times$ and the image of $x\mapsto x^{p^{e-f}}$ in $(\Z/p^e\Z)^\times$ is compatible with the Conrey labeling system.

title: Conrey index of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The **Conrey index** (or **index**) of a Dirichlet character $\chi$ of modulus $q$ is the unique positive integer $n < \max(q,2)$ for which $\chi = \chi_q(n,\cdot)$ in the Conrey labeling system.

title: Labels for Galois orbits of Dirichlet Characters
snippet: A Galois orbit of Dirichlet characters is labelled as $\texttt{q.A}$, where $\texttt{q}$ is the modulus of the characters in the orbit, and $\texttt{A}$ is a lower-case letter which corresponds to the lexicographic ordering of the Galois-invariant vector $\left( \textup{Order}(\chi), \mathrm{Tr}(\chi(2)), \cdots, \mathrm{Tr}(\chi(\texttt{q} - 1) \right)$, for $\chi$ any Dirichlet character in the orbit. See Section 3.3 of \cite{arXiv:2002.04717} for more details.

title: Order of a Dirichlet character from its label
snippet: Since $\chi_q(m^r,n)=\chi_q(m,n)^r,$ a Dirichlet character of Conrey index $m$ has order $r$, where $r$ is the multiplicative order of $m$ in $(\Z/q\Z)^\times$.

title: Parity of a Dirichlet character from its Conrey label
snippet: Let $$\chi_q(n,\cdot)=\prod_{p|q}\chi_{p^e}(n,\cdot)$$ be the unique factorization of the Dirichlet character $\chi_q(n,\cdot)$ into characters of prime power modulus $p^e$ under the Conrey labeling system. The parity of $\chi_q(n,\cdot)$ is the sum of the parities of the Dirichlet characters $\chi_{p^e}(n,\cdot)$, which can be computed as follows: - for $p>2$, the character $\chi_{p^e}(n,\cdot)$ is even if and only if $n$ is a square modulo $p$; - for $p=2$ and $e>1$ the character $\chi_{p^e}(n,\cdot)$ is even if and only if $n$ is a square modulo $4$.

title: Conrey primitive generator modulo $p$
snippet: For an odd prime $p$ and an exponent $e\geq1$, the group $(\Z/p^e\Z)^\times$ is cyclic. It happens that any integer $a$ which generates $(\Z/p^2\Z)^\times$ will also be a generator of $(\Z/p^e\Z)^\times$ for all $e\geq 1$. We call the least such $a$ the **Conrey (primitive) generator** modulo p. Interestingly, the Conrey generator is also the smallest generator of $(\Z/p\Z)^\times$ for all primes $p<10^{12}$ except two cases: - for $p=40487$, the Conrey generator is $10$ instead of $5$. - for $p=6692367337$, the Conrey generator is $7$ instead of $5$.

title: Data for a dirichlet character
snippet: This knowl can display data for a Dirichlet character specified by its label as a parameter.

title: Degree of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The **degree** of a Dirichlet character $\chi$ is the degree of its field of values $\Q(\chi(\Z))$. If $\chi$ has order $n$ then its degree is $\phi(n):=\#(\Z/n\Z)^\times$.

title: Fixed field of a Dirichlet character
snippet: Let $\chi:\mathbb{Z}\to \mathbb{C}$ be a Dirichlet character with modulus $q$. The **fixed field** of $\chi$ is $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_q)^{\ker(\chi)}$. By definition, this is the subfield of elements fixed under Galois morphisms $\zeta_q\mapsto \zeta_q^k$ for all $k$ such that $\chi(k)=1$. In fact $\chi$ determines a homomorphism $\chi:(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times\to \mathbb{C}^\times$.

title: Galois orbit of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The **Galois orbit** of a Dirichlet character $\chi$ of modulus $q$ and order $n$ is the set $[\chi]:=\{\sigma(\chi): \sigma\in \Gal(\Q(\zeta_n)/\Q)\}$, where $\sigma(\chi)$ denotes the Dirichlet character of modulus $q$ defined by $k \mapsto \sigma(\chi(k))$. The map $\chi\to \sigma(\chi)$ defines a faithful action of the nf.galois_group $\Gal(\Q(\zeta_n)/\Q)$ on the set of Dirichlet characters of modulus $q$ and order $n$, each of which has $\Q(\zeta_n)$ as its field of values.

title: Orbit index of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The Galois orbits of Dirichlet characters of modulus $q$ are ordered as follows. Let $\chi$ be any character in the Galois orbit $[\chi]$ and define the $N$-tuple of integers \[ t([\chi]) := (n,t_1,t_2,\ldots,t_{q-1}) \in \Z^q, \] where $n$ is the order of $\chi$ and $t_i:=\mathrm{tr}_{\Q(\chi)/\Q}(\chi(i))$ is the trace of $\chi(i)$ from the field of values of $\chi$ to $\Q$. The $q$-tuple $t([\chi])$ is independent of the choice of representative $\chi$ and uniquely identifies the Galois orbit $[\chi]$.

title: Label of a Galois orbit of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The **label** of a Galois orbit of a Dirichlet character $\chi$ of modulus $N$ takes the form $N.a$, where $a$ is a letter or string of letters representing the index of the Galois orbit. The index $1$ is written as $a$, the index $2$ is written as $b$, the index $27$ is written as $ba$, and so on.

title: Gauss sum
snippet: Let $\chi$ be a Dirichlet character with modulus $q$. For $a \in \mathbb{Z}$, the **Gauss sum** associated to $\chi$ and $a$ is given by $$ \large \tau_{a}(\chi) = \sum_{r \,\in\, \mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z}} \chi(r)\, e^{2\pi iar/q}. $$ The Gauss sum $\tau_1(\chi)$ is often called "the Gauss sum" of $\chi$.

title: Dirichlet character group
snippet: The Dirichlet characters $\chi\colon\Z\to\C$ of modulus $q$ form a group under pointwise multiplication. {# $(\chi_1\chi_2)(m):=\chi_1(m)\chi_2(m)$. #} In the Conrey labeling system we simply have $\chi_q(n_1,\cdot)\chi_q(n_2,\cdot) = \chi_q(n_1n_2,\cdot)$. This group is (non-canonically) isomorphic to the multiplicative group $(\Z/q\Z)^\times$.

title: Generators of a Dirichlet character group
snippet: A set of **generators** of the Dirichlet character group $G$ of modulus $q$ is a set of Dirichlet characters $\chi_q(n_i,\cdot)$ that generate $G$ under multiplication. These may be chosen so that $G$ is the direct product of the cyclic groups generated by each of the $\chi_q(n_i,\cdot)$.

title: Order of a Dirichlet character group
snippet: The set of Dirichlet characters $\chi\colon\Z\to\C$ of modulus $q$ form a group isomorphic (though not canonically) to $(\Z/q\Z)^\times$, which has **order** $\varphi(q)$, where $\varphi$ is the Euler function.

title: Structure of a Dirichlet group
snippet: The **structure** of a Dirichlet character group of Dirichlet characters of modulus $q$ refers to its isomorphism type as a finite abelian group, which can be uniquely expressed as a product of cyclic groups $C_{n_1}\times C_{n_2}\cdots\times C_{n_r}$ with $n_1|n_2|\cdots |n_r$. This is the same as the structure of the group $(\Z/q\Z)^\times$.

title: Induced Dirichlet character
snippet: A Dirichlet character $\chi_1$ of modulus $q_1$ is said to be induced by a Dirichlet character $\chi_2$ of modulus $q_2$ dividing $q_1$ if $\chi_1(m)=\chi_2(m)$ for all $m$ coprime to $q_1$. A Dirichlet character is primitive if it is not induced by any character other than itself; every Dirichlet character is induced by a uniquely determined primitive Dirichlet character.

title: Jacobi sum
snippet: Given two Dirichlet characters $\chi$ and $\psi$ modulo $q$, the **Jacobi sum** associated to these characters is given by $$J(\chi,\psi) = \sum_{r \,\in\, \mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z}} \chi(r)\, \psi(1-r).$$

title: Jacobi symbol
snippet: The **Jacobi symbol** \(\displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)\) is an extension of the Legendre symbol to all positive odd integers $b$ as follows. We set $ \displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{1}\right) =1 $ for all $a.$ If $b>1,$ then it has a decomposition into distinct *odd* primes of the form $b = p_1^{e_1} p_2^{e_2}\cdots p_r^{e_r}$ and the Jacobi symbol is defined as the product of Legendre symbols $$ \displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{b}\right) = \left(\frac{a}{p_1}\right)^{e_1} \left(\frac{a}{p_2}\right)^{e_2}\cdots \left(\frac{a}{p_r}\right)^{e_r}.$$ The Jacobi symbol is multiplicative in the sense…

title: Kloosterman sum
snippet: Let $\chi$ be a Dirichlet character with modulus $q$. The **Kloosterman sum** $K(a,b,\chi)$ is defined by \[K(a,b,\chi) = \sum_{r \in (\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\times} \chi(r)\zeta^{ar+br^{-1}},\] where $\zeta = e^{2\pi i/q}$. This reduces to the Gauss sum if $b=0$.

title: Kronecker symbol
snippet: The **Kronecker symbol** \(\displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{n}\right)\) is the multiplicative extension of the character.dirichlet.jacobi_symbol \(\displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{m}\right)\), which is defined on all positive odd integers \(m\), to all integers \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\) as follows. We set $$\displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{-1}\right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{cl} 1 & \text{if } a\geq 0\\ -1 & \text{if } a < 0; \end{array} \right.$$ $$\displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{2}\right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{cl} 0 & \text{if } a\equiv 0 \bmod 2\\ 1 & \text{if } a\equiv \pm 1 \bmod 8\\ -1 & \text{if } a \equiv \pm…

title: Legendre symbol
snippet: Let $x$ be an integer and $p$ be an odd prime. The **Legendre symbol** \(\displaystyle\left(\frac{x}{p}\right)\) is defined to be \[ \left( \frac{x}{p} \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{cl} 0 & \text{if } p \mid x, \\ 1 & \text{if } x \bmod p \text{ is a non-zero square in } \Z/p\Z, \\ -1 & \text{if } x \bmod p \text{ is not a square in } \Z/p\Z. \end{array} \right. \] It can be computed efficiently by using the following formulae inductively: - (Multiplicativity) $\left(\frac{ab}{p}\right) = \left(\frac{a}{p}\right) \left(\frac{b}{p}\right)$.

title: Minimal Dirichlet character
snippet: A Dirichlet character $\chi$ of prime power modulus $N$ is **minimal** if the following conditions both hold: 1. The conductor of $\chi$ does not lie in the open interval $(\sqrt{N},N)$, and if $N$ is a square divisible by 16 then ${\rm cond}(\chi)\in \{\sqrt{N},N\}$. 2. Both the order and conductor of $\chi$ are minimal among the set of all Dirichlet character $\chi\psi^2$ for which ${\rm cond}(\psi){\rm cond}(\chi\psi) | N$. This includes all primitive Dirichlet characters of prime power modulus, but not every minimal Dirichlet character of prime power modulus is primitive.

title: Modulus of a Dirichlet character
snippet: A **Dirichlet character** is a function $\chi: \mathbb Z\to \mathbb C$ together with a positive integer $q$, called the **modulus** of the character, such that $\chi$ is completely multiplicative, i.e. $\chi(mn)=\chi(m)\chi(n)$ for all integers $m$ and $n$, and $\chi$ is periodic modulo $q$, i.e. $\chi(n+q)=\chi(n)$ for all $n$. If $(n,q)>1$ then $\chi(n)=0$, whereas if $(n,q)=1$, then $\chi(n)$ is a root of unity.

title: Data for a Galois orbit of Dirichlet characters
snippet: This knowl can display data for a Galois orbit of Dirichlet characters specified by either the orbit label or a label for any character in the orbit.

title: Order of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The order of a Dirichlet character $\chi$ is the least positive integer $n$ such that $\chi^n$ is the trivial character of the same modulus as $\chi$. Equivalently, it is the order $n$ of the image of $\chi$ in $\mathbb{C}^\times$, the group of $n$th roots of unity.

title: Parity of a Dirichlet character
snippet: A character has odd/even **parity** if it is odd/even as a function. A Dirichlet character $\chi\colon\Z\to\C$ is odd if $\chi(-1) = -1$ and even if $\chi(-1)=1.$

title: Primitive Dirichlet character
snippet: A Dirichlet character $\chi$ is primitive if its conductor is equal to its modulus; equivalently, $\chi$ is not induced by a Dirichlet character of smaller modulus.

title: Principal Dirichlet character
snippet: A Dirichlet character is **principal** (or **trivial**) if it has order $1$, equivalently, if it is induced by the unique Dirichlet character of modulus 1. The value of the principal Dirichlet character of modulus $q$ at an integer $n$ is $1$ if $n$ is coprime to $q$ and $0$ otherwise.

title: Real Dirichlet character
snippet: A character is **real** if its image is contained in $\mathbb R.$

title: Number fields related to a Dirichlet character
snippet: There are two number fields naturally associated to a Dirichlet character $\chi$ of modulus $q$: - the field of values: $\Q(\chi(1),\ldots,\chi(q-1))$; - the fixed field: $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_q)^{\ker(\chi)}$.

title: Find a Dirichlet character
snippet: To find a specific Dirichlet character, enter its label, or the label of its Galois orbit.

title: Field of values of a Dirichlet character
snippet: The **field of values** of a Dirichlet character $\chi\colon\Z\to\C$ is the field $\Q(\chi(\Z))$ generated by its values; it is equal to the cyclotomic field $\Q(\zeta_n)$, where $n$ is the order of $\chi$.

title: Values of a Dirichlet character
snippet: Complete information about a Dirichlet character $\chi\colon \Z\to\C$ of modulus $q > 1 $ is contained in the list of values $(\chi(1)=1,\ \chi(2),\ \chi(3),\ \dots,\ \chi(q-1),\ \chi(q)=0)$, each of which is a root of unity $e(r) := \exp({2 \pi i r}$), for some rational number $r$.

title: Values of a Dirichlet character on generators
snippet: A Dirichlet character $\chi\colon \Z\to \C$ of modulus $q$ is determined (via multiplicativity) by the values $\chi(g_1), \ldots, \chi(g_r)$ it takes on any list of generators $g_1,\ldots,g_r$ for the group $(\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})^\\times$. The generators $g_i$ can each be specified as integers in the interval $[1,q-1]$, and the values $\chi(g_i)$ are roots of unity $e(r):=\exp(2\pi i r)$, where $r$ is a rational number.

title: Hecke character
snippet: Let $K$ be a number field with ring of integers $\Z_K$ and let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an integral ideal of $\Z_K$. Let $I(\mathfrak{m})$ be the group of fractional ideals of $\Z_K$ coprime to $\mathfrak{m}$. A **Hecke character** modulo $\mathfrak{m}$ is a group homomorphism $\chi:I(\mathfrak{m})\to\mathbb{C}^\times$ with the property there exists another group homomorphism $\chi_\infty:K^\times/\mathbb{Q}^\times\to\mathbb{C}^\times$ such that \[ \chi(\alpha\mathbb{Z}_K)=\chi_\infty(\alpha) \text{ for all }\alpha\in K^\times\text{ such that }\alpha\equiv1 \mod ^*\mathfrak{m}, \] where by…

title: Underlying unit group for a Dirichlet character
snippet: A Dirichlet character $\chi: \Z\to \C$ has a modulus $n$ such that $\chi$ is induced from a function $\chi:\Z/n\Z\to \C$. It gives a homomorphism $(\Z/n\Z)^\times\to \C^\times$. The **underlying unit group** is $(\Z/n\Z)^\times$, the group of units of the ring $\Z/n\Z$.

title: Data for a cluster picture
snippet: This knowl can display data for a cluster picture specified by its label as a parameter.

title: Classical modular form
snippet: Let $k$ be a positive integer and let $\Gamma$ be a finite index subgroup of the modular group $\SL(2,\Z)$. A (classical) **modular form** $f$ of weight $k$ on $\Gamma$, is a holomorphic function defined on the upper half plane $\mathcal{H}$, which satisfies the transformation property \[ f(\gamma z) = (cz+d)^k f(z) \] for all $z\in\mathcal{H}$ and $\gamma=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\in \Gamma$ and is holomorphic at all the cusps of $\Gamma$.
