All supersolvable groups are monomial, and all abelian, nilpotent, metacyclic groups, Z-groups and groups of square-free order are supersolvable.

title: Sylow subgroup
snippet: If $p$ is a prime and $G$ is a finite group of order $p^nm$ where $p\nmid m$, then a **$p$-Sylow subgroup** of $G$ is any subgroup of order $p^n$. Sylow subgroups exist for every finite group and prime $p$.

title: Symmetric group
snippet: The **symmetric group** is the group $S_n$ of bijections from $n$ element sets to themselves (called permutations), which is of order $n!$. This group is solvable for $n\leq 4$ and almost simple since $S_n=A_n\rtimes C_2$ for $n\geq 5$.

title: Symplectic group
snippet: The **symplectic group** $\Sp(2n, q)$ is the group of invertible $2n \times 2n$ matrices $M$ with entries in $\F_q$, which satisfy the condition that $M^T \Omega M = \Omega$, for $\Omega$ is any fixed invertible skew-symmetric matrix over $\F_q$. For example, $\Omega$ can be taken to be the block matrix $$ \Omega = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & I_n \\ -I_n & 0 \end{pmatrix}, $$ where $I_n$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix.

title: Torsion group
snippet: A **torsion group** is a group in which every element has finite order. The elements of finite order in an abelian group $A$ form a torsion group called the **torsion subgroup** of $A$.

title: Transitive degree
snippet: Given a finite group $G$, we call the minimal degree of a faithful transitive permutation representation of $G$ the **minimal transitive degree** of $G$. Equivalently, it is the smallest positive integer $n$ such that $S_n$ has a transitive subgroup isomorphic to $G$. This can be computed as the minimal index of a subgroup with trivial core.

title: Triangle group
snippet: Given positive integers $l, m, n \ge 2$, the **triangle group** $\Delta(l, m, n)$ is given by the presentation $\langle a, b, c | a^2 = b^2 = c^2 = (ab)^l = (bc)^m = (ca)^n \rangle$. It is a Coxeter group, and forms the symmetry group of a tiling of either the sphere (if $\frac1l + \frac1m + \frac1n > 1$), the Euclidean plane (if $\frac1l + \frac1m + \frac1n = 1$) or the hyperbolic plane (if $\frac1l + \frac1m + \frac1n < 1$) by triangles with angles $\frac{\pi}l, \frac{\pi}m$ and $\frac{\pi}n$.

title: Trivial subgroup
snippet: If $G$ is a group with identity element $e$, then $\{e\}$ is a subgroup isomorphic to the trivial group. It is the **trivial subgroup** of $G$, and all other subgroups are **non-trivial**.

title: Type of groups
snippet:
For a finite group $G$, we write **Type - length** as follows
  • If $G$ is cyclic we write "Cyclic". For abelian groups that are not cyclic we write "Abelian - $n$", where $n$ is the number of cyclic groups in the unique cyclic decomposition of $G$ as $C_{d_1} \times C_{d_2}\times \cdots \times C_{d_n}$ with $d_i\mid d_{i+1}$ and $d_i>0$.
  • If $G$ is nilpotent but not abelian then we write "Nilpotent - $n$", where $n$ is the nilpotency class of $G$.
  • If $G$ is solvable but not nilpotent then we write "Solvable - $n$", where $n$ is the derived length of $G$.
  • …

title: Under-subgroup
snippet: If $H \subseteq G$ is a subgroup, an **under-subgroup** is a subgroup $K \subseteq G$ with $K \subseteq H$. A **maximal under-subgroup** is an under-subgroup $K \ne H$ that is maximal with respect to inclusion.

title: Unitary group
snippet: The **(general) unitary group** $\GU(n, q)$ is the group of $n \times n$ unitary matrices with entries in the field $\F_{q^2}$ (the finite field with $q^2$ elements). Here, a matrix is *unitary* if the inverse of $M$ equals the conjugate transpose of $M$. For matrices $M$ over the finite field $\F_{q^2}$, the *conjugate* of $M$ is defined as the matrix obtained by applying the map $x \mapsto x^q$ (i.e. the $r$-th power of the Frobenius automorphism, where $q = p^r$) to each entry of $M$.

title: Upper central series of a group
snippet: If $G$ is a group, then its **upper central series** is a normal series of subgroups $$ \langle e\rangle = Z_0(G) \unlhd Z_1(G) \unlhd Z_2(G) \unlhd \cdots $$ where $Z_0(G)=\langle e\rangle$ and $Z_i(G)/Z_{i-1}(G)$ is the center of $G/Z_{i-1}(G)$, i.e., $Z_i(G)$ is the inverse image of the center of $G/Z_{i-1}(G)$. Each of the subgroups $Z_i(G)$ is characteristic. The group is nilpotent if and only if $Z_i(G)=G$ for some $i$.

title: Weight function
snippet: Let $G$ be a finite group. A weight function on $G$ is a function $w:G \to \R_{\geq 0}$ such that: 1. $w(g) = 0$ if and only if $g$ equals the identity element of $G$. 2. $w(h g h^{-1}) = w(g)$ for all $g,h \in G$, i.e. $w$ is invariant under conjugacy. 3. $w(g^n) = w(g)$ for all $g \in G$ and all natural numbers $n$ with $n \nmid \#G$, i.e. $w$ is invariant under invertible powering. Any weight function takes constant value on each rational conjugacy class of $G$.

title: Weyl group
snippet: The **Weyl group** associated to a subgroup $H \subseteq G$ is the quotient $W = N_G(H) / Z_G(H)$ of the normalizer of $H$ by the centralizer of $H$. The terminology is taken from the theory of Lie groups, where there is a unique conjugacy class of maximal tori in a Lie group $G$ and if $T$ is any such maximal torus then $Z_G(T) = T$ has finite index in $N_G(T)$. In that context we may thus speak of the Weyl group of $G$.

title: Wreath product
snippet: If $S\leq S_n$ and $H$ are groups, the **wreath product** of $H$ and $S$ is the semidirect product $$H \wr S = H^n \rtimes S$$ where $S$ acts on $H^n$ via $S\subseteq S_n$, permuting the $n$ copies of $H$. If $S$ is a transitive subgroup of $S_n$, we say that the wreath product is **transitive**.

title: Z-group
snippet: A **Z-group** is a (finite) group all of whose Sylow subgroups are cyclic. Such groups are metacyclic, supersolvable and monomial. Every cyclic group is a Z-group, but the converse does not hold, as shown by these examples.

title: GSp4 subgroup data
snippet: This knowl can display data for a subgroup of $\GSp(4,\widehat \Z)$ specified by its label.

title: Hecke algebra
snippet: For a commutative ring $R$ and an $R$-module $M$ equipped with an action of Hecke operators, such as spaces of modular forms or modular symbols, the associated $R$-Hecke algebra is the $R$-subalgebra of the $R$-endomorphism ring of $M$ generated by the Hecke operators. We usually include all Hecke operators, i.e. also those the index of which divides the level or is a multiple of the characteristic (if applicable). If the ring $R$ is not mentioned, then it is either $\Q$ or $\C$, depending on the context.

title: Bézout determinant
snippet: The **Bézout determinant** of a hypergeometric motive $H$ is the determinant of its Bézout matrix.

title: Bézout matrix of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: For a list of integers $C=[c_1,\ldots, c_n]$ we associate the polynomial \[ \Phi_C(x) = \prod_{j=1}^n \Phi_{c_j}(x) \] where $\Phi_n(x)$ is the $n$th cyclotomic polynomial. Given a hypergeometric motive with defining parameters $A$ and $B$, its **Bézout matrix** is the Bézout matrix of $\Phi_A(x)$ and $\Phi_B(x)$ of degree $\max(\deg \Phi_A(x), \deg \Phi_B(x))$.

title: Bézout module of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: The **Bezout module** of a hypergeometric motive $M$ is the finite $\mathbb{Z}$-module whose relations matrix is the Bézout matrix $B$ of $M$. In other words, it is the quotient $\mathbb{Z}^d/B \mathbb{Z}^d$, where $d$ is the degree of $M$.

title: Conductor of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: The exponents $c_p$ in the **conductor** $N = \prod_p p^{c_p}$ of a hypergeometric motive $H(A,B,t)$ can be positive only if $p$ is tame or wild for $H(A,B,t)$.

title: Cusp index
snippet: A **cusp-index** of degree $d$ is a weakly increasing list of positive integers $[u_1,\dots,u_k]$ with $\sum_{i=1}^k \phi(u_i) = d$. Hypergeometric families $H(A,B)$ are indexed by pairs $(A,B)$ of cusp-indices of a common degree having no common entries.

title: p-part of defining parameters of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: For a hypergeometric motive with defining parameters $[A, B]$ and a prime number $p$, we define the $p$-part $[A_p, B_p]$ as follows. For each $u=a_i$ or $b_j$ in $A=(a_1,\ldots, a_m)$ and $B=(b_1,\ldots, b_n)$ we write $u=p^s v$ where $p \nmid v$. We replace $u$ with $\phi(v)$ copies of $p^s$, to get $[A', B']$, and then remove equal numbers of any overlapping elements between the multisets $A'$ and $B'$ to get $A_p$ and $B_p$. We denote the list of these removed overlapping elements by $C_p$.

title: prime to $p$-part of defining parameters of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: For a hypergeometric motive with defining parameters $[A, B]$ and a prime number $p$, we define the prime-to-$p$-part $[A^p, B^p]$ as follows. For each $u=a_i$ or $b_j$ in $A=(a_1,\ldots, a_m)$ and $B=(b_1,\ldots, b_n)$ we write $u=p^s v$ where $p \nmid v$. We replace $u$ with $\phi(p^s)$ copies of $v$, to get $[A', B']$, and then remove equal numbers of any overlapping elements between the multisets $A'$ and $B'$ to get $A^p$ and $B^p$.

title: Defining parameters of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: A hypergeometric motive $H(A,B,t)$ in $M(\Q,\Q)$ is described by a hypergeometric family $H(A,B)$ in $M(\Q(t),t)$ along with a specialization point $t \in \Q^\times$. Here, $A$ and $B$ are disjoint multisets of positive integers and are the **defining parameters** of the hypergeometric family; they are subject to a constraint that $$ \sum_{a \in A} \phi(a) = \sum_{b \in B} \phi(b); $$ this common value is the degree of the family. The value $t$ along with $A$ and $B$ are the **defining parameters** of the hypergeometric motive $H(A,B,t)$.

title: Degree of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: Associated to a motive are vector spaces coming from cohomology of an algebraic variety. The dimension of these vector spaces is the **degree** of the motive. For a hypergeometric motive with defining parameters $A=[a_1,\ldots,a_m]$, $B=[b_1,\ldots,b_n]$, the degree is the common value \[ \sum_{j=1}^m \phi(a_j) = \sum_{j=1}^n \phi(b_j)\] where $\phi$ is the Euler totient function.

title: Hypergeometric family over $\Q$
snippet: A **hypergeometric family** over $\Q$ is a motive over the field $\Q(t)$ with coefficients in $\Q$. The families are indexed by non-overlapping ordered pairs of multisets of positive integers $(A,B)$ (called cusp-indices) with $\sum_{a \in A} \phi(a) = \sum_{b \in B} \phi(b) = d \in \Z_{\geq 1}$, where $d$ is the degree and $\phi$ is Euler's totient function.

title: Hypergeometric family over $\Q$
snippet: A **hypergeometric family** over $\Q$ is a motive over the field $\Q(t)$ with coefficients in $\Q$. The families are indexed by non-overlapping ordered pairs of multisets of positive integers $(A,B)$ (called cusp-indices) with $\sum_{a \in A} \phi(a) = \sum_{b \in B} \phi(b) = d \in \Z_{\geq 1}$, where $d$ is the degree and $\phi$ is Euler's totient function.

title: Label of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: Hypergeometric motives $H(A,B,t) \in M(\Q,\Q)$ are labeled following the pattern of $H([3,3],[2,2,4],-1/3)$ having label A3.3_B2.2.4_t-1.3 and the corresponding family having label A3.3_B2.2.4 .

title: Primes of good and bad reduction of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: A **prime of bad reduction** or **bad prime** of a hypergeometric motive \(M\) is a prime dividing the conductor of \(M\). A bad prime is either tame or wild. A **prime of good reduction** or **good prime** is a prime that is not a bad prime, i.e., a prime that does not divide the conductor.

title: Hodge vector of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: Let $H(A,B)$ be a hypergeometric family of degree $d$ and weight $w$ with defining parameters $A=[a_1,\ldots,a_m]$ and $B=[b_1,\ldots,b_n]$. The **Hodge vector** $(h^{0,w},\dots,h^{w,0})$ of $H(A,B)$ records the dimensions $h^{p,q}$ (with $p+q=w$) of the Hodge subspaces associated to the family. The Hodge vector depends on how the indices $a_j$ and $b_k$ intertwine on the circle $\R/\Z$, and we have $d = \sum_{p=0}^{w} h^{p,w-p}$. At the extreme of complete intertwining, the Hodge vector is $(h^{0,0}) = (d)$.

title: Imprimitivity index of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: If a hypergeometric family has defining parameters $A=(a_1,\ldots,a_n)$ and $B=(b_1,\ldots,b_m)$, then we can view the parameters as giving two multisets of roots of unity, namely for $A$, the primitive $a_i$-th roots of unity repeated according to their multiplicities in $A$, and similarly for $B$. These sets has a rotational symmetry group $R$, which may be trivial. The **rotation number** is the order of $R$.

title: Levelt matrices of a hypergeometric family
snippet: The monodromy representation associated to a hypergeometric family is generated by two matrices $h_{\infty}$ and $h_0$ describing the local monodromy around $\infty$ and $0$, respectively. The characteristic polynomial of $h_{\infty}$ is $\prod_j (T - e^{2\pi i \alpha_j})$ and of $h_0$ is $\prod_j (T - e^{2\pi i \beta_j})$, where $(\alpha_j)$ and $(\beta_j)$ are from the defining parameters of the family. The choice of matrices satisfying this condition is determined up to simultaneous conjugacy. The presentation used here is due to Levelt \cite{MR:0145108}.

title: The local discriminant of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: Given a hypergeometric motive $M$ and a prime $p$, the **local discriminant** of $M$ at $p$ is the discriminant of the number field defined by the characteristic polynomial of Frobenius of $M$ at $p$.

title: Monodromy group of a hypergeometric family
snippet: The monodromy representation associated to a hypergeometric family is generated by two matrices $A$ and $B$ describing the local monodromy around $\infty$ and $0$, respectively. The presentation used here is due to Levelt \cite{MR0145108}. The monodromy groups of hypergeometric families of weight 0 were computed by Beukers and Heckman \cite{MR0974906, doi:10.1007/BF01393900}.

title: Hypergeometric motive
snippet: **Hypergeometric motives** $H(A,B,t)$ in a family $H(A,B)$ are indexed by rational numbers $t \not \in \{0,1\}$, with the case $t=1$ allowed if special attention is given. For example $H([3],[1,1],t)$ is the motive $H^1(E_{1728t},{\Bbb Q})$ where $E_{j}$ is an elliptic curve with $j$-invariant $1728j$. Also special hypergeometric motives $H(A,B,1)$ are defined, having degree $1$ or $2$ less than the other $H(A,B,t)$.

title: Imprimitivity index of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: If a hypergeometric family has defining parameters $A=(a_1,\ldots,a_n)$ and $B=(b_1,\ldots,b_m)$, then we can view the parameters as giving two multisets of roots of unity, namely for $A$, the primitive $a_i$-th roots of unity repeated according to their multiplicities in $A$, and similarly for $B$. These sets has a rotational symmetry group $R$, which may be trivial. The **rotation number** is the order of $R$.

title: Find a hypergeometric motive
snippet: To find a specific hypergeometric motive or family, enter its label.

title: hgm.signature
snippet: Complex conjugation acts on the vector space corresponding to the middle entry in the Hodge vector. The **signature** of a hypergeometric motive is the difference between the number of $+1$ eigenvalues and the number of $-1$ eigenvalues for this operator. When there is no middle entry (because the Hodge width is odd), the signature is $0$. The archimedean root number can be recovered from the signature $s$ and the Hodge vector $(h_n)$.

title: Tame primes for hypergeometric families
snippet: A prime $p$ is called **tame** for a hypergeometric motive $H(A,B,t)$ if $p$ is not wild and either $v_p(t) \ne 0$ or $v_p(t-1) \ne 0$. The conductor of $H(A,B,t)$ is supported at a subset of the tame and wild primes.

title: The type of a hypergeometric motive
snippet: A hypergeometric motive $M$ of weight $w$ is of **orthogonal** or **symplectic** **type** according as $w$ is even or odd. This corresponds to the fact that the motivic Galois group of $M$ is either a subgroup of the conformal orthogonal group or conformal symplectic group depending on the parity of $w$.

title: weight
snippet: Associated to a motive are vector spaces coming from cohomology $H^*(X)$ of an algebraic variety $X$. If these vector spaces arise in $H^w(X)$, then $w$ is the **(motivic) weight** of the motive. The weight is the number of commas in the Hodge vector, i.e., the Hodge vector is of the form $(h^{0,w},\dots,h^{w,0})$.

title: Wild primes for hypergeometric families
snippet: A prime $p$ is called **wild** for a hypergeometric family $H(A,B)$ if it divides an element of the defining parameters $A$ and $B$. The name is appropriate since if $p$ is such a prime, then for most $t \in \Q^\times$ the motive $H(A,B,t)$ is wildly ramified at $p$.

title: Zigzag plot
snippet: The **zigzag plot**, or **zigzag procedure**, is a graphical representation of the defining parameters of a hypergeometric family $\alpha$ and $\beta$. For improved readability, we color the $\alpha$ parameters with red, and the $\beta$ parameters with blue. It is constructed by ordering the parameters $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_j$. One draws a point at $(0,0)$ corresponding to the lowest parameter. One then proceeds in uniform steps from left to right, drawing a point for each parameter and then moving diagonally upwards after red points and diagonally downwards after blue points.

title: Arithmetic genus
snippet: The **arithmetic genus** of a projective scheme $X$ is the alternating sum $\chi(\mathcal{O}_X) = \sum_{i=0}^{\dim X} (-1)^i \dim H^i(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$. Note that some authors call this quantity the holomorphic Euler characteristic. Additionally, some authors refer to the quantity $p_a(X) = (-1)^{\dim X} (\chi(\mathcal{O}_X) - 1)$ as the arithmetic genus.

title: Component ideal
snippet: The adelic Hilbert modular surface may be geometrically reducible, and its components are in bijection with the narrow class group of the base field $F$. The component corresponding to a given narrow ideal class $[\mathfrak{b}]$ parametrizes abelian surfaces with endomorphism algebra isomorphic to $F$ whose polarization module is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_F \oplus \mathfrak{b}$. In particular, the surfaces parametrized are principally polarized if and only if the component ideal is the inverse different of the base field $F$.

title: Congruence Subgroup
snippet: Let $F$ be a real quadratic field with ring of integers $\Z_F$. Let $\frak{b}$ be a fractional ideal in $F$ (the component ideal ). A **principal congruence subgroup** $\Gamma_{\frak{b}}(\frak{N})$ is the kernel of reduction-modulo-$\frak{N}$ within $\GL^+(\Z_F \oplus \frak{b})$ or $\SL(\Z_F \oplus \frak{b})$. A **congruence subgroup** $\Gamma$ is a subgroup of either $\GL^+(\Z_F \oplus \frak{b})$ or $\SL(\Z_F \oplus \frak{b})$ which contains a principal congruence subgroup $\Gamma_{\frak{b}}(\frak{N})$. The minimal such $\frak{N}$ is called the **level**.

title: Cusps
snippet: Given a Hilbert modular surface $X$ defined by a Hilbert modular group $\Gamma$ with base field $F$, the *cusps* of $X$ are the orbits of $\mathbb{P}^1(F)$ under $\Gamma$.

title: Elliptic point
snippet: A transformation $(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n) \in \GL_2^+(\R)^n$ is called **elliptic** if each $$ \operatorname{tr}(\alpha_i)^2 - 4\det(\alpha_i) < 0. $$ If $F$ is a real quadratic field, an element $\alpha \in \GL_2^+(F)$ is called **elliptic** if the above condition holds for each real embedding of $F$. An **elliptic point** is the (necessarily unique) fixed point in $\frak{h}^2$ of an elliptic element.

title: Topological Euler number
snippet: The **(topological) Euler number** of a smooth projective variety $X$ over a field of characteristic zero is the quantity $$ \chi = \sum_{i=0}^{2\dim X} (-1)^i \cdot H^i(X(\mathbb{C}), \mathbb{Z}) $$ where the cohomology is singular cohomology on the topological space $X(\mathbb{C})$.

title: Hilbert modular surface
snippet: A **Hilbert modular surface** is a nice algebraic surface that parametrizes abelian surfaces with real multiplication and equipped with level structure. More precisely, let $F$ be a real quadratic field. Let $\Gamma < \GL_2^+(F)$ be a congruence subgroup. Then $\Gamma$ is a discrete group acting properly by isometries on the product $\overline{\mathfrak{h}}^2$ of completed upper half-planes.

title: Hodge numbers
snippet: The **Hodge numbers** $h^{p,q}$ of a smooth projective variety $X$ over $\mathbb{C}$ are the dimensions of the complex vector spaces $H^{p,q}(X) := H^q(X,\Omega^p)$ appearing in the Hodge decomposition of the cohomology group $H^n(X,\mathbb{C}) \cong \bigoplus_{p+q=n} H^{p,q}(X)$.

title: Self-intersection of canonical divisor
snippet: The self-intersection of the canonical divisor $K$ of the Hilbert modular surface. Note that this is also the first Chern number $c_1^2$.

title: Kodaira dimension
snippet: The **Kodaira dimension** of a projective variety $X$ with canonical bundle $K$ is the smallest integer $d$ such that the limit $$ \lim_{m \to \infty} \frac{H^0(X, K^{\otimes m})}{m^d} $$ exists and is non-zero. In the case no such integer exists, our convention is to take $d=-1$.

title: Label of a Hilbert modular surface
snippet: The label of a Hilbert modular surface is of the form `F-N-b-t` where - `F` is a number field label for the base field; - `N` is an ideal label for the level; - `b` is an ideal label for the component ideal; - `t` is the type of the congruence subgroup. The value for `t` can be either `sl-0` or `gl-0` to indicate the congruence subgroup.

title: Definition of the level
snippet: The **level** is a nonzero ideal $\mathfrak{N}$ which determines the congruence subgroup $\Gamma_0(\mathfrak{N})_\mathfrak{b}$. In the LMFDB, such an ideal $\mathfrak{N}$ is identified by a label.

title: Level norm
snippet: The level norm is the absolute norm of the level of the Hilbert modular surface.

title: polarization module
snippet: Let $(A, \iota)$ be an abelian variety with RM by $\iota : \Z_F \to \End(A_{\overline{\Q}})$. The **polarization module** of $A$ is the $\Z_F$-module $$ \mathcal{PM}(A) = \{ \lambda \in \Hom_{\Z_F}(A, A^{\vee}) : \lambda = \lambda^{\vee} \} \subseteq NS(A). $$ The **polarization cone** is the cone of polarizations $ \mathcal{PM}^+(A) \subseteq \mathcal{PM}(A)$, which equips the polarization module with a notion of positivity as a $\Z_F$-module.

title: Find a Hilbert modular surface
snippet: Enter the label of a Hilbert modular surface.

title: Standard Hilbert modular surfaces
snippet: Let $\Gamma$ be the congruence subgroup associated to a Hilbert modular surface. Similar to families of modular curves, each type of congruence subgroup parametrizes abelian varieties with certain level structure. Presently, two special cases are prioritized in this database, namely, hmsurface.x0$') }} and hmsurface.x01$') }}.

title: $X_0(\mathfrak{N})_{\mathfrak{b}}$
snippet: $X_0(\mathfrak{N})_{\mathfrak{b}}$ is the Hilbert modular surface $X(\Gamma)$ for $\Gamma = \Gamma_0(\mathfrak{N})_{\mathfrak{b}} \le \GL^+(\Z_F \oplus \frak{b})$ the inverse image of $\begin{pmatrix} \ast & \ast \\ 0 & \ast \end{pmatrix} \subset \GL((\Z_F/\frak{N}) \oplus (\frak{b} / \frak{N} \frak{b}))$.

title: $X_0^1(\mathfrak{N})_{\mathfrak{b}}$
snippet: $X_0^1(\mathfrak{N})_{\mathfrak{b}}$ is the Hilbert modular surface $X(\Gamma)$ for $\Gamma = \Gamma_0^1(\mathfrak{N})_{\mathfrak{b}} \le \SL(\Z_F \oplus \frak{b})$ the inverse image of $\begin{pmatrix} \ast & \ast \\ 0 & \ast \end{pmatrix} \subset \SL((\Z_F/\frak{N}) \oplus (\frak{b} / \frak{N} \frak{b}))$.

title: Introduction
snippet: Welcome to the LMFDB, the database of L-functions, modular forms, and related objects. Our goal is to describe this data in ways that faithfully exhibit the connections between these objects and prompt further exploration and discovery. We note the following features. **Knowls:** Interspersed throughout the LMFDB are underlined words. These words represent knowls, which are bits of context free information. A knowl is identified by a word or phrase underlined with a dotted line. Clicking on a knowl brings up a box of text containing information about the object.

title: The API
snippet: The LMFDB offers an Application Programming Interface (API). This provides an HTTP-interface for directly querying the tables of data that are used to render object homepages. You can construct queries programatically and send them to the LMFDB's webserver, so this method is available both interactively and from any programming language that can request URLs. There are two main limitations: - Each query is limited to 10,000 results, of which at most 100 can be retrieved in a single request.

title: Command line interface
snippet: The LMFDB offers a command line interface, which you can access by cloning the [LMFDB repository](https://github.com/lmfdb/lmfdb); see the documentation [here](https://github.com/lmfdb/lmfdb/blob/cmdline/CommandLineSearch.md). Running this interface requires that [Sage](https://sagemath.org) be installed, but it offers several kinds of access via the command line: * search input and output parsing and download files as done through the website, replicating many online features.

title: Direct SQL connection
snippet: You can open a direct connection to a read-only copy of the LMFDB's PostgreSQL database, using the SQL Mirror access information below. There are three ways to do this: - You can exploit standard third-party tools or programming language drivers for working with PostgreSQL databases; these require knowledge of SQL.

title: Download from search result pages
snippet: Every search results page in the LMFDB has "Download" buttons located immediately above and below the list of search results. This is the simplest option, and it allows you to control the records to be downloaded (by specifying search options), the columns to be included (via the "columns to display" drop down menu), and the order of the results (via the "Sort order" drop down menu). There are two main limitations: - Downloads are currently limited to 100MB and 30 seconds. - Columns typically cover only a subset of the information available on an object's home page.

title: Introduction/Features
snippet: In the LMFDB, we aim for every object to have its own homepage. Note the following features: **Knowls:** Interspersed throughout the LMFDB are underlined words. These words represent knowls, which are bits of context free information. A knowl is identified by a word or phrase underlined with a dotted line. Clicking on a knowl brings up a box of text containing information about the object. Clicking on the knowl a second time will retract the box of text. **Navigating:** - ***Browsing:*** One way to investigate information on the database is through browsing.

title: MCP server
snippet: You can connect the LMFDB to many large language models by using the [MCP server](https://github.com/AndrewVSutherland/lmfdb-mcp). This allows the models to query the LMFDB via a direct SQL connection in order to answer natural language queries.

title: Search
snippet: * The objects returned will satisfy all of the constraints that you enter. * Within a single text field, you can use commas to get a union of a set of desired inputs. * You can indicate a range by using either a hyphen or a .., such as "3-9" or "3..9". * You can negate a query by inserting a "~" at the beginning; for example "~3,5" excludes objects for which the relevant attribute has value 3 or 5.

title: A tour of the Riemann zeta function
snippet: The simplest of all L-functions is the Riemann zeta function, a function whose analytic properties were first discovered by Bernhard Riemann in 1859 in his attempts to find a formula for the number of primes smaller than a given number $x$. It gives us a model for how to think about other L-functions. Let's begin by taking a look at this prototype: the Riemann zeta function homepage.

title: Additively indecomposable lattice
snippet: A positive definite integral lattice $L$ is **additively indecomposable** if it cannot be written as a sum of two nonzero positive semi-definite integral lattices. Every additively indecomposable lattice is (orthogonally) indecomposable, though the converse is not true in general. However, every unimodular (orthogonally) indecomposable lattice is additively indecomposable. The only additively indecomposable integral lattices of rank up to $8$ are $\Z$, $E_6$, $E_7$, and $E_8$, proven by Mordell and Ko.

title: Algebraic lattice
snippet: A lattice is called **algebraic** if it can be realized as a free $\mathbb Z$-module in an algebraic number field.

title: Arithmetic lattice
snippet: A lattice is called **arithmetic** if it is similar to an integral lattice.

title: Automorphism group of an integral lattice
snippet: The **automorphism group** of an integral lattice $L$ is the group of isometries from $L$ to itself. For a positive definite lattice, this is a finite group.

title: Catalogue of Lattices
snippet: The Catalogue of Lattices is an online database of lattices maintained by [Gabriele Nebe](http://www.math.rwth-aachen.de/~Gabriele.Nebe/) and [Neil J.A. Sloane](http://neilsloane.com).

title: Class number of an integral lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice, $L$, the number of isometry classes in the genus of $L$ is the **class number** of $L$.

title: Conway symbol
snippet: The **Conway genus symbol** of an integral lattice $L$ is an invariant of the genus of $L$. It is given in the form $\text{I}_{r,s}(\cdots)$ or $\text{II}_{r,s}(\cdots)$ where the Roman numeral $\text{I}$ or $\text{II}$ indicates the type of $L$, and $(r,s)$ is the signature of $L$. For a complete description of the Conway symbol, see Conway-Sloane, Chapter 15.

title: Covering radius
snippet: **Covering radius** (also known as **inhomogeneous minimum**) of a convex $\boldsymbol 0$-symmetric set $K$ in $\mathbb R^n$ with respect to a full-rank lattice $L \subset \mathbb R^n$ is $\mu = \min \{ t > 0 : \bigcup_{\boldsymbol x \in L} (\boldsymbol x + tK) = \mathbb R^n \}$. Covering radius satisfies Jarn\'ik's inequality: $$\frac{1}{2} \lambda_n \leq \mu \leq \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i \leq \frac{n}{2} \lambda_n,$$ where $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$ are the successive minima of $K$ with respect to $L$.

title: Data for an integral lattice
snippet:
This integral lattice is known with name .

.

This knowl can display comments about an integral lattice, if comments are specified as a parameter. Similarly, also any known name for an integral lattice is displayed, provided that it is specified as a parameter.

title: Integral Lattice
snippet: Let $V$ be an $n$-dimensional vector space over a topological field $F$ (with $\text{char}(F) \neq 2$) equipped with a nonsingular symmetric bilinear map $B:V\times V\rightarrow F$ and associated quadratic map $Q(v):=B(v,v).$ The pair $(V,Q)$ is a quadratic space. Fixing a basis, $\{v_1,...,v_n\}$ for $V$, the space $(V,Q)$ has an associated quadratic form, that is, a homogeneous degree 2 polynomial, given by \[ f(x_1,...,x_n)=\sum_{i,j}B(v_i,v_j)x_ix_j, \] where $B(v_i,v_j)=B(v_j,v_i)$ since $B$ is symmetric.

title: Density of an integral lattice
snippet: The density of a positive definite integral lattice $L$ of rank $n$ is the density of the lattice-centered sphere packing in $\mathbb R^n$. The volume of a sphere in dimension $n$ is \[ V_n = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{(n/2)!} = \frac{2^n \pi^{(n-1)/2}((n-1)/2)!}{n!}, \] where the first form is most useful when $n$ is even and the second form most useful when $n$ is odd. Suppose that $L$ has Gram matrix $G$ and minimum $2\rho$. Then the density of $L$ is \[ \Delta = \frac{V_n \rho^n}{\sqrt{\det G}} \] while the center density of $L$ is \[ \delta = \frac{\Delta}{V_n} = \frac{\rho^n}{\sqrt{\det G}}.

title: Determinant of an integral lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice $L$, the **determinant** of $L$ is defined as the determinant of the Gram matrix associated to any basis of $L$. If $L$ is positive definite of rank $n$, then the determinant of $L$ is also the square of the volume of the fundamental parallelotope of the Euclidean embedding of $L$ in $\R^n$.

title: Rank of an integral lattice
snippet: The **rank** of an integral lattice is the rank of its underlying free $\Z$-module.

title: Discriminant of an integral lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice $L$, the discriminant $D$ of $L$ is defined as the determinant of the Gram matrix associated to any basis of $L$. There are several different conventions for the term "discriminant" in the literature; we give them different names here for clarity. The definition we take above is used in Cassels' *Rational Quadratic Forms* and Conway-Sloane's *Sphere Packings, Lattices and Groups*. * The Witt discriminant of $L$ is the quantity $(-1)^{\binom{n}{2}} D$.

title: Discriminant group
snippet: For an integral lattice $L$ with associated bilinear form $B$, the **discriminant group** is the finite abelian group $L^\vee / L$, where $L^\vee$ is the dual lattice of $L$. The discriminant group is trivial if and only if $L$ is self-dual (i.e. unimodular). The discriminant group $L^\vee/L$ is equipped with a **torsion bilinear form**: $$ B_L\colon L^\vee/L\times L^\vee/L\to \mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z},\, (u+L, v+L)\mapsto B(u,v)+\mathbb{Z}.$$ If the integral lattice $L$ is even, the group $L^\vee/L$ is moreover equipped with a **torsion quadratic form**: $$ Q_L\colon…

title: Dual lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice, $L$, its **dual** is the set $L^\#=\{\vec{v}\in \Q L:B(\vec{v},L)\subseteq \mathbb Z\}$. There is a unique rescaling of the bilinear map $B$ for which $L^\#$ is a primitive integral lattice; we will refer to the resulting integral lattice as the rescaled dual of $L$.

title: Euclidean basis of a lattice
snippet: Let $L$ be a positive definite integral lattice of rank $n$ with Gram matrix $G = (g_{ij})$. A **Euclidean basis** for $L$ is a collection of vectors $v_1, \dots, v_n \in \R^n$ such that $$ \langle v_i, v_j \rangle = g_{ij}, $$ for all $i, j$. Equivalently, if $B$ denotes the matrix whose rows are $v_i$, then $G = BB^T$. This gives an explicit realization of the lattice $L \hookrightarrow \R^n$ as a discrete lattice in $\R^n$, with respect to the usual dot product norm.

title: Eutactic lattice
snippet: A lattice $L$ in $\mathbb R^n$ is called **eutactic** if there exist positive real numbers $c_1,\dots,c_m$ such that for every $v \in \mathbb R^n$, the inner product $$(v,v) = \sum_{j=1}^n c_j (v,x_j)^2,$$ where the sum is over all the minimal vectors of $L$. A eutactic lattice is well-rounded, and there are only finitely many similarity classes of eutactic lattices in each dimension $n \geq 2$. A lattice is called **strongly eutactic** if the eutaxy coefficients $c_1 = \dots = c_m$. Strongly eutactic lattices are arithmetic.

title: Even sublattice
snippet: The **even sublattice** of an integral lattice $L$ is the sublattice of $L$ spanned by vectors of even length. An integral lattice $L$ is even exactly when it is equal to its even sublattice. In general, the even sublattice $L_0$ of an integral lattice $L$ has index at most equal to 2.

title: Extreme lattice
snippet: A lattice $L$ in $\mathbb R^n$ is called **extreme** if it is a local maximum of the packing density function in $\mathbb R^n$. A theorem of Voronoi asserts that a lattice is extreme if and only if it is perfect and eutactic.

title: Festi-Veniani index of a lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice $L$, the **Festi--Veniani index** of $L$ is the index of the automorphism group of $L$ in the automorphism group of the discriminant group $L^\vee/L$.

title: Genus of an integral lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice, $L$ with underlying quadratic space $V$, the **genus** of $L$ is the set of all lattices $M$ on $V$, such that $M_p\cong L_p$ for all primes $p$.

title: Label of a genus of lattices
snippet: Label is in the format $r.s.d.j_1.j_2....j_k.x$, where - $r$ is the rank of the lattices - $s$ is the signature ($n_+ - n_-$) - $d$ is the absolute value of the determinant - If $p_1, \ldots , p_k$ are the primes whose squares divide $d$ ($p_i^2 \mid d$), then $j_1, \ldots ,j_k$ are corresponding rank decompositions of their Jordan forms, omitting the first, encoded in base $62$ (digits $0-9$, then lowercase $a-z$, then uppercase $A-Z$). For example, if the pairs of (valuation, rank) appearing in the decomposition are $(3, 1), (4,10), (6,37)$, it will be encoded as 01a0B.

title: Gram matrix
snippet: Fixing a basis $\{v_1,...,v_n\}$ for a space $V$, the **Gram matrix** is the $n\times n$ symmetric matrix given by $[B(v_i,v_j)]$. Similarly, if we fix a basis $\{w_1,...,w_n\}$ for a lattice $L$, then it is also a basis for $V$, and the Gram matrix for $L$ is given by $[B(w_i,w_j)]$. For example, given the integral lattice $\mathbb Z^3$ on the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^3$, in the standard basis $\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}$ the lattice (and hence the space) has a Gram matrix \[ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0\\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.

title: Group order of an integral lattice
snippet: The **group order** of an integral lattice is the size of its automorphism group.

title: Hermite number
snippet: The **Hermite number** of a positive definite integral lattice $L$, with absolute determinant $d$, is given by \[ \frac{m(L)}{d^{\frac{1}{n}}}, \] where $n$ is the rank of $L$, and $m(L)$ denotes the minimum of $L$.

title: History of Lattices
snippet: Integral lattices have a deep connection to the objects in the LMFDB in that every lattice, $L$, has an associated theta series, $\Theta_L$, which is a holomorphic modular form of half-integral weight. Specifically, the theta series are a type of Siegel modular form, first introduced by Siegel in the 1930's \cite{MR0332663}. Later, in 1973, Shimura developed a partial correspondence lifting half-integral weight theta series to full integral weight series \cite{MR0332663}, allowing lattices to be studied using the full analytic machinery of modular forms.

title: Hyperbolic plane
snippet: The hyperbolic plane $U$ is the integral lattice with Gram matrix \[ \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}. \] Over a field with characteristic not $2$, every nondegenerate indefinite quadratic space is isometric to a direct sum of a definite quadratic space and some number of copies of the hyperbolic plane. A version also holds for even unimodular lattices, which can be written as $U^{\oplus r} \oplus E_8^{\oplus s}$ or $U^{\oplus r} \oplus E_8(-1)^{\oplus s}$. But this direct sum decomposition does not hold for lattices.

title: Lattice Invariants
snippet: The basic invariants of an integral lattice $L$ are: - the rank $n$, - the signature $(n_+, n_-)$, - the determinant, - the discriminant, - the level, - the class number.

title: Isometries between integral lattices
snippet: Two integral lattices, $L$ and $L'$, are **isometric** if there exists a map $\sigma:L\rightarrow L'$ such that $Q(\vec{v})=Q(\sigma(\vec{v}))$ for all $\vec{v}\in L$. If this is the case, we say $L\cong L'$. The set of all lattices $M$ such that $M\cong L$ is called the **isometry class** (or simply the **class**) of $L$. If $L$ and $L'$ have associated Gram matrices $G$ and $G'$, then $L\cong L'$ implies that $G=T\cdot G'\cdot T^t$ for some $T\in GL_n(\mathbb Z)$.

title: Kissing number
snippet: The **kissing number** of a lattice, $L$, is the number of vectors of minimal length. The vectors in the list of minimal length vectors are normalized, so the kissing number will always be twice as large as the number of vectors in this list. The **lattice kissing number problem** in dimension $n$ is to find the lattices of dimension $n$ which maximize the kissing number. In general, these may not be solutions of the **general kissing number problem**, which asks for the maximum number of spheres with non-overlapping interiors in dimension $n$ which are all tangent to a single sphere.

title: LMFDB label of an integral lattice
snippet: The label of a lattice is given by dimension.determinant.level.class_number.N where N is a counter enumerating lattices for which the first four parameters are identical. If the class number is not available, then the corresponding field is set to 0.

title: Level of an integral lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice, $L$, the **level** is the smallest integer, $N$, such that $N\cdot B(\vec{v},\vec{v})\in 2\mathbb Z$ for all $\vec{v}$ in the dual of $L$. This is related to the exponent $e$ of the discriminant group: either $N = e$ or $N = 2e$. You can determine when the second case occurs using the Conway genus symbol: the level will be $2e$ when the highest $2$-adic block is of type I, and it will be $e$ when the highest $2$-adic block is of type II (or if $2$ does not divide the discriminant).

title: Mass of a lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice $L$, the **mass** of $L$ is the weighted sum $$ \sum_{\Lambda} \frac{1}{\# \text{Aut} (\Lambda)} $$ where the sum runs over all isometry classes of lattices $\Lambda$ in the same genus as $L$. Here $\text{Aut}(\Lambda)$ denotes the automorphism group of $\Lambda$.

title: Minimum
snippet: For an integral lattice, $L$, the **minimum**, denoted $m(L)$, is the length of the shortest non-zero vector in $L$. A **minimal vector** in $L$ is a vector $\vec{v}$ such that $Q(\vec{v})=m(L)$.

title: Minimum
snippet: For an integral lattice, $L$, the **minimum**, denoted $m(L)$, is the length of the shortest non-zero vector in $L$. A **minimal vector** in $L$ is a vector $\vec{v}$ such that $Q(\vec{v})=m(L)$.

title: Name of a lattice
snippet: Certain integral lattices have names in the LMFDB. These include root lattices (A2...A14, D3=A3, E8), duals of root lattices (A3\*=D3\*), square lattices (Z2, Z3), and the Leech lattice (Leech).

title: Nondegenerate lattice
snippet: An integral lattice $L$ with bilinear form $B$ is nondegenerate if \[ \{w \in L : B(v, w) = 0 \text{ for all } v \in L\} = 0. \] Otherwise, we call $L$ degenerate. All lattices in the database are nondegenerate, but some definitions require degenerate lattices. In particular, the signature of a (possibly degenerate) lattice is a triple $(n_+, n_0, n_-)$ where $n_+$ and $n_-$ are as in the standard signature and $n_0$ is the number of $0$ eigenvalues for a Gram matrix. A lattice is degenerate if and only if $n_0 > 0$. We say $L$ is positive semidefinite if $n_-=0$.

title: Norm of a vector
snippet: Given an integral lattice $L$ with associated bilinear map $B$, the **norm** of a vector $v \in L$ is $B(v, v)$. If $L$ has Gram matrix $A$, then the norm of $v$ is $v^T A v$.

title: Norm 1 sublattice
snippet: Given an integral lattice $L$, the **norm 1 sublattice** is the sublattice of $L$ generated by all vectors of norm 1 in $L$. If $L$ is even, then the norm 1 sublattice is trivial. If $L =I_n$, then the norm 1 sublattice is the lattice $I_n$ itself.

title: Normalized minimal vector of a lattice
snippet: For an integral lattice $L$, the minimal vectors of $L$ come in pairs: if $\vec{v}$ is a minimal vector, then so is $-\vec{v}$. A minimal vector is **normalized** if its first nonzero component is positive.

title: Orthogonal decomposition of a lattice
snippet: Let $L$ be an integral lattice, with bilinear form $B : L \times L \to \Z$. An **orthogonal decomposition** of $L$ is a direct sum decomposition $$ L = L_1 \oplus L_2 \oplus \dots \oplus L_r $$ such that $B(x_i, x_j) = 0$ for all $x_i \in L_i$, $x_j \in L_j$, where $i \not = j$. Equivalently, if $G$ is the Gram matrix of $L$, then after a suitable change of basis, it has the block diagonal form $$ G = \begin{pmatrix} G_1 & & & \\ & G_2 & & \\ & & \ddots & \\ & & & G_r \end{pmatrix} $$ where each $G_i$ is a Gram matrix for $L_i$.

title: Parity of a lattice
snippet: If an integral lattice $L$ has Gram matrix $M$, we say that $L$ is even if all of the diagonal entries of $M$ are even, and odd otherwise.

title: Perfect lattice
snippet: A lattice $L$ in $\mathbb R^n$ is called **perfect** if the space of $n \times n$ real symmetric matrices is spanned by the set of $n \times n$ symmetric matrices $$\{ x x^{\top} : x \in S(L) \},$$ where $S(L)$ is the set of minimal vectors of $L$. A perfect lattice is well-rounded, arithmetic, and is uniquely determined by its minimal vectors. Further, there are only finitely many similarity classes of perfect lattices in each dimension $n \geq 2$.

title: p-Neighbor of a lattice
snippet: Given an integral lattice $L$ and a prime $p$, a **$p$-neighbor** of $L$ is an integral lattice $M$ such that $$ [L : L \cap M ] = [M : L \cap M ] = p.$$ We denote the $p$-neighborhood relation as $L \sim_p M$. This is a symmetric relation, but in general is not reflexive nor transitive. If two lattices are $p$-neighbors, then they lie in the same genus.

title: p-Neighbor adjacency matrix of a lattice
snippet: Let $\mathcal{G}$ be a genus of integral lattices, with class number $h$ and with representatives $\{L_1, L_2, \dots, L_h\}$. The **p-neighbor adjacency graph** of $\mathcal{G}$ is the weighted directed graph with $h$ vertices $\{L_1, L_2, \dots, L_h\}$, and with a directed edge from $L_i$ to $L_j$, where the edge weight is the number of $p$-neighbors of $L_i$ which are isometric to $L_j$. The **$p$-neighbor adjacency matrix** is the adjacency matrix of the $p$-neighbor adjacency graph. In general, the $p$-neighbor adjacency matrix does not need to be symmetric, and can contain self-edges.

title: Positive definite lattice
snippet: An integral lattice $L$ is **positive definite** if the lattice.gram of the bilinear form $B$ on $L$ is a positive definite matrix. This is equivalent to the condition that $B(u,u)>0$ for all $u \in L \setminus\{0\}$. The signature of a rank $n$ positive definite integral lattice is $(n, 0)$.

title: Positive definite lattice
snippet: An integral lattice $L$ is **positive definite** if the lattice.gram of the bilinear form $B$ on $L$ is a positive definite matrix. This is equivalent to the condition that $B(u,u)>0$ for all $u \in L \setminus\{0\}$. The signature of a rank $n$ positive definite integral lattice is $(n, 0)$.

title: Primitive integral lattice
snippet: An integral lattice, $L$, with an associated Gram matrix, $G=[g_{ij}]$, is called **primitive** if $\gcd(\{g_{ij}:1\leq i\leq n,i\leq j\leq n\})=1$.

title: Quadratic form of a lattice
snippet: Given an integral lattice $L$ with Gram matrix $G$, the quadratic form of $L$ is the function on $L \otimes \mathbb{Q}$ defined by \[ Q(\mathbf{v}) = \langle \mathbf{v},\mathbf{v}\rangle = \mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{T}}G\mathbf{v}. \] We will refer to the value $Q(v)$ as the norm of $v$. By construction, $Q(L) \subseteq \mathbb{Z}$, and we say that $Q$ or $L$ is universal if $Q(L) = \mathbb{Z}$. An even lattice can never be universal since $Q(L) \subseteq 2\mathbb{Z}$; we say that $L$ is even universal if $Q(L) \supseteq 2\mathbb{Z}$.

title: Root lattice
snippet: A **root system** is a finite set $\Phi$ of nonzero vectors (called **roots**) in a Euclidean space $E$ satisfying the following conditions. - The roots span $E$. - For $\alpha \in \Phi$, the only scalar multiples of $\alpha$ in $E$ are $\pm \alpha$. - For $\alpha, \beta \in \Phi$, the quantity $2 \frac{\alpha \cdot \beta}{\alpha \cdot \alpha}$ is an integer. - For $\alpha,\beta \in \Phi$, we have $\beta - 2 \frac{\alpha \cdot \beta}{\alpha \cdot \alpha} \alpha \in \Phi$. That is, $\Phi$ is stable under the reflection through the hyperplane perpendicular to $\alpha$.

title: Root sublattice
snippet: The **root sublattice** of a positive definite integral lattice $L$ is the sublattice of $L$ spanned by vectors of length at most equal to 2. A positive definite integral lattice $L$ is called a **root lattice** if it is equal to its root sublattice.

title: Find an integral lattice
snippet: To find a specific integral lattice, enter its label or name.

title: Signature of an integral lattice
snippet: For a nondegenerate integral lattice $L$, the **signature** of $L$ is a pair $(n_+, n_-)$, where $n_+$ (resp. $n_-$) is the number of positive (resp. negative) eigenvalues of a Gram matrix for $L$. Note that the signature of a degenerate lattice has an extra component $n_0$ for the number of $0$ eigenvalues. The signature classifies lattices up to isometry over the real numbers. If $L$ has signature $(n,0)$ we say it is positive definite; if it has signature $(0,n)$ we say it is negative definite; otherwise we say it is indefinite.

title: Similarity of lattices
snippet: Lattices $L$ and $M$ are called **similar** if there exists a real constant $\alpha$ so that $L$ is isometric to $\alpha M$. Key properties of lattices, such as well-roundedness, eutaxy, perfection, packing density are preserved on similarity classes. Each similarity class that contains an integer lattice contains a unique primitive integer lattice.

title: Spherical design lattice
snippet: A collection of $m \geq 1$ points $\{ v_1,\dots,v_m \}$ on a sphere $S_n(r)$ of radius $r >0$ in $\mathbb R^n$ centered at the origin is a called a **spherical $t$-design** if for every polynomial $f(x) = f(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ in $n$ variables with real coefficients of degree no bigger than $t$, $t \geq 1$, $$\int_{S_n(r)} f(x) dx = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m f(v_i),$$ where the measure $dx$ is normalized so that the measure of $S_n(r)$ is $1$. A lattice $L$ is called a **spherical $t$-design lattice** if its set of minimal vectors $S(L)$ is a spherical $t$-design.

title: Sublattice
snippet: Given an integral lattice $L$, a **sublattice** of $L$ is a subgroup $M \subseteq L$ which is itself a lattice. There are several special named sublattices of $L$: - **Norm 1 sublattice** - the sublattice generated by vecotrs of norm $1$. - **Root sublattice** - the sublattice generated by all vectors of norm $2$. - **Min vector sublattice** - the sublattice generated by all minimal vectors. - **Even sublattice** - the sublattice generated by vectors of even norm. - **Max diag sublattice** - the maximal sublattice whose Gram matrix is diagonal.

title: Successive minima
snippet: **Successive minima** $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$ of a $\boldsymbol 0$-symmetric convex body $K \subset \mathbb R^n$ with respect to a full-rank lattice $L \subset \mathbb R^n$ is defined as $\lambda_i = \min \{ t > 0 : \text{dim}_{\mathbb R} \text{span}_{\mathbb R}(tK \cap L) \geq i \}$. Then $0 < \lambda_1 \leq \dots \leq \lambda_n$, and $$\frac{2^n \text{det}(L)}{n! \text{Vol}(K)} \leq \lambda_1 \cdots \lambda_n \leq \frac{2^n \text{det}(L)}{\text{Vol}(K)},$$ by Minkowski's Successive Minima Theorem. Successive minima of $L$ are the successive minima of the unit ball with respect to $L$.

title: Tensor decomposition of a lattice
snippet: Given integral lattices $L_1$ and $L_2$, we define the **tensor product** of $L_1$ and $L_2$ as the lattice $L = L_1 \otimes_{\Z} L_2$, where the associated bilinear form is defined as $$ B( v_1 \otimes w_1, v_2 \otimes w_2 ) = B_{L_1} ( v_1, v_2) B_{L_2} (w_1, w_2), $$ extended bilinearly. The rank of $L$ is the product of the ranks of $L_1$ and $L_2$, and the determinant of $L$ is $ \det(L_1)^{\operatorname{rank}(L_2)} \cdot \det(L_2)^{\operatorname{rank}(L_1)}$.

title: Theta series for an integral lattice
snippet: The **theta series** $\Theta_L$ of an integral lattice $L$ is a generating function for the number of vectors $v\in L$ of norm $n$. Specifically, we define \[ \Theta_L(q) = \sum_{v \in L} q^{\frac{1}{2}\langle v,v \rangle} \] If $L$ has rank $n$ and level $N$ and we take $q = e^{2\pi i z}$ as usual, this will be a modular form of weight $k = \frac{n}{2}$ and level $N$. In the case that $n$ is odd, to get a classical modular form with integer weight one may apply the Shimura lift, which will produce a modular form of weight $n-1$.

title: Unimodular lattice
snippet: An integral lattice is **unimodular** if its determinant is equal to 1.

title: Well-rounded lattice
snippet: A lattice $L$ is called **well-rounded** if its set of minimal vectors $S(L)$ spans a sublattice $M$ of finite index, and $L$ is said to be **generated by minimal vectors** if $M = L$. The lattice $L$ is **strongly well-rounded** if the set $S(L)$ contains a basis for $L$. Being generated by minimal vectors is a strictly stronger condition than well-rounded for lattices of rank $\geq 5$, and a strictly weaker condition than strongly well-rounded for lattices of rank $\geq 10$.

title: Local algebra
snippet: This knowl can display data for a local algebras specified by a list of labels (in the form of a string) as a parameter.

title: Associated inertia
snippet: Let $L/K$ be a finite extension, let $L^{un}/K$ be the maximal unramified subextension of $L/K$, and let $\varphi \in L^{un}[x]$ be an Eisenstein polynomial defining $L/L^{un}$. The **associated inertia** of a segment of the ramification polygon of $\varphi$ is the least common multiple of the degrees of the irreducible factors of the residual polynomial of that segment. The associated inertias are divisors of the inertia degree of $\varphi$ and are invariants of $L/K$.

title: Automorphism group of a field extension
snippet: If $K/F$ is an extension of fields, its **automorphism group** is $$ \textrm{Aut}(K/F) = \{\sigma:K\to K\mid \forall a\in F, \sigma(a)=a, \text{ and } \sigma \text{ is an isomorphism}\}.$$ Note, a finite extension is Galois if and only if $|\textrm{Aut}(K/F)| = [K:F]$.

title: Defining polynomial of a local field
snippet: The **defining polynomial** of a $p$-adic field $K$ is an irreducible polynomial $f(x)\in\mathbb{Q}_p[x]$ such that $K\cong \mathbb{Q}_p(a)$, where $a$ is a root of $f(x)$. The defining polynomial can be chosen to be monic with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}_p$; by Krasner's lemma, we can further take $f(x)\in \mathbb{Z}[x]$. The LMFDB uses the following conventions for choosing defining polynomials: 1. For unramified extensions, the polynomial needs to be irreducible modulo $p$. When it is feasible to compute, we use the Conway polynomial for the residue prime $p$ and the given degree. 2.

title: Degree of a local field extension
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields. The **degree** of $L/K$ is the dimension of $L$ as a vector space over $K$. The **base degree** $n_0$ of $L/K$ is the degree of $K/\Q_p$. The **absolute degree** $n_{\mathrm{abs}}$ of $L/K$ is the degree of $L/\Q_p$.

title: Discriminant exponent of a local field
snippet: Let $L/K$ be a finite extension of $p$-adic fields, with rings of integers $\mathcal{O}_L$ and $\mathcal{O}_K$ and uniformizers $\pi_L$ and $\pi_K$. The **discriminant** of $L/K$ is the square of the determinant of the matrix \[ \left( \begin{array}{ccc} \sigma_1(\beta_1) & \cdots & \sigma_1(\beta_n) \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ \sigma_n(\beta_1) & \cdots & \sigma_n(\beta_n) \\ \end{array} \right) \] where $\sigma_1,..., \sigma_n$ are the embeddings of $L$ into an algebraic closure $\overline{K}$, and $\{\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n\}$ is a basis for $\mathcal{O}_L$ as a free $\mathcal{O}_K$-module.

title: Discriminant root field of a local field
snippet: Note if $F=\mathbb{Q}_p$, there are only a small number of possibilities for quadratic extensions of $\mathbb{Q}_p$. If $p$ is odd, we let $u$ denote any unit in $\mathbb{Z}_p$ which is not a square modulo $p$. Then, the quadratic extensions of $\mathbb{Q}_p$ are $\mathbb{Q}_p(\sqrt{u})$ (which is the unramified quadratic extension), $\mathbb{Q}_p(\sqrt{p})$, and $\mathbb{Q}_p(\sqrt{pu})$. For $p=2$, there are seven quadratic extensions of $\mathbb{Q}_2$.

title: Eisenstein diagram
snippet: Let $K$ be a $p$-adic field whose residue field has $q$ elements. Let $I/K$ be a Herbrand invariant with ramification index $e = \epsilon p^w$ and residue field degree $f$. Then $I$ can be expressed in terms of Swan slopes as $I=[s_1, \dots, s_w]_{\epsilon}^f$, in terms of means as $I=\langle m_1,\dots,m_w\rangle_{\epsilon}^f$, or in terms of rams as $I=(r_1, \dots, r_w)_{\epsilon}^f$. The **Eisenstein diagram of $I/K$** gives a visual summary of the process that produces the generic polynomial \[x^e + \pi \sum_\sigma f_\sigma \pi^j x^i\] associated to $I/K$.

title: Eisenstein form
snippet:
Let $K$ be a $p$-adic field, $\mathcal{O}_K$ its ring of integers, and $\pi$ a uniformizer for $\mathcal{O}_K$. A monic polynomial $f\in \mathcal{O}_K[x]$ is of **Eisenstein form** if there exists a monic polynomial $
u(x)\in\mathcal{O}_K[x]$, irreducible modulo $\pi$, and \(e\in\Z^+\) such that $$ f(x)=
u(x)^e+\sum_{i=1}^{e-1}g_i(x)
u(x)^i+g_0(x)$$ where for all \(0\le i\le e-1\), \(g_i\in\pi\mathcal{O}_K[x]\) with \(\deg g_i<\deg
u\), and the minimum of the valuation of the coefficients of \(g_0\) is one.

title: Eisenstein polynomial
snippet: Let $D$ be an integral domain and $P$ a prime ideal in $D$. A polynomial $a_n x^n + \cdots + a_0\in D[x]$ is **Eisenstein** (with respect to $P$) if - $a_n \not\in P$ - $a_i \in P$ for all $0\leq i < n$ - $a_0 \not\in P^2$ An Eisenstein polynomial is irreducible in $F[x]$ where $F$ is the field of fractions of $D$. If $K$ is a finite extension of $\Q_p$ with ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$, then a finite extension $L/K$ is totally ramified if and only if $L=K(\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is a root of an Eisenstein polynomial in $\mathcal{O}_K[x]$.

title: Ambiguity of a family
snippet: Let $K$ be be a $p$-adic field and let $I=(r_1,\dots,r_w)_{\epsilon}^f$ be a Herbrand invariant which is compatible with $K$. Let $f_0$ be the residue field degree of $K/\Q_p$, and set $q=p^{f_0f}$. Let $r_{i_1},\dots,r_{i_k}$ be the distinct integral rams of $I$, and for $1\le j\le k$ let $\rho_j$ be the multiplicity of $r_{i_j}$ in $I$.

title: Base field of a family of $p$-adic field extensions
snippet: A family of extensions of $p$-adic fields is a collection of extensions $L/K$ with $K$ a fixed $p$-adic field. The field $K$ is called the **base field** of the family.

title: $p$-adic field family diagrams
snippet: There are three diagrams that are helpful for visualizing a $p$-adic family with wild ramification: * The Eisenstein diagram, which explains the construction of the defining polynomial of the family. * The ramification polygon, whose slopes are the rams of every extension in the family. * The graph of the Herbrand function of the family, which helps clarify the relationship between slopes, rams and means.

title: Field count
snippet: Let $I/K$ be a family of extensions of $p$-adic fields. The **field count** of $I/K$ is the number of extensions in $I/K$.

title: $p$-adic family invariants
snippet: The following extension invariants are the same for all $L/K$ in a $p$-adic family $I/K$: - the residue field characteristic $p$, - the degree $d$, - the base field $K$, - the ramification index $e$, - the residue field degree $f$. - the discriminant exponent $c$, - the Artin slopes $[\tilde{s}_1,\dots,\tilde{s}_w]$, - the Swan slopes $[s_1,\dots,s_w]$, - the means $\langle m_1,\dots,m_w\rangle$, - the rams $(r_1,\dots,r_w)$. The following data is associated to the family as a whole: - the field count $N$, - the ambiguity $C$, - the mass $M$, - the absolute mass $M_{\rm abs}$,

title: Family labels
snippet: Let $K$ be a finite extension of $\Q_p$, with $K\ne\Q_p$. The label associated to a relative family $I/K$ has the form $s\text{-}f.e.c\ell$, where - $s$ is the label attached to the base field $K$, - $f$ is the residue field degree of every $L/K\in I/K$, - $e$ is the ramification index of every $L/K\in I/K$, - $c$ is the discriminant exponent of every $L/K\in I/K$, - $\ell$ is a string of one or more letters used to distinguish families with the same basic data.

title: Mass of a family
snippet: Let $I/K$ be a family of extensions of $p$-adic fields with base field $K$. Let $f$ be the residue field degree of the family, and let $K_f$ be the unramified extension of $K$ of degree $f$, which is contained in every $L/K \in I/K$. To each $L/K\in I/K$ we assign a **mass** $1/|\operatorname{Aut}(L/K_f)|$. The **mass of the family** $I/K$ is the sum of the masses of the elements of $I/K$. We also assign an **absolute mass** $1/|\operatorname{Aut}(L/\Q_p)|$ to each $L/K\in I/K$, and define the **absolute mass of the family** to be the sum of the absolute masses of the elements of $I/K$.

title: Families of extensions of local fields
snippet: Let $K$ be a $p$-adic field whose residue field has cardinality $q$ and let $I=[s_1,\dots,s_w]_{\epsilon}^f$ be a Herbrand invariant. The **family** $I/K$ of extensions associated to this data is the set of all $K$-isomorphism classes of extensions $L/K$ of residue field degree $f$ and ramification index $e=\epsilon p^w$ whose Swan slopes are $s_1,\dots,s_w$ (see Monge \cite{MR:3256847,arXiv:1109.4617,doi:10.1142/S1793042114500511}). If $K=\Q_p$ we say that $I/\Q_p$ is an **absolute family**; otherwise say $I/K$ is a **relative family**.

title: Data that varies within families
snippet: The following invariants of field extensions may not be the same for all $L/K$ in a $p$-adic family $I/K$. For each invariant we list the values that occur for extensions in the family. - the Galois group $\operatorname{Gal}(L^{\rm gal}/K)$, - the hidden Artin slopes $[\tilde{s}_{w+1},\dots,\tilde{s}_{w+u}]$ of $L/K$, - the indices of inseparability $[i_0,i_1,\dots,i_w]$ of $L/K$, - the associated inertia $[I_1,\dots,I_v]$ of $L/K$, - the jump set $[j_0,j_1,\dots,j_w]$ of $L$.

title: Data for a local field
snippet: This knowl can display data for a local number field specified by its label as a parameter.

title: Label for a $p$-adic field
snippet: Each $p$-adic family has a label of the form "p.f.e.cA", where - p is the residue field characteristic, - f is the residue field degree of $K$ over $\mathbb{Q}_p$, - e is the ramification index of $K$ over $\mathbb{Q}_p$, - c is the discriminant exponent of $K$ over $\mathbb{Q}_p$, - A is a lower case letter code, with families sorted by their number of wild segments, then the list of lengths of the wild segments, then the rams.

title: p-adic field label
snippet: The label associated to a $p$-adic field $K$ has the form $p.f.e.c\ell m.n$, where - $p$ is the characteristic of the residue field of $K$. - $f$ is the residue field degree of $K/\Q_p$. - $e$ is the ramification index of $K/\Q_p$, - $c$ is the discriminant exponent of $K/\Q_p$, - $\ell$ is a string of one or more letters used to identify the absolute family $I/\Q_p$ that contains $K/\Q_p$, - $m$ identifies the subfamily of $I/\Q_p$ that contains $K/\Q_p$, - $n$ is a number used to distinguish fields in the same subfamily.

title: Invariants of the Galois closure
snippet: Let $K$ be a $p$-adic field and let $K^{gal}/\Q_p$ be the Galois closure of $K/\Q_p$. Some invariants associated to $K^{gal}/\Q_p$ are - The Galois group $G=\Gal(K^{gal}/\Q_p)$ of $K^{gal}/\Q_p$, - The inertia subgroup $I$ of $G$, - The wild inertia subgroup of $I$, - The unramified degree of $K^{gal}/\Q_p$, - The tame degree of $K^{gal}/\Q_p$, - The Artin slopes of $K^{gal}/\Q_p$, - The Swan slopes of $K^{gal}/\Q_p$, - The Galois mean slope of $K^{gal}/\Q_p$, - A Galois splitting model for $K/\Q_p$, if one is available.

title: Galois invariants of a local field
snippet: Let $K^{gal}$ be the Galois closure of a $p$-adic field $K$. The first Galois invariant of $K$ is the Galois group $G=\Gal(K^{gal}/\mathbb{Q}_p)$. Most of the remaining invariants relate to the filtration on $G$ by higher ramification groups. Let $G^i$ be the $i$th higher ramification group in upper numbering from Serre's Local Fields \cite{doi:10.1007/978-1-4757-5673-9}. They form a descending filtration of normal subgroups where $i$ is a continuous parameter, $i\geq -1$. Let $G^{(j)} = G^{j-1}$, and $G^{(j+)} = \cap_{\epsilon>0} G^{(j+\epsilon)}$. Then - $G^{(0)}=G$.

title: Galois mean slope
snippet: Let $K$ be a finite Galois extension of $\Q_p$ for some prime $p$. Its **Galois mean slope** is the nonnegative rational number $\alpha$ such that the discriminant ideal for $K$ over $\Q_p$ is $(p^{[K:\Q_p]\cdot \alpha})$. The Galois mean slope can be computed as a weighted average of the wild Artin slopes and tame degree of $K$.

title: Galois splitting model of a local field
snippet: Let $K$ be a finite extension of $\Q_p$. A **Galois splitting model** of $K$ is an irreducible polynomial $f\in\Q[x]$ such that $K\cong \Q_p[x]/(f)$, and the Galois group of $f$ over $\Q$ is isomorphic to the Galois group of $f$ over $\Q_p$. Most, but not all, local number fields have Galois splitting models. With a Galois splitting model, the computation of various invariants related to $K$, such as the Galois invariants, can be carried out more easily using $f$.

title: Heights of an extension
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields. The **height** $H(L/K)$ of $L/K$ is defined by the formula $c=f(e−1+H(L/K))$, where $c$ is the discriminant exponent, $f$ the residue field degree, and $e$ the ramification degree of $L/K$. Since $H(L/K)$ is 0 if $L/K$ is tamely ramified, and positive otherwise, $H(L/K)$ is a measure of the wild ramification of $L/K$. The height $H(L/K)$ can be expressed in terms of the mean $m$ of $L/K$ by $H(L/K)=em$. If $L/K$ is unramified (i.e., $f=1$) then $H(L/K)$ is equal to the 0th index of inseparability $i_0$.

title: Herbrand function of a family
snippet: Let $I/K$ be a family of extensions of a $p$-adic field $K$. We can express $I$ in terms of rams as $I=(r_1,\dots,r_w)_{\epsilon}^f$, in terms of Swan slopes as $I=[s_1,\dots,s_w]_{\epsilon}^f$ or in terms of the means $\langle m_1,\dots,m_w\rangle_\epsilon^f$. The **Herbrand function** of $I/K$ is the piecewise linear function $\phi_{I/K}:\R_{\ge0}\rightarrow\R_{\ge0}$ whose graph is obtained by joining the points $(0,0),(r_1,s_1),\dots,(r_w,s_w)$ with line segments; in addition, there is a ray with slope $1/(\epsilon p^w)$ starting at $(r_w,s_w)$.

title: Specifying a Herbrand invariant
snippet: To specify a Herbrand invariant you can enter rational numbers inside square brackets to give the Swan slopes, enter rational numbers inside parentheses to give the rams, or enter rational numbers inside angle brackets to give the means. You should not include the residue field degree or ramification index, since those are specified in another search input.

title: Herbrand invariant
snippet: A **Herbrand invariant** $I$ is the collection of ramification data associated to a finite separable extension of $p$-adic fields. Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields with residue characteristic $p$. Let $f$ be the residue field degree and $\epsilon p^w$ the ramification index of $L/K$, with $p\nmid\epsilon$. The Herbrand invariant of $L/K$ can be expressed in terms of the Swan slopes of $L/K$ as $I=[s_1,\dots,s_w]_{\epsilon}^f$; in terms of the rams of $L/K$ as $I=(r_1,\dots,r_w)_{\epsilon}^f$; or in terms of the means of $L/K$ as $I=\langle m_1,\dots,m_w\rangle_{\epsilon}^f$.

title: Hidden slopes of an extension
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields and let $L^{Gal}/K$ be the Galois closure of $L/K$. The **Galois Swan slopes** of $L/K$ is the multiset $S^{Gal}$ of Swan slopes of $L^{Gal}/K$ and let $S$ be the multiset of Swan slopes of $L/K$. The multiset $H$ of **hidden Swan slopes** of $L/K$ is obtained by subtracting $S$ from $S^{Gal}$. Thus $S^{Gal}$ is the multiset sum of $S$ and $H$. The **Galois Artin slopes** and **hidden Artin slopes** are defined analogously. In particular, they can be obtained from the corresponding Swan slopes by adding $1$ to each.

title: Indices of inseparability
snippet:
Let $L/K$ be a finite extension of $p$-adic fields and let $K'/K$ be the maximum unramified subextension of $L/K$. Set $n=[L:K']$ and write $n=up^{
u}$ with $p
mid u$. Let $\pi_L$ be a uniformizer for $L$ and let $f(X)=X^n+c_{n-1}X^{n-1}\cdots+c_1X+c_0$ be the minimal polynomial of $\pi_L$ over $K'$. For $k\in\Z$ define $\overline{v}_p(k)=\min\{v_p(k),
u\}$. For $0\le j\le
u$ set \[i_j^{\pi_L}=\min\{nv_{K'}(c_h)+h-n: 0\le h\le n-1,\;\overline{v}_p(h)\le j\},\] or let $i_j^{\pi_L}=\infty$ if the set above is empty.

title: Inertia group
snippet: Let - $K$ be a $p$-adic field. - $L$ a finite Galois extension of $K$. - $\mathcal{O}_K$, $\mathcal{O}_L$ the rings of integers for $K$, $L$, - $P_K$, $P_L$ the unique maximal ideals of $\mathcal{O}_K$, $\mathcal{O}_L$, and - $\kappa=\mathcal{O}_K/P_K$, $\lambda=\mathcal{O}_L/P_L$ the residue fields of $K$, $L$. Then each $\sigma\in \Gal(L/K)$ induces a element of $\Gal(\lambda/\kappa)$. The kernel of the resulting homomorphism \[ \Gal(L/K) \to \Gal(\lambda/\kappa)\] is the **inertia group** of $L/K$.

title: Inertia group searching
snippet: When searching by inertia subgroups, it can be specified as an abstract group by either a GAP id or a group name from transitive group label. It can also be specified by as a transitive group label. In this case, it will only match $p$-adic fields where the defining polynomial is irreducible over the unramified extension of $\Q_p$ of degree unramified degree and the Galois group of the polynomial over that extension matches the specified transitive group.

title: Intermediate fields
snippet: For a local field $K$ which is a finite extension of $\Q_p$ for some prime $p$, **intermediate fields** $F$ are fields with $\Q_p\subsetneqq F \subsetneqq K$.

title: Local field invariants
snippet: The basic invariants of a $p$-adic field $K$ are: - the residue field characteristic $p$, - the degree $d$, - the ramification index $e$, - the residue field degree $f$. - the discriminant exponent $c$, - the discriminant root field $\mathbb Q_p(\sqrt{\textrm{disc}(K)})$, - the root number $\varepsilon$, - the automorphism group $\textrm{Aut}(K/\mathbb Q_p)$.

title: Jump set
snippet: Let $K$ be a $p$-adic field which contains a primitive $p$th root of unity $\zeta_p$. Set $e=v_K(p)$ and $r=v_p(e)$. The **jump set** associated to $K$ is an increasing sequence of $r+1$ positive integers which determines the structure of the 1-units of $K$ as a filtered $\mathbb{Z}_p$-module. If $K$ does not contain a primitive $p$th root of unity then the jump set of $K$ is not defined.

title: Local field
snippet: A **local field** is a field $K$ with a non-trivial absolute value $|\ |$ that is locally compact in the topology induced by the distance metric $d(x,y):=|x-y|$. An **archimedean local field** is a local field whose absolute value is archimedean; such a field is isomorphic to $\R$ or $\C$. A nonarchimedean local field is a local field whose absolute value is nonarchimedean. Such a field is either isomorphic to a finite extension of $\Q_p$ when $K$ has characteristic zero (in which case it is a $p$-adic field), or to a finite extension of $\F_p((t))$ when $K$ has characteristic $p$.

title: Local height function
snippet: Let $G$ be a finite group and $w$ a weight function on $G$. Let $p$ be a prime with $p \nmid \#G$ and $\Gamma_{\Q_p}$ the absolute Galois group of $\Q_p$. Let $\varphi_p: \Gamma_{\Q_p} \to G$ be a continuous homomorphism and $K_{\varphi_p} = \overline{\Q}_p^{\ker \varphi_p}$ the associated finite extension of $\Q_p$. As $p \nmid \#G$ the extension $\Q_p \subset K_{\varphi_p}$ is tamely ramified, thus its inertia group is cyclic \cite{href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/09EE}{tag 09EE}}. Let $\tau_p \in \Gal(K_{\varphi_p}/\Q_p)$ be a choice of generator of the inertia group.

title: $p$-adic logarithm
snippet: Let $K$ be a finite extension of $\Q_p$ for some prime $p$, and $$ S=\{z \in K : |z|_p < 1\}$$ where $|\cdot|_p$ is an absolute value on $K$ normalized so that $|p|_p=1/p$. Then the **$p$-adic logarithm** $ \log_p:S\to K$ is given by $$ \log_p(1+z) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^n \frac{z^n}{n}.$$ One can extend $\log_p$ to $K$ by defining $\log_p(p)=0$ and $\log_p(\zeta)=0$ where $\zeta$ is any root of unity.

title: Maximal ideal of a local field
snippet: The maximal ideal of a nonarchimedean local field $K$ is the unique maximal ideal of its ring of integers $\mathcal O_K$. It consists of all elements of $\mathcal O_K$ that are not units, equivalently, all elements of $K$ whose absolute value is strictly less than 1.

title: Means of an extension
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields. The **mean** $m$ of $L/K$ is determined by the formula $c=f(e−1+em)$, where $c$ is the discriminant exponent, $f$ is the residue field degree, and $e$ is the ramification index of $L/K$. The mean of $L/K$ is related to the height $H(L/K)$ by the formula $H(L/K)=em$. Let $K_f/K$ be the maximal unramified subextension of $L/K$. For each subextension $L'/K_f$ of $L/K_f$ set $e'=[L':K_f]$ and let $m'$ be the mean of $L'/K_f$. We associate to $L'$ the point $(e',e'm')$ in the Cartesian plane.

title: Newton polygon of a polynomial
snippet: Let $K$ be a nonarchimedean local field with discrete valuation $v_K$, and $f \in K[x]$, given by $$ f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_nx^n,$$ and such that $a_0a_n \neq 0$. Then the **Newton polygon** of $f$ is the lower convex hull of the set of points $(i,v_K(a_i))$, if we ignore all points with $a_i=0$. In the context of isogeny classes of abelian varieties over finite fields, the constant term $a_0=1$ always, and therefore the Newton polygon begins at $(0,0)$.

title: Slopes of the Newton polygon of a polynomial
snippet: Given a polynomial $f$ defined over a nonarchimedean local field $K$, the **slopes** of its Newton polygon are a list of increasing numbers $[s_1,s_2,s_3, \ldots]$ such that the Newton polygon of $f$ is given by a line segment of width 1 with slope $s_1$, followed by a line segment of width 1 with slope $s_2$, etc. Note that it is possible for a slope to appear multiple times if the Newton polygon has the same slope for a width of more than 1.

title: Packets in a family
snippet: Let $I/K$ be a family of extensions of $p$-adic fields. Say the extensions $L_1/K,L_2/K\in I/K$ are in the same **packet** if their residual polynomials are equivalent and they have the same hidden invariants. Specifically, let $L_1^{Gal}/K$, $L_2^{Gal}/K$ be the Galois closures of $L_1/K$, $L_2/K$. Then $L_1/K$, $L_2/K$ are in the same packet if - $\Gal(L_1^{Gal}/K)$ and $\Gal(L_2^{Gal}/K)$ are isomorphic as permutation groups, - $L_1^{Gal}/K$ and $L_2^{Gal}/K$ have the same residue field degree, - $L_1^{Gal}/K$ and $L_2^{Gal}/K$ have the same ramification index, and - $L_1/K$ and $L_2/K$…

title: $p$-adic field
snippet: A **$p$-adic field** (or **local number field**) is a finite extension of $\Q_p$, equivalently, a nonarchimedean local field of characteristic zero.

title: Definition of $\Q_p$
snippet: If $p$ is a prime number, then every rational number $r$ can be written in the form $r=p^j \frac{a}{b}$ where $\gcd(p, ab)=1$. We define $|r|_p = p^{-j}$, and define a metric on $\Q$ by $d(u,v) = |u-v|_p$; in other words, $u$ and $v$ are close to each other if they are congruent modulo a larger power of $p$. The completion of $\Q$ with respect to this metric is the field of p-adic numbers, denoted as $\Q_p$.

title: Ramification index of an extension of $p$-adic fields
snippet: Let $L/K$ be a finite extension of $p$-adic fields. Then $\mathcal{O}_L$ and $\mathcal{O}_K$, the rings of integers of $L$ and $K$, are discrete valuation domains, so they have unique maximal ideals $P_L$ and $P_K$ which are principal. If $P_K=(\pi_K)$, the element $\pi_K$ is a uniformizer for $K$. We have $\pi_K\mathcal{O}_L=P_L^e$ for some positive integer $e$, which factors as $e = p^w e_{\mathrm{tame}}$ with $e_{\mathrm{tame}}$ coprime to $p$.

title: Ramification polygon
snippet: Let $L/K$ be a finite extension of $p$-adic fields and let $K'/K$ be the maximal unramified subextension of $L/K$. Then $L/K'$ is totally ramified, and if $\alpha \in L$ is a uniformizer then the minimal polynomial $\varphi(x) \in K'[x]$ of $\alpha$ over $K'$ will be Eisenstein. The **ramification polygon** $P$ of $L$ is essentially the Newton polygon of the **ramification polynomial** $$\rho(x)=\alpha^{-n}\varphi(\alpha x + \alpha)\in L[x]$$ of $L/K$. Write $\rho(x)=x^n+\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\rho_ix^i$.

title: Ramification polygon diagram
snippet: The blue graph shows the ramification polygon. * The blue number to the right of each segment is its slope. * Coordinates are given for the black points at $(-p^j, i_j)$ where $[i_0,...,i_\nu]$ are the indices of inseparability. These points include the vertices of the ramification polygon. In some cases there are additional points on or above the polygon. * Each residual polynomial $A(z)$ of $L/K$ is associated to a segment $S$ of the ramification polygon; if $S$ has positive slope then the coefficients of the polynomial come from the black points which lie on the segment.

title: Rams
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields with ramification index $e = \epsilon p^w$. The **rams** $r_1 \le \dots \le r_w$ of $L/K$ are the same as the positive lower ramification jumps of $L/K$. In the case where $L/K$ is a Galois extension the lower jumps are defined in Chapter IV of Serre's Local Fields \cite{doi:10.1007/978-1-4757-5673-9,MR:0554237}. For the general case where $L/K$ is separable but not necessarily Galois see the appendix to Deligne \cite{MR:0771673}.

title: Residual polynomials
snippet: Let $L/K$ be a totally ramified extension of $p$-adic fields, let $\alpha$ be a uniformizer of $L$ with minimal polynomial $\varphi$ over $K$ and denote by $v_\alpha$ the (exponential) valuation that is normalized such that $v_\alpha(\alpha)=1$. Let $\underline{L}$ be the residue field of $L$, and for $\delta \in \mathcal{O}_L$ write $\underline{\delta}$ for the image of $\delta$ in $\underline{L}$. Let $$\rho(x)=\sum_i \rho_i x^i\in\mathcal{O}_L[x]$$ be the ramification polynomial of $L/K$.

title: Residue field
snippet: The residue field of a nonarchimedean local field is the quotient of its ring of integers by its unique maximal ideal. The residue field is finite and its characteristic $p$ is the **residue field characteristic**. Finite extensions of $\Q_p$ have residue field characteristic $p$.

title: Residue field degree of a local field
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields. Let $\kappa$ be the residue field of $K$ and let $\lambda$ be the residue field of $L$. The **residue field degree** $f$ of $L/K$ is the degree of the field extension $\lambda/\kappa$. The **base residue field degree** $f_0$ of $L/K$ is the residue field degree of $K/\Q_p$. The **absolute residue field degree** $f_{\mathrm{abs}}$ of $L/K$ is the residue field degree of $L$ over $\Q_p$.

title: Ring of integers of a local field
snippet: The ring of integers of a local field $K$ with absolute value $|\ |$ is the subring $\mathcal O_K := \{x\in K:|x|\le 1\}$; it is a discrete valuation ring.

title: Root number of a local field
snippet: For a finite extension $K/\mathbb{Q}_p$, we give the **local root number**, or local sign, $\epsilon(K) \in \{1,i,-1,-i\}$. We start with a general description where we allow $p=\infty$, writing $\Q_\infty = \R$. Local root numbers, $\epsilon(\rho) \in \C^\times$, are defined for representations $\rho : \mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\Q}_p/\Q_p)\rightarrow \GL_n(\C)$. They have absolute value $1$, are multiplicative in the sense that $\epsilon(\rho_1 \oplus \rho_2) = \epsilon(\rho_1) \epsilon(\rho_2)$, and for $p \neq \infty$, any unramified representation has root number $1$.

title: Roots of unity
snippet: This is the order of the group of roots of unity of $K$, written in the form $(p^f-1)\cdot p^r$, where $f$ is the residue field degree of $K/\Q_p$.

title: Find a p-adic field
snippet: Enter the label of a $p$-adic field.

title: Slope content of an extension of $p$-adic fields
snippet: Let $L/K$ be an extension of $p$-adic fields. Let $G$ be the Galois group of the Galois closure $L^{gal}/K$ of $L/K$. Then $G$ has a filtration by higher ramification groups, which in turn are connected to the discriminants of subfields of $L^{gal}$.
