Each prime ideal in $\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_F$ is drawn as a point, and the lines connecting the points are purely for aesthetical purposes, having no deeper meaning.

title: Place of a number field
snippet: A **place** $v$ of a field $K$ is an equivalence class of non-trivial absolute values on $K$. As with absolute values, places may be classified as archimedean or nonarchimedean, since these properties are preserved under equivalence. Each place induces a distance metric that gives $K$ a metric topology. The **completion** $K_v$ of $K$ at $v$ is the completion of this metric space, which is also a topological field.

title: Canonical defining polynomial for number fields
snippet: Every number field $K$ can be represented as $K = \Q[X]/P(x)$ for some monic $P\in\Z[X]$, called a **defining polynomial** for $K$. Among all such defining polynomials, we define the **reduced defining polynomial** as follows. Recall that for a monic polynomial $P(x) = \prod_i(x-\alpha_i)$, the $T_2$ norm of $P$ is $T_2(P) = \sum_i |\alpha_i|^2$. - Let $L_0$ be the list of (monic integral) defining polynomials for $K$ that are minimal with respect to the $T_2$ norm. - Let $L_1$ be the sublist of $L_0$ of polynomials whose discriminant has minimal absolute value.

title: Discriminant of polynomial
snippet: The **discriminant** of a monic polynomial $f(x) = \prod_{i=1}^d (x - \alpha_i)$ is the quantity \[ \Delta = \prod_{i < j} (\alpha_i - \alpha_j)^2. \] If $f$ has integral coefficients, $K$ is the number field defined by $f$ and $\alpha$ is a root of $f$ in $K$, then the discriminant $D$ of $K$ divides $\Delta$ and the ratio $\Delta/D$ is the square of the index of $\Z[\alpha]$ in the ring of integers of $K$.

title: Prime of a number field
snippet: A **prime** $\mathfrak p$ of a number field $K$ is a nonzero prime ideal of its ring of integers $\mathcal O_K$. The ideal $\mathfrak p \cap\mathcal O_K$ is a nonzero prime ideal of $\Z$ (a prime of $\Q$), which is necessarily a principal ideal $(p)$ for some prime number $p$. The prime $\mathfrak p$ is then said to be a **prime above** $p$.

title: Primitive element
snippet: A primitive element for a number field $K$ is a field generator of $K$. In other words, it is an element $\alpha \in K$ such that the inclusion $\Q(\alpha) \subseteq K$ is an equality. The _primitive element theorem_ asserts that every number field admits a primitive element.

title: Radical discriminant of a number field
snippet: The radical discriminant of a number field $K$ is the product of the primes dividing the discriminant of $K$, i.e. the product of the primes which ramify in $K$.

title: Ramified (rational) prime of a number field
snippet: A prime integer $p$ is a **ramified prime** of a number field $K$ if, when the ideal generated by $p$ is factored into prime ideals in the ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$ of $K$, $$p\mathcal{O}_K= \mathcal{P_1}^{e_1}\cdots \mathcal{P_k}^{e_k},$$ there is an $i$ such that $e_i\geq 2$. The ramified primes of $K$ are the primes dividing the discriminant of $K$.

title: Rank of a number field
snippet: The **rank** of a number field $K$ is the size of any set of fundamental units of $K$. It is equal to $r = r_1 + r_2 -1$ where $r_1$ is the number of real embeddings of $K$ into $\C$ and $2r_2$ is the number of complex embeddings of $K$ into $\C.$

title: Real embedding
snippet: A **real embedding** of a number field $K$ is a field homomorphism $K\to \R$. A single number field may have several distinct real embeddings.

title: Reflex field
snippet: Let $K$ be a nf.cm_field and let $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ be the algebraic closure of $\Q$ in $\C$. A subset $\Phi \subset \mathrm{Hom}(K, \overline{\mathbb{Q}})$ is called a **CM type** if for every embedding $\iota \in \mathrm{Hom}(K, \overline{\mathbb{Q}})$ either $\iota \in \Phi$ or $\overline{\iota} \in \Phi$, but not both, where $\overline{\iota}$ is the complex conjugate of $\iota$.

title: Reflex field of the reflex field
snippet: Let $K$ be a nf.cm_field and let $N$ a normal closure of $K$, let $\Phi \subset \mathrm{Hom}(K, \overline{\mathbb{Q}})$ be a CM type and $L$ its associated reflex field. Then $\Phi$ induces a CM type $\Phi_N \subset \mathrm{Hom}(N, \mathbb{C})$ by taking the maps that restrict to a map inside $\Phi$ on $K$. The maps in $\Phi_N$ are isomorphisms on the image $F$ of $N$ inside $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ and by inverting them, we obtain a CM type on $F$ with values in $N$. The **reflex field of the reflex field** is the reflex field of this CM type. It can also be computed as follows.

title: Regulator of a number field
snippet: Let $\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_{r_1}$ be the real embeddings of a number field $K$ into the complex numbers $\mathbb{C}$, and $\sigma_{r_1+ 1},\ldots,\sigma_{r_1+r_2}$ be complex embeddings of $K$ into $\C$ such that no two are complex conjugate. Let $u_1,\ldots,u_r$ be a set of fundamental units of $K$. Then $r = r_1 + r_2 -1$. Let $M$ be the $(r_1+ r_2-1)\times (r_1+r_2)$ matrix $(d_j\log{ |\sigma_j(u_i)|})$, where $d_j=1$ if $j\leq r_1$, i.e, if $\sigma_j$ is a real embedding, and $d_j=2$ otherwise, i.e., if $\sigma_j$ is a complex embedding. The sum of the columns of $M$ is the zero vector.

title: Relative class number of a CM field
snippet: If $K$ is a number field with CM with class number $h$, and $K^+$ is its maximal totally real subfield with class number $h^+$, then $h^+$ divides $h$ and the **relative class number** is $h/h^+$.

title: Ring of integers of a number field
snippet: The ring of integers of a number field $K$ is the integral closure of $\Z$ in $K$.

title: Root discriminant of a number field
snippet: If $K$ is a number field of degree $n$ and discriminant $D$, then the **root discriminant** of $K$ is \[ \textrm{rd}(K) = |D|^{1/n}.\] It gives a measure of the discriminant of a number field which is normalized for the degree. For example, if $K\subseteq L$ are number fields and $L/K$ is unramified, then $\textrm{rd}(K)=\textrm{rd}(L)$.

title: Find a number field
snippet: You can search for a specific number field by entering its label, its name, or its defining polynomial. You can also enter any comma-separated list of labels, names, or polynomials, to return a corresponding search results page.

title: Separable extension
snippet: If $K/F$ is a finite degree field extension, $\alpha\in K$ is **separable** over $F$ if its monic irreducible polynomial has distinct roots in the algebraic closure $\overline{F}$. The extension $K/F$ is **separable** if every $\alpha\in K$ is separable over $F$. All algebraic extensions of local and global number fields are separable.

title: Separable algebra
snippet: A (finite) **separable algebra** $A$ over a field $F$, also called an **\'etale $F$-algebra**, is an $F$-algebra of finite dimension that is isomorphic to a product of separable field extensions of $F$. If $L/K$ is a field extension and $A$ is a separable $K$-algebra then $A\otimes_K L$ is a separable $L$-algebra (which is typically not a field, even when $A$ is).

title: Serre Odlyzko bound
snippet: For each positive integer $n$, let $C_n$ for the minimum root discriminant for all number fields of degree $n$. Assuming the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis, $\limsup C_n \geq \Omega$ where $$ \Omega = 8\pi e^\gamma\approx 44.7632\ldots$$ and $\gamma$ is the Euler–Mascheroni constant. Lower bounds for the $C_n$ were deduced by analytic methods through the work of Odlyzko and others.

title: Twin sextic algebra
snippet: If $A$ is a separable algebra of degree 6 over a field $F$ (so $\dim_F A=6$), then it has a _twin algebra_ $B$ that is also a separable algebra of degree 6 over $F$; the $F$-algebras $A$ and $B$ are related by the non-trivial outer automorphism of $S_6$. The symmetric group $S_n$ has a non-trivial outer automorphism if and only if $n=6$, in which case it is unique up to inner automorphisms. This automorphism interchanges $S_5\leq S_6$ with $\PGL_2(5)\leq S_6$. In terms of Galois theory, this inclusion gives an absolute resolvent of degree $[G:\PGL_2(5)]=6$.

title: Sibling fields and algebras
snippet: Two finite separable extension fields $K_1$ and $K_2$ of a ground field $F$ are called **siblings** if they are not isomorphic, but have isomorphic Galois closures. A finite dimensional separable $\Q$-algebra is isomorphic to a product of number fields. By its Galois closure, we mean the compositum of the Galois closures of the constituent fields. Then two algebras are **siblings** if they have isomorphic Galois closures, but are not isomorphic as $\Q$-algebras.

title: Signature of a number field
snippet: The **signature** of a number field $K$ is the pair $[r_1,r_2]$ where $r_1$ is the number of real embeddings of $K$ and $r_2$ is the number of conjugate pairs of complex embeddings. The degree of $K$ is $r_1+2r_2$. {# original author: Cremona #}

title: Prime spectrum of ring of integers in a number field
snippet: The *(prime) spectrum* of a ring $R$, $\operatorname{Spec} R$, is the set of prime ideals in $R$. In particular, if $F$ is a number field with ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_F$, the spectrum $\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_F$ can be thought of as a curve over $\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}$.

title: Stem field for a Galois extension
snippet: If $K/F$ is a Galois extension of fields, a **stem field** for $K/F$ is a field $E$ such that $F\subseteq E\subseteq K$ and $K$ is the Galois closure of $E/F$. This is connected to the notion of the stem field of a polynomial. If $f\in F[x]$ is a separable irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ with roots $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n$ (in some extension field), then the fields $F(\alpha_i)$ are the **stem fields of the polynomial** $f$.

title: Unit group torsion
snippet: A **torsion generator** of a number field is a primitive root of unity that generates the torsion subgroup of the unit group (which is necessarily cyclic).

title: Totally imaginary
snippet: A number field $K$ is **totally imaginary** (or **totally complex**) if it cannot be embedded in the real numbers $\R$; equivalently, $\R$ does not contain the image of any of the homomorphisms from $K$ to $\C$.

title: Totally positive
snippet: An element $\alpha$ in a number field $K$ is **totally positive** if $\sigma(\alpha)>0$ for all real embeddings $\sigma$ of $K$ into $\R$.

title: Totally real
snippet: A global number field $K$ is always of the form $K=\Q(\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ has monic irreducible polynomial $f(x)\in\Q[x]$. The field is **totally real** if all of the roots of $f(x)$ in $\C$ lie in the real numbers $\R$. Equivalently, $K$ is totally real if all the embeddings of $K$ into $\C$ have image contained in $\R$.

title: Unit group of a number field
snippet: The **unit group** of a number field $K$ is the group of units of the ring of integers of $K$. It is a finitely generated abelian group with cyclic torsion subgroup. A set of generators of a maximal torsion-free subgroup is called a set of fundamental units for $K$. The unit group of $K$ has as invariants the rank and the regulator of $K$.

title: Unit signature rank of a number field
snippet: Let $K$ be a number field with at least one real embedding, and let $\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_{r_1} : K \to \R$ be the $r_1$ real embeddings of $K$. The **signature** of a nonzero element $\alpha \in K$ is the $r_1$-tuple of signs of $\sigma(\alpha)$ over all real embeddings $\sigma$ of $K$, viewed as lying in the additive group $\F_2$. I.e. the signature is the vector $(\text{sign}(\sigma_1(\alpha)), \dots, \text{sign}(\sigma_{r_1}(\alpha))) \in \F_2^{r_1}$, where $\text{sign}(x) = 0$ if $x$ is positive, otherwise $\text{sign}(x) = 1$ if $x$ is negative.

title: Unramified (rational) prime of a number field
snippet: A **unramified (rational) prime** of a number field $K$ is a prime integer $p$ such that the ideal generated by $p$ is factored into distinct prime ideals in the ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$ of $K$ $$p\mathcal{O}_K = \mathcal{P}_1\cdots \mathcal{P}_k.$$ The unramified primes of $K$ are the primes which do not divide the discriminant of $K$.

title: Two notions of equivalence for valuations
snippet: Let $v_1$ and $v_2$ are valuations of a ring $R$, $p \in \mathbb{R}_{>1}$, and we set $\lvert x \rvert_1 = p^{-v_1(x)}$ and $\lvert x \rvert_2 = p^{-v_2(x)}$, the following are equivalent: * there exists a positive real number $c$ such that $|x|_1 = |x|_2^c$ for all $x\in R$, * for all elements $r, s \in R$, $v_1(r) \le v_1(s)$ if and only if $v_2(r) \le v_2(s)$. The second form is the definition used in mathlib.

title: Weil height
snippet: The (**logarithmic**) **Weil height** of a nonzero rational number $a/b\in\mathbb{Q}$ in lowest terms is the quantity $$ h(a/b) = \log\max\bigl\{|a|,|b|\bigr\}. $$ The height of $0$ is taken to be $0.$ The (**absolute logarithmic**) **Weil height** of an element $\alpha$ in a number field $K$ is the quantity $$ h(\alpha) = \frac{1}{[K:\mathbb Q]} \sum_{v\in M_K} [K_v:\mathbb Q_v]\log\max\bigl\{\|\alpha\|_v,1\bigr\}, $$ where $M_K$ is an appropriately normalized set of inequivalent absolute values on $K$.

title: Weil polynomial
snippet: For a prime power $q$, a **Weil $q$-polynomial** is a monic polynomial with integer coefficients whose complex roots are of absolute value $\sqrt{q}$. Given $q$ and a nonnegative integer $d$, there are only finitely many Weil $q$-polynomials of degree $d$. The characteristic polynomial of an abelian variety over $\F_q$ is a Weil $q$-polynomial, but it is not quite true that every Weil $q$-polynomial arises in this way.

title: Index of a number field
snippet: If $K$ is a number field with ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$, then for all $\alpha\in\mathcal{O}_K$ such that $K=\Q(\alpha)$, the index of $\alpha$, $i(\alpha)$ is the index of the order $\Z[\alpha]$. The **index of the number field** is the greatest common divisor of all $i(\alpha)$ with $\alpha$ as above.

title: Paramodular group in degree g
snippet: A paramodular group $\Gamma$ of degree $g$ preserves an integral alternating $2g \times 2g$ matrix $T=\left( \begin{array}{ll} 0 & d \\ -d & 0 \end{array}\right)$ for $d=\mathrm{diag}(d_1,d_2,\dots,d_g)$ with $d_j\mid d_{j+1}$ for $1\leqslant j \leqslant g-1$: $ \Gamma=\left\{\gamma \in \GL(2g,\mathbb{Z})\, : \, \gamma^t T \gamma=T\right\}. $ For $\delta= \left( \begin{array}{ll} 1_g & 0 \\ 0 & d \end{array}\right)$, the conjugate $\Gamma^{\mathrm{para}}(d)=\delta\Gamma\delta^{-1}\subseteq \mathrm{Sp}(2g,\Q)$ is also referred to as a paramodular group.

title: Pictures for classical modular forms
snippet: For each newform or trace form, we use domain coloring to plot the associated trace form $\mathrm{Tr}(f)$. At each point $z$ in the unit disk $\mathcal{D}$, we assign a color based on the value of $\mathrm{Tr}(f)(\mu(z))$, where $\mu$ is the Möbius transform $\mu: \mathcal{D} \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}$ given by $\mu(z) = \frac{1 - iz}{z - i}$. Writing $\mathrm{Tr}(f)(\mu(z)) = r e^{i \theta}$, the color is determined entirely by the argument $\theta$. The color starts as a dull mustard yellow at $\theta = 0$ and becomes brighter and more yellow as $\theta$ increases towards $\pi$.

title: Pictures for elliptic curves over number fields
snippet: For each elliptic curve $E$ over a number field $K$, the plot displayed consists of the graphs of the real points of $E$ with respect to each real embedding of $K$. Complex embeddings of $K$ are ignored, so for example there is no plot shown when $K$ is a totally complex field such as an imaginary quadratic field. The plots are produced using SageMath, which uses plotting logic designed by David Roe to determine which subset of $E(\mathbb{R})$ in the real affine plane to include in the plot.

title: Pictures for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$
snippet: For each elliptic curve $E$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, the plot displayed is of part of $E(\mathbb{R})$, the set of real points of $E$. The plots are produced using SageMath, which uses plotting logic designed by David Roe to determine which subset of $E(\mathbb{R})$ in the real affine plane to include in the plot.

title: Explaination of pictures of Galois groups
snippet: If $G$ is a transitive subgroup of $S_n$, then we draw $n$ dots to represent the numbers from $1$ to $n$. For each generator of the group, we draw an arrow from the $i$th dot to the $j$th dot if the permutation sends $i$ to $j$. There are no arrows drawn for fixed points, and a transposition is represented by a single double-headed arrow. One color is assigned to each generator for its arrows. For groups where the diagram has not yet been created, we display a picture of Évariste Galois. ## Implementation ## Diagrams were created by John Jones using the package graphviz.

title: Explanation of pictures for abstract groups
snippet: Pictures for abstract groups consist of several disks arranged in concentric circles. The size, position, and brightness each encode information about the group. ## Quick summary ## - Each disk corresponds to a conjugacy class. - Conjugacy classes with more elements are larger than those with fewer elements. - Disks are arranged according to the number of prime divisors of the order of elements in the conjugacy class. The identity is in the center. - Darker disks of the same hue typically have orders that are more divisible by $2$ than brighter disks with the same hue.

title: Pictures for Maass forms
snippet: For each Maass newform $f$, we use domain coloring to show the plot of $f$. The form $f$ is approximated using the first $50$ coefficients across a $200 \times 200$ mesh. At each point $z$ in the unit disk $\mathcal{D}$, we assign a color based on the value of $f(\mu(z))$, where $\mu$ is the Möbius transform $\mu: \mathcal{D} \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}$ given by $\mu(z) = \frac{1 - iz}{z - i}$.

title: Pictures of modular curves
snippet: For each modular curve $X_H$ of level $N$, we visualize the modular curve through the action on the $\mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{Z})$ fundamental domain $\mathcal{F}$ by $X_H \cap \mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{Z} / N\mathbb{Z})$. In order to better see distinct translations of $\mathcal{F}$, we color the fundamental domain with two separate colors and preserve these colors through translation. Two modular curves having the same intersection with $\mathrm{SL}(2, \mathbb{Z})$ will have the same picture.

title: Source, reliability, and completeness
snippet: The LMFDB is divided into sections that are listed on the left side of your screen, including L-functions, modular forms, varieties, fields, representations, and groups. Each section is further divided into subsections that contain information related to a specific type of mathematical object, such as elliptic curves over $\Q$ or number fields. Within each subsection you will find a "Learn more" panel that appears on the right of your screen.

title: Completeness and extent of Artin representation data
snippet: Data for Artin representations are complete in the following cases based on \cite{MR:3669395, doi:10.1007/s40993-017-0079-5, arXiv:1610.01228}. In each case, the LMFDB contains all faithful representations for a given group and irreducible representation with conductor less than or equal to the stated bound. Representations are distinguished by their dimension, and the smallest containing permutation representation. In addition, the LMFDB contains data on Artin representations for a variety of other number fields and Galois groups.

title: Completeness of data on isogeny classes of abelian varieties over finite fields
snippet: For each dimension $g$ and field $\mathbb{F}_q$ with a nonempty collection of isogeny classes in the database, the collection of isogeny classes is complete. As of October 2020, the data was complete for the following pairs $(g, q)$, where $q$ ranges over all primes and powers of a prime in the interval: - for $g = 1$, data is available for all $2 \leq q \leq 499$ and $q \in \{512, 625, 729, 1024\}$, - for $g = 2$, data is available for all $2 \leq q \leq 211$ and $q \in \{243, 256, 343, 512, 625, 729, 1024\}$, - for $g = 3$, data is available for all $2 \leq q \leq 25$, - for $g = 4$, data…

title: Completeness of data for Belyi maps
snippet: As described in \cite{arXiv:1805.07751}, the Belyi database contains maps up to degree $9$ and it is complete up to (and including) degree $6$.

title: Completeness of Dirichlet character data
snippet: The database contains information (basic properties, minimality, Galois orbit, field of values) for all Dirichlet characters of modulus up to $10^6$. Basic properties are available for all Dirichlet characters of modulus up to $10^{20}$, but this information is not stored in the database and is not searchable. L-functions are available for characters of modulus up to 2800 (and some of higher modulus).

title: Completeness of Classical Modular Form Data
snippet: The classical modular form database currently contains information for all newforms $f\in S_k^\mathrm{new}(N,\chi)$ of level $N\ge 1$, weight $k\ge 1$, character $\chi\colon (\Z/N\Z)^\times\to \C$, for which any of the following hold: * $Nk^2\le 4000$; * $\chi$ trivial and $Nk^2\le 40\,000$; * $N \le 24$ and $Nk^2 \le 40\,000$; * $N \le 10$ and $Nk^2 \le 100\,000$; * $N \le 100$ and $k \le 12$; * $k > 1$ and $\dim S_k^{\rm new}(N,\chi) \le 100$ and $Nk^2 \le 40\,000$. * $k = 2$, $\chi$ is trivial, and $N\le 50\,000$ or $N \le 10^6$ is prime.

title: Completeness of classical modular form data
snippet: The classical modular form database currently contains information for all newforms $f\in S_k^\mathrm{new}(N,\chi)$ of level $N\ge 1$, weight $k\ge 1$, character $\chi\colon (\Z/N\Z)^\times\to \C$, for which any of the following hold: * $Nk^2\le 4000$; * $\chi$ trivial and $Nk^2\le 40\,000$; * $N \le 24$ and $Nk^2 \le 40\,000$; * $N \le 10$ and $Nk^2 \le 100\,000$; * $N \le 100$ and $k \le 12$; * $k > 1$ and $\dim S_k^{\rm new}(N,\chi) \le 100$ and $Nk^2 \le 40\,000$. * $k = 2$, $\chi$ is trivial, and $N\le 50\,000$ or $N \le 10^6$ is prime.

title: Completeness of data for families of higher genus curves with automorphisms
snippet: Currently the database contains all groups $G$ acting as automorphisms of curves $X$ from genus $2$ up to genus $15$ such that the quotient $X/G$ has genus $0$, as well as genus $2$ through $4$ with quotient genus greater than $0$.

title: Completeness of elliptic curve data
snippet: This information can only be displayed with a more recent version of the LMFDB. ### Imaginary quadratic fields Over each imaginary quadratic number field of absolute discriminant less than \(700\), the database contains elliptic curves of conductor norm up to a bound that depends on the field, corresponding to the level norm bound for Bianchi modular forms over that field.

title: Completeness of elliptic curve data over $\Q$
snippet: The database of elliptic curves over $\Q$ consists of the following sets of curves: - all curves of conductor less than $500{,}000$; - all curves whose conductor is $7$-smooth (that is, only divisible by primes $p\le 7$); - all curves of prime conductor $p \le 300{,}000{,}000$.

title: Completeness of genus 2 curve data
snippet: The database contains all genus 2 curves over $\Q$ that can defined by an integral equation of the form \[ y^2+(h_3x^3+h_2x^2+h_1x+h_0)y=f_6x^6+f_5x^5+f_4x^4+f_3x^3+f_2x^2+f_1x+f_0, \] with $h_i\in\{0,1\}$ and $|f_i|\le 90$ and absolute discriminant $|\Delta|\le 10^6$. As explained in \cite{MR:3540958,arXiv:1602.03715}, it also includes all genus 2 curves of absolute discriminant $|\Delta|\le 10^6$ defined by an integral equation as above such that at least one of the following holds: - $h_i\in \{0,1\}$ and $|f_i|\le 2 (3.51)^{6-i}$ for $0\le i\le 6$; - $h_i\in\{0,1\}$ and $|f_i|\le…

title: Completeness and extent of Galois group data
snippet: There is data for every transitive subgroup of $S_n$, up to conjugation, for $n\leq 47$ and $n\neq 32$. For transitive subgroups of $S_{32}$, there is data for all groups $G$ where either $|G|<512$ or $|G|> 40{,}000{,}000{,}000$, plus some other groups of intermediate sizes when they appear elsewhere in the LMFDB. For most groups, sibling and resolvent information is given for subgroups of index $\leq 47$. In some cases, this information was only computed with smaller bounds, and the individual group pages so indicate.

title: Completeness and extent of abstract group data
snippet: The database of finite groups consists of groups, subgroups, characters and conjugacy classes. The set of groups included contains: * All groups of order up to 2000, except those whose order is larger than 500 and divisible by 128, * All groups with a faithful transitive permutation representation of degree up to 47, except those of degree 32 and order between 512 and $4 \times 10^{10}$, * All groups with a faithful permutation representation of degree up to 15, * All groups with a rational matrix representation of dimension up to 6, * Finite groups of Lie type up to certain bounds, *…

title: Completeness of data on hypergeometric motives
snippet: The database contains all families of hypergeometric motives over $\Q$ with degree $\leq 7$. There is no claim made of completeness for individual motives.

title: Completeness of Hilbert modular surface data
snippet: The database contains the Hilbert modular surfaces $X_0(\mathfrak{N})_\mathfrak{b}$ and $X_0^1(\mathfrak{N})_\mathfrak{b}$ for the - real quadratic fields with discriminant $d \leq 3000$, - levels $\mathfrak{N}$ bounded by $\text{Nm}(\mathfrak{N}) \leq 5000/d^{3/2}$, - and all representatives $\mathfrak{b}$ for the narrow class group. For each Hilbert modular surface, we compute the cusps, elliptic points, topological Euler number, holomorphic Euler characteristic, Hodge diamond, self-intersection $K^2$, and Kodaira dimension.

title: Completeness of data for Integral Lattices
snippet:
The density and Hermite number of an integral lattice are computed in Magma using floating-point approximation; digits displayed are guaranteed to be correct.

This information can only be displayed with a more recent version of the LMFDB.

title: Completeness and extent of local field data
snippet: The database contains all extension fields of $\Q_p$ of degree $n$ for $n < 24$ and $p<200$. It also contains all absolute families with degree $n < 48$ and $p < 200$, as well as all relative families with absolute degree $n_{\mathrm{absolute}} < 48$, base degree $n_0 < 16$ and $p < 200$.

title: Completeness of L-function Data
snippet: The database currently contains more than $20$ million L-functions. Most of these are stored in the database along with related information such as zeros and analytic rank that have been precomputed using explicit precision bounds (see the ``reliability'' link on each L-function's home page for further details). A small number of dynamically computed L-functions are also available, as noted in the summary below.

title: Completeness and extent of Maass form data
snippet: The database currently contains data for Maass newforms on $\Gamma_0(N)$ of weight 0 with trivial character and Laplace eigenvalue $\lambda = \frac14 + R^2$ that lie primarily in the ranges: - $1\le N \le 10$ and $0<R\le 10$; - prime $100 < N < 250$ and $0<R\le 2$; - prime $250 < N < 1000$ and $0<R\le 1$. The database is not necessarily complete in these ranges, but it contains most of the Maass newforms with these parameters as well as examples with larger values of $R$ for small $N$.

title: Completeness and extent of rigorous Maass form data
snippet: The database currently contains data for cuspidal Maass newforms on $\Gamma_0(N)$ of weight 0 with trivial character and Laplace eigenvalue $\lambda = \frac14 + R^2$ with level $N$ in the range $1 \leq N \leq 105$, and where $N$ is squarefree. For each of these levels, the database contains consecutive Maass forms and is complete in the given ranges. Each database entry corresponds to exactly one Maass form. As each piece of data associated to each of these Maass forms is conjecturally transcendental, it's not possible to give an *exact* Maass form.

title: Completeness of Bianchi modular form data
snippet: The database contains information about Bianchi modular forms over all imaginary quadratic number fields of absolute discrimant up to \(2500\), for a range of levels which depends on the field. ### Newform data Bianchi Newform data is available for levels of the form $\Gamma_0(\mathfrak{n})\le \GL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$ and not for the larger spaces forms of level $\Gamma_0(\mathfrak{n})\cap \SL_2(\mathcal{O}_K)$.

title: Completeness of Hilbert modular form data
snippet: The database contains 368,356 Hilbert newforms over 400 totally real number fields of degree up to 6. For each field for which forms are presented, the data is complete up to a given level norm. These upper bounds are given on the browse pages; see the browse pages for degree [2](https://www.lmfdb.org/ModularForm/GL2/TotallyReal/browse/2/), [3](https://www.lmfdb.org/ModularForm/GL2/TotallyReal/browse/3/), [4](https://www.lmfdb.org/ModularForm/GL2/TotallyReal/browse/4/), [5](https://www.lmfdb.org/ModularForm/GL2/TotallyReal/browse/5/), and…

title: Completeness of Siegel modular form data
snippet: The Siegel modular form database currently contains information for all newforms $ f \in S_{k,j}^{\text{new}}(\Gamma_F(N)) $ of level $ N \ge 1 $, weight $(k,j) \ge (3,0)$ and trivial character, for which any of the following hold: * $\Gamma_F = K$ is the paramodular family, $ (k,j) = (3,0) $, $N < 1000$ and $N$ admits a prime divisor $p \parallel N$.

title: Completeness of the modular curve database
snippet: The database currently contains data on all modular curves $X_H$ with $\det(H)=\widehat\Z^\times$ such that at least one of the following hold: - $H$ has level at most $70$; - $H$ has prime-power level at most $335$; - $H$ contains $-I$ and has genus at most $24$ and level at most $335$; - $H$ does not contain $-I$ and has genus at most $8$ and level at most $335$. Information on rational cusps and rational CM points is available for every $X_H$. Information on the analytic rank, and the isogeny decomposition of the Jacobian is available for every $X_H$ of level $N\le 70$.

title: Completeness and extent of global number field data
snippet: The PARI database contains complete lists of fields with the absolute discriminant $|D|$ less than given bounds, and was compiled from the work of several authors. Fields from Voight extend these lists in cases where the number of complex places is \(0\) or \(1\). In other cases, the bounds have been extended by Jones and Roberts. The bounds for octics come from results of Diaz y Diaz and Battistoni and those for nonics are due to Battistoni.

title: Completeness of the Shimura curve database
snippet: The database currently contains data on the following Shimura curves: - $X(D;1)$ for $D\in\Z_{>0}$ squarefree and $D\leq 1000$, - all $X_H$ for $H \le$ $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$ $\ltimes (O/NO)^{\times}$ where $O$ is a maximal order of reduced discriminant $6$, $H$ coarse, and $N=2, 3, 4$, and $6$.

title: Completeness of Sato-Tate Group Data
snippet: The database currently contains all rational Sato-Tate groups of weight 1 and degree up to 6, as well as the two rational Sato-Tate groups of weight 0 and degree 1. Pages for irrational Sato-Tate groups of weight 0 and degree 1 are available for component groups of order up to $10^{20}$, but only those order up to $10^{4}$ are shown in search results.

title: Completeness of Riemann Zeta Zeros Data
snippet: This database contains the $103{,}800{,}788{,}359$ zeros of the Riemann zeta function $\zeta(s)$ with imaginary part in the interval $(0,30\,610\,046\,000]$, all of which have real part $\frac{1}{2}$.

snippet: If you use the database of Belyi maps in your research please cite: *A database of Belyi maps*, by Michael Musty, Sam Schiavone, Jeroen Sijsling, and John Voight, Proceedings of the Thirteenth Algorithmic Number Theory Symposium (ANTS-XIII), eds. Renate Scheidler and Jonathan Sorenson, Open Book Series 2, Mathematical Sciences Publishers, Berkeley, 2019, 375-392 \cite{mr:3952023}, as well as any of the references cited above that are of particular relevance.

Citation

If you use the database of higher genus curves with automorphisms in your research please cite: J. Paulhus, **A database of group actions on Riemann surfaces**, in _Birational geometry, K\"ahler-Einstein metrics and degenerations_, 693--708, Springer Proc. Math. Stat., 409, Springer, Cham, \cite{MR:4606662}, as well as any of the references cited above that are of particular relevance.
