How to cite elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$
How to cite genus 2 curves
snippet:
Citation

If you use the database of genus 2 curves in your research please cite: *A database of genus 2 curves over the rational numbers*, by Andrew R. Booker, Jeroen Sijsling, Andrew V. Sutherland, John Voight, and Dan Yasaki, Twelfth Algorithmic Number Theory Symposium (ANTS XII), LMS Journal of Computation and Mathematics 19 (2016), 235-254 \cite{mr:3540958}, as well as any of the references cited above that are of particular relevance.

title: How to cite hypergeometric motive data
How to cite Bianchi modular forms
How to cite Hilbert modular forms
Source of abstract group data
snippet: Small groups were pulled from the small group database in Magma, and rely on the following work: * O'Brien, "The groups of order $256$" \cite{MR:1128656, doi:10.1016/0021-8693(91)90261-6} * Besche and Eick, "The groups of order at most 1000 except 512 and 768" \cite{MR:1681347, doi:10.1006/jsco.1998.0259} * Besche, Eick and O'Brien, "A millennium project: constructing small groups" \cite{MR:1935567, doi:10.1142/S0218196702001115}

title: Release notes for version 1.3
snippet: These are the release notes for version 1.3 of the LMFDB.

title: Reliability of Artin representation data
snippet: The computation of Artin representations is rigorous; it does not rely on heuristics or conjectures.

title: Reliability of Weil polynomial data
snippet: Except where noted below, all Weil polynomial data has been computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on any unproven assumptions or conjectures: * Angle ranks have been computed numerically using 500 bits of precision and \cite{href{https//pari.math.u-bordeaux.fr/}{Pari/GP}}'s lindep function.

title: Reliability of Belyi map data
snippet: To ensure that the map $X \to \mathbb{P}^1$ provided is indeed a Belyi map, we first verify by taking (exact) preimages that the ramification above $\{0,1,\infty\} \subseteq \mathbb{P}^1$ is correct (in particular, it has the correct degree). We then also verify that $X$ has the correct genus; it follows by the Riemann-Hurwitz theorem that there can be no other ramification, so the map is indeed a Belyi map.

title: Reliability of Dirichlet Character Data
snippet: Dirichlet character data is rigorously computed without assumptions or heuristics.

title: Reliability of the Classical Modular Form Data
snippet: All modular form data currently in the database has been computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on any unproved assumptions or conjectures. In particular * All self twists and inner twists have been rigorously verified either by checking exact algebraic coefficients or complex analytic coefficients computed to sufficient precision to uniquely identify every such twist (as of February 2020).

title: Reliability of the Classical Modular Form Data
snippet: All modular form data currently in the database has been computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on any unproved assumptions or conjectures. In particular * All self twists and inner twists have been rigorously verified either by checking exact algebraic coefficients or complex analytic coefficients computed to sufficient precision to uniquely identify every such twist (as of February 2020).

title: Reliability of the higher genus curves with automorphisms data
snippet: All the data on higher genus curves with automorphisms was computed with rigorous algorithms and without heuristics or conjectures.

title: Reliability of elliptic curve data
snippet: The reliability of the completeness claims for the elliptic curves over each number field represented in the database depends on the field and the current status of modularity of elliptic curves over the field in question. Over all totally real fields of degree \(2\) and \(3\), and most of degree $4$, it is known that all elliptic curves are modular (see \cite{doi:10.1007/s00222-014-0550-z} for degree $2$, \cite{arxiv:1901.03436} for degree $3$ and \cite{doi:10.1090/tran/8557,arxiv:2103.13975} for degree $4$) and hence the database contains all curves of conductor norm up to the bound for…

title: Reliability of elliptic curve data over $\Q$
snippet: ### Completeness of the collection All elliptic curves defined over \(\mathbb{Q}\) are known to be modular, and hence arise (up to isogeny) via the Eichler-Shimura construction from classical modular forms of weight \(2\), trivial character, level \(N\) equal to the conductor of the curve, and having rational Fourier coefficients.

title: Reliability of Genus 2 Curve Data
snippet: As described in Section 3.4 of \cite{MR:3540958,arXiv:1602.03715}, the completeness of the genus 2 curve database for curves of absolute discriminant $|\Delta|\le 10^6$ has been tested against other tables of genus 2 curves, including those of \cite{href{http://www.mathe2.uni-bayreuth.de/stoll/data/}{Stoll}}, and Merriman and Smart \cite{doi:10.1017/S030500410007153X}.

title: Reliability of transitive group data
snippet: All data for transitive groups was computed using GAP or Magma, and so the rigor of the computations depends on the internal algorithms of these programs, none of which relies on heuristics or conjectures. Since the objects in question are finite groups, every calculation can be reproduced given sufficient computing resources.

title: Reliability of abstract groups data
snippet: All groups data has been computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on unproven assumptions or conjectures. The code to generate the data is primarily written in Magma and hence depends on the accuracy of Magma code. While rare, as we created this database we did find a few errors in Magma (and subsequently reported those errors to Magma, many of which have now been fixed).

title: Reliability of local field data
snippet: All data on local field pages was computed rigorously, i.e., without heuristics or conjectures.

title: Reliability of L-function data
snippet: TBA

title: Reliability of Curve L-function Data
snippet: L-functions of genus 2 curves over $\Q$ in the database have been precomputed using heuristic precision bounds that should (but are not guaranteed to) ensure that all zeros and special values are accurate to the displayed precision (up to an error in the last digit), and that the list of zeros is complete (within the region covered by the list, which includes the lowest zero). All L-function computations for genus 2 curves are conditional on the assumption that the L-function lies in the Selberg class, and in particular, has an analytic continuation that satisfies the functional equation.

title: Reliability of Dirichlet L-function Data
snippet: All Dirichlet L-functions in the database have been precomputed using rigorous precision bounds and interval arithmetic ensure that zeros and special values are accurate to the displayed precision (up to an error in the last digit displayed), and that the list of zeros is complete (within the region covered by the list, including the lowest zero). For Dirichlet L-functions in the database, all listed zeros are known to lie on the critcial line, and the analytic rank is known to be zero.

title: Reliability of Elliptic Curve L-function Data
snippet: L-functions of elliptic curves over $\Q$ in the database have been precomputed using heuristic precision bounds that should (but are not guaranteed to) ensure that all zeros and special values are accurate to the displayed precision (up to an error in the last digit), and that the list of zeros is complete (within the region covered by the list, including the lowest zero).

title: Reliability of Dynamically Computed L-function Data
snippet: Dynamically computed L-functions are computed on-the-fly at low precision and are meant only to indicate the general shape of the L-function. Information that cannot be computed quickly may be missing (possibly including zeros). The displayed data may be inaccurate, and in particular may not have the accuracy suggested by the displayed precision.

title: Reliability of Maass form L-function data
snippet: Data for precomputed Maass form L-functions was computed using non-rigorous heuristics. The displayed data may be inaccurate, and in particular, may not have the accuracy suggested by the displayed precision.

title: Reliability of Modular L-function Data
snippet: L-functions of classical, Hilbert, and Bianchi modular forms in the database have been precomputed using rigorous precision bounds and interval arithmetic ensure that zeros and special values are accurate to the displayed precision (up to an error in the last digit displayed), and that the list of zeros is complete (within the region covered by the list, including the lowest zero). Each zero is known to lie within a disc whose radius is determined by the displayed precision, but note that this is not sufficient to prove that the lie on the critical line.

title: Reliability of Maass forms data
snippet: Maass form data, in the LMFDB and elsewhere, is inherently heuristic. The data are decimal approximations to numbers which (in general) are conjectured to be transcendental and not expressable in terms of well-known constants, and only in a few special \cite{MR2249995} cases have those approximations been *proven* to be accurate. Nevertheless, there are reasons for believing that the available data is actually correct, which we now describe.

title: Reliability of rigorous Maass forms data
snippet: Maass form data, in the LMFDB and elsewhere, is inherently approximate. The data are decimal approximations to numbers which (in general) are conjectured to be transcendental and not expressible in terms of well-known constants. In the LMFDB, each rigorous Maass form is *proven* to be an accurate approximation to a true Maass form. Further, rigorous Maass forms are *proven* to be consecutive, i.e. there are no "missing" Maass forms with eigenvalues between known rigorous Maass forms.

title: Reliability of Bianchi modular form data
snippet: All Bianchi modular form data has been computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on any unproved assumptions or conjectures.

title: Reliability of Hilbert modular form data
snippet: All Hilbert modular form eigenform data has been computed using rigorous algorithms. - When ``is base change'' says _no_, we have certified that the form is not a base change; when _yes_, there is strong evidence of a base change (Hecke eigenvalues seem to match their conjugates under a nontrivial subgroup of automorphisms) but this has not been rigorously established.

title: Reliability of Siegel modular form data
snippet: All Siegel modular form data currently in the database has been computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on any unproved assumptions or conjectures. In addition to using mathematically rigorous algorithms whenever possible, we have performed a variety of consistency checks intended to catch any errors in the software packages used to compute modular forms data, or any errors that might have been introduced during post-processing.

title: Reliability of modular curve data
snippet: None of the data in the modular curves database depends on any heuristics or unproven conjectures, but it is still in an early beta state and should be regarded as provisional, **use with caution**! In particular, there are some **known issues with the rational points data** (the modular curve labels $\texttt{N.i.g.c.n}$ associated to rational points may have an incorrect value of $\texttt{n}$. We are in the process of correcting this data and will update this knowl once that has been done.

title: Reliability of mod-$\ell$ Galois representation data
snippet: TBA

title: Reliability of global number field data
snippet: Global number field data was computed without heuristics or conjectures with the exception of certain class numbers, class groups, and generators for unit groups where, in some cases, the generalized Riemann hypothesis was used. In cases where GRH was used, the data is marked accordingly.

title: Reliability of Shimura curve data
snippet: None of the data in the Shimura curves database depends on any heuristics or unproven conjectures, but it is still in an early alpha state and should be regarded as provisional, **use with caution**!

title: Reliability of Sato-Tate Group Data
snippet: All data in the Sato-Tate group database was computed using rigorous algorithms that do not depend on any unproved hypotheses. The completeness of the list of 52 rational Sato-Tate groups of weight 1 and degree 4 in the database is the main theorem of \cite{arXiv:1110.6638,doi:10.1112/S0010437X12000279,MR:2982436}. As noted in this paper, there are 3 additional subgroups of $\mathrm{USp}(4)$ that satisfy the Sato-Tate axioms in weight 1 but do not arise as Sato--Tate groups of abelian surfaces; these are not included in the database.

title: Reliability of Riemann Zeta Zeros Data
snippet: The imaginary part of each zero is recorded with an absolute precision of $\pm 2^{−102}$ and the completeness of the list was checked using a rigorous version of Turing's method; see \cite{MR:3315519,doi:10.1090/S0025-5718-2014-02884-6} for further details. The results have been checked to a separate list of zeros independently computed by Jan B\"uthe to an accuracy of $\pm 2^{-64}$ (this comparison found 108 minor discrepancies, each of which was resolved in favor of the data presented here).

title: Source of Artin representation data
snippet: There are two parts to computing Artin representations: computing number fields of interest, and then for each such field, computing the data for each irreducible complex representation of its Galois group. The number fields came from the LMFDB number field collection, or were computed by J. Jones and D. Roberts. The computation of Artin representation data makes heavy use of Magma code written by Tim Dokchitser.

title: Source of isogeny classes of abelian varieties over finite fields
snippet: The computation is based of the Honda-Tate theorem which states that isogeny classes of abelian varieties over finite fields are completely determined by the characteristic polynomial of their Frobenius automorphism acting on the first $\ell$-adic cohomology group. For a given dimension $g$ and base field of size $q$, a complete list of all Weil polynomials that do occur can be enumerated using a technique developed by Kedlaya \cite{mr:2459990,arxiv:math/0608104}.

title: Source of Belyi maps data
snippet: The database of Belyi maps was constructed by Michael Musty, Sam Schiavone, Jeroen Sijsling, and John Voight. A detailed description of its construction can be found in \cite{arXiv:1805.07751}.

title: Source of Dirichlet character data
snippet: Labels and invariants for Dirichlet characters and their Galois orbits are computed using [Magma code](https://github.com/AndrewVSutherland/magma) that was developed in conjunction with the paper _Computing Classical Modular Forms_ \cite{arXiv:2002.04717} by Alex J. Best, Jonathan Bober, Andrew R. Booker, Edgar Costa, John Cremona, Maarten Derickx, David Lowry-Duda, Min Lee, David Roe, Andrew V. Sutherland, and John Voight. Computations for individual Conrey characters is performed using the efficient implementation available in Pari/GP.

title: Source of Classical Modular Forms Data
snippet: Details regarding the construction of the database of classical modular forms can be found in the paper *Computing Classical Modular Forms* \cite{arXiv:2002.04717,doi:10.1007/978-3-030-80914-0_4} by Alex J. Best, Jonathan Bober, Andrew R. Booker, Edgar Costa, John Cremona, Maarten Derickx, David Lowry-Duda, Min Lee, David Roe, Andrew V. Sutherland, and John Voight.

title: Source of Classical Modular Forms Data
snippet: Details regarding the construction of the database of classical modular forms can be found in the paper *Computing Classical Modular Forms* \cite{arXiv:2002.04717,doi:10.1007/978-3-030-80914-0_4} by Alex J. Best, Jonathan Bober, Andrew R. Booker, Edgar Costa, John Cremona, Maarten Derickx, David Lowry-Duda, Min Lee, David Roe, Andrew V. Sutherland, and John Voight.

title: Source of higher genus curves with automorphisms data
snippet: A paper with a detailed description of the data is cited at the bottom of the page and may be found \cite{href{https://jenpaulhus.com/research/Paulhus-lmfdb2022.pdf}{here}}. ### Original Data of Thomas Breuer The basis for this data is the work of Thomas Breuer \cite{MR1796706} to classify all group actions on Riemann surfaces. Breuer wrote code to implement his algorithm, and ran that code in GAP up to genus 48.

title: Source of elliptic curve data
snippet: ## Source of the curves The elliptic curves over number fields other than $\Q$ come from several sources. ### Imaginary quadratic fields The curves defined over the first five (Euclidean) imaginary quadratic fields of class number one include the curves in John Cremona's 1981 thesis, extended by him with Warren Moore in 2014 and subsequently.

title: Source of elliptic curve data over $\Q$
snippet: The elliptic curves over $\Q$ in the LMFDB come from three sources: - Complete lists of elliptic curves defined over $\Q$ for a given conductor $N$ were computed for $N\le500000$ by John Cremona using the modular symbols method described in \cite{href{http://homepages.warwick.ac.uk/~masgaj/book/fulltext/index.html}{Cremona97}, MR1628193}, as implemented in his eclib package \cite{doi:10.5281/zenodo.29671}.

title: Source of Genus 2 Curve Data
snippet: The database of genus 2 curves was constructed by Andrew Booker, Jeroen Sijsling, Andrew Sutherland, John Voight, and Dan Yasaki. A detailed description of its construction can be found in \cite{doi:10.1112/S146115701600019X,arXiv:1602.03715,MR:3540958}. - Geometric invariants, minimal discriminants, automorphism groups, local solubility, number of rational Weierstrass points, 2-Selmer rank, torsion subgroup of the Jacobian, and squareness of Sha were computed using built-in Magma functions; code to reproduce these computations can be obtained on each curve's home page.

title: Source of transitive group data
snippet: Data here was computed using GAP and Magma, and relies on the following work: * Butler and McKay, "The transitive groups of degree up to eleven" \cite{MR:695893, doi:10.1080/00927878308822884} * Royle, "The transitive groups of degree twelve" \cite{MR:922391, doi:10.1016/S0747-7171(87)80068-8} * Butler, "The transitive groups of degree fourteen and fifteen" \cite{MR:1271082, doi:10.1006/jsco.1993.1056} * Hulpke, "Constructing transitive permutation groups" \cite{MR:2168238, doi:10.1016/j.jsc.2004.08.002} * Cannon and Holt, "The transitive permutation groups of degree 32" \cite{MR:2455702,…

title: Source  of abstract group  data
snippet: All groups in the database, as well as their attributes, subgroups, character tables, etc. were computed using Magma from code and algorithms developed and written by Michael Bush, Lewis Combes, John Jones, Jen Paulhus, David Roe, Manami Roy, and Sam Schiavone. The code is available on GitHub.

title: Source of hypergeometric data
snippet: Data for families of hypergeometric motives were computed by David Roberts using magma. Data for individual motives was computed by David Roberts and Mark Watkins.

title: Hilbert modular surfaces source
snippet: The database of Hilbert modular surfaces was computed by Eran Assaf, Angelica Babei, Ben Breen, Edgar Costa, Juanita Duque-Rosero, Aleksander Horawa, Jean Kieffer, Avinash Kulkarni, Grant Molnar, Sam Schiavone, and John Voight using algorithms implemented in \cite{href{http://magma.maths.usyd.edu.au/magma/}{Magma}}. The computational effort is described in \cite{arxiv:2301.10302}.

title: Source of lattice data
snippet: The database contains lattice data (Gram matrices) from the lattice.catalogue_of_lattices, maintained by Gabriele Nebe and Neil J.A. Sloane. The Catalogue contains some tables compiled by other authors, which are cited individually in the LMFDB. For lattices of class number one, completeness of the data is confirmed by a calculation of Kirschmer and Lorch (\cite{arxiv:1208.5638}, \cite{doi:10.1112/S1461157013000107}, \cite{MR3091733}). Additional data was computed in Magma and PARI using code written by Anna Haensch and Samuele Anni.

title: Source of p-adic field data
snippet: Except as noted below, all data on p-adic fields come from the computations of Jones and Roberts as described in \cite{MR:2194887}. * Galois groups in degree 12 were computed by C. Awtrey, and many of the Galois groups were confirmed using Magma packages written by C. Doris. Global splitting models were computed by Jones and Carrillo.

title: Source of L-function data
snippet: The source of the L-function data varies by origin. Additional information can be found on individual L-function pages.

title: Source of L-function Data
snippet: All L-functions of genus 2 curves over $\Q$ in the database were computed by Andy Booker using code created at the Dublin workshop on genus 2 curves. Details of these computations can be found in \cite{MR:3540958,,arXiv:1602.03715}

title: Source of Dirichlet L-function Data
snippet: The Dirichlet L-functions in the database were computed by David Platt using algorithms described in his PhD thesis \cite{\href{https://people.maths.bris.ac.uk/~madjp/thesis5.pdf}{Computing Degree-1 L-functions Rigorously}}.

title: Source of Elliptic Curve L-function Data
snippet: All L-functions of elliptic curves over $\Q$ in the database were computed by Andy Booker using code created at the Dublin workshop on genus 2 curves.

title: Source of Dynamically Computed L-function Data
snippet: Dynamically computed L-function data is computed on-the-fly at low precision using the L-function calculator provided by Sage, which is based on the $\texttt{lcalc}$ software library developed by Michael Rubenstein.

title: Source of Maass form L-function Data
snippet: The precomputed Maass form L-functions stored in the database were computed using software developed by Fredrik Stromberg to generate Dirichlet coefficients and Michael Rubinstein's $\texttt{lcalc}$ library to compute L-function data for the resulting Dirichlet series.

title: Source of Modular L-function Data
snippet: All L-functions of classical, Hilbert, and Bianchi modular forms in the database were computed by Edgar Costa using source code provided by David Platt.

title: Source of Maass forms data
snippet: The database of Maass forms was computed by Fredrik Stroemberg, Stefan Lemurell, Holger Then, and David Farmer. The algorithms are based on ideas of Stark \cite{MR0803370} and Hejhal \cite{MR1106989}. Details can be found in \cite{MR2429890}, \cite{MR2895234}, \cite{MR2164106}, and \cite{arXiv:math/0506102}.

title: Source of rigorous Maass form data
snippet: Rigorous Maass forms were computed by Kieran Child, Andrei Seymour-Howell, and David Lowry-Duda. The algorithms are based on ideas of Stark \cite{MR0803370} and Hejhal \cite{MR1106989} and described further in \cite{arXiv:2204.11761} and \cite{MR4493409}. Heuristic Maass forms were computed by Fredrik Strömberg, Stefan Lemurell, Holger Then, and David Farmer. Details can be found in \cite{MR2429890}, \cite{MR2895234}, \cite{MR2164106}, and \cite{arXiv:math/0506102}.

title: Source of Bianchi modular form data
snippet: The database of Bianchi modular forms was mostly computed by John Cremona using modular symbol algorithms developed in his [1981 DPhil thesis](http://http://www.warwick.ac.uk/staff/J.E.Cremona/theses/JCthesis-scan.pdf) (see also [J. E. Cremona, Hyperbolic tessellations, modular symbols, and elliptic curves over complex quadratic fields Compositio Mathematica, tome 51, no 3 (1984), p.

title: Source of Hilbert modular form data
snippet: The database of Hilbert modular forms was computed by Steve Donnelly and John Voight using algorithms implemented in \cite{href{http://magma.maths.usyd.edu.au/magma/}{Magma}} by Lassina Dembele, Steve Donnelly, Markus Kirschmer, and John Voight; the computational effort is described in Donnelly-Voight \cite{arXiv:1605.02637}. A detailed overview and mathematical description of these algorithms can be found in a survey by Demb\'el\'e-Voight \cite{arXiv:1010.5727}. Galois conjugate levels were added by John Cremona and Aurel Page.

title: Source of Siegel modular form data
snippet: Details regarding the construction of the database of paramodular forms can be found in the paper _A database of paramodular forms from quinary orthogonal modular forms_ [[preprint](https://math.dartmouth.edu/~jvoight/articles/paramodular-lucant-012223.pdf)] by Eran Assaf, Watson Ladd, Gustavo Rama, Gonzalo Tornaría and John Voight.

title: Source of modular curve data
snippet: The modular curves database was constructed during two workshops run by the Simons Collaboration in Arithmetic Geometry, Number Theory, and Computation in 2022. Participants included Nikola Adžaga, Eran Assaf, Jennifer Balakrishnan, Barinder Banwait, Shiva Chidambaram, Garen Chiloyan, Edgar Costa, Juanita Duque-Rosero, Noam Elkies, Asimina Hamakiotes, Sachi Hashimoto, Daniel Hast, Aashraya Jha, Timo Keller, Jean Kieffer, Jun Bo Lau, David Lowry-Duda, Alvaro Lozano-Robledo, Kimball Martin, Pietro Mercuri, Philippe Michaud-Jacobs, Grant Molnar, Steffen M\"uller, Filip Najman, Ekin Ozman, Oana…

title: Source of mod-$\ell$ Galois representation data
snippet: Data for dual pairs of algebras associated to mod-$\ell$ Galois representations were computed by Peter Bruin available at this GitHub repo.

title: Source of global number field data
snippet: Number fields were drawn from the following sources. <ul> <li>The PARI database from the Bordeaux PARI group \cite{href{https://pari.math.u-bordeaux.fr/pub/numberfields/}{pari.math.u-bordeaux.fr/pub/numberfields/}}, which in turn, was a combination of work by several authors: <ul> <li> M. Olivier for degree 3 fields; <li> J. Buchmann, D. Ford, and M. Pohst for degree 4 fields \cite{MR:1176706}; <li> A. Schwarz, M. Pohst, and F. Diaz y Diaz for degree 5 fields \cite{MR:1219705}; <li> M.

title: Source of Sato-Tate Group Data
snippet: Sato-Tate groups in weight 0 and degree 1 are computed on demand. Rational Sato-Tate groups in weight 1 and degree 2 are taken from _Hyperelliptic curves, L-polynomials, and random matrices_, by K.S. Kedlaya and A.V. Sutherland \cite{arXiv:0803.4462,doi:10.1090/conm/487,MR:2555991}. Rational Sato-Tate groups in weight 1 and degree 4 are taken from _Sato-Tate distributions and Galois endomorphism modules in genus $2$_, by F. Fité, K.S. Kedlaya, V. Rotger, and A.V. Sutherland \cite{arXiv:1110.6638,doi:10.1112/S0010437X12000279,MR:2982436}.

title: Source of Riemann Zeta Zeros Data
snippet: The database of Riemann zeta zeros was computed by David Platt using the algorithm described in \cite{MR:3315519,doi:10.1090/S0025-5718-2014-02884-6}. The imaginary part of each zero is stored with an absolute precision of $±2^{−102}$ and the completeness of the list was verified using a rigorous version of Turing's method.

title: Representation
snippet:
Browse

  • Dirichlet characters
  • Artin representations

title: Highest weight of an irreducible representation of $\GL(g,\C)$.
snippet: Let $\rho\colon \GL(g,\C)\to \GL(V)$ be a finite-dimensional irreducible representation Then there exists a unique $1$-dimensional subspace $\langle v_0 \rangle$ of $V$ and a unique $g$-tuple of integers $(\lambda_1 \geqslant\lambda_2 \geqslant \ldots \geqslant \lambda_g)$ such that $\rho(\mathrm{diag}(a_1,\ldots,a_g))\,v_0=\prod_{i=1}^{g}a_i^{\lambda_i}\, v_0$. The $g$-tuple of integers $(\lambda_1,\lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_g)$ is called ** the highest weight** of $\rho$.

title: Irreducible representations of $\GL(g,\C)$
snippet: The irreducible finite-dimensional representations of $\GL(g,\C)$ correspond bijectively to the $g$-tuples of integers $(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_g)$ with $\lambda_1\geqslant \lambda_2\geqslant\ldots\geqslant \lambda_g$ called highest weight of the representation.

title: Definition of ring
snippet: A ring is a set $R$ with two binary operations $+$ and $\cdot$ such that 1. $R$ is an abelian group with respect to $+$ 2. $\cdot$ is associative on $R$ 3. the distributive laws hold, i.e., for all $a,b,c\in R$, $$ a\cdot(b+c) = a\cdot b+a\cdot c \qquad \text{and}\qquad (b+c)\cdot a = b\cdot a+c\cdot a$$ 4. there is an identity element with respect to the operation $\cdot$, typically denoted by $1_R$ or, more simply, by $1$. The identity element of $R$ as a group with respect to $+$ is typically denoted by $0_R$ or, more simply, by $0$.

title: $A$-field
snippet: Let $A$ be a commutative ring. An **$A$-field** is an $A$-algebra that is a field.

title: Associate of an element
snippet: If $R$ is a commutative ring, two elements $x, y \in R$ are **associates** if there is a unit $u \in R$ with $y = ux$. This defines an equivalence relation on the elements of $R$.

title: Characteristic of a ring
snippet: The **characteristic** of a ring is the least positive integer $n$ for which \[ \underbrace{1+\cdots + 1}_n = 0, \] if such an $n$ exists, and $0$ otherwise. Equivalently, it is the exponent of the additive group of the ring. The characteristic of a field $k$ is either $0$ or a prime number $p$, depending on whether the prime field of $k$ is isomorphic to $\Q$ or $\F_p$.

title: Dedekind domain
snippet: A Dedekind domain $D$ is a integral domain which is not a field such that 1. $D$ is Noetherian; 2. every non-zero prime ideal is maximal; 3. $D$ is integrally closed. The ring of integers of a number field is always a Dedekind domain, as is every discrete valuation ring. In a Dedekind domain, every non-zero ideal $I$ can be written as a product of non-zero prime ideals, $$I=P_1P_2\cdots P_k,$$ and the product is unique up to the order of the factors. Repeated factors are often grouped, so we write $I=Q_1^{e_1}\cdots Q_g^{e_g}$ where the $Q_i$ are non-zero prime ideals of $D$.

title: Euclidean domain
snippet:
An **Euclidean domain** is an integral domain $R$ with a function \[ 
u: R-\{0\}	o \Z_{\geq 0} \] such that for all $a\in R$ and all $b\in R-\{0\}$, there exists $q,r\in R$ such that $a=bq+r$ and $r=0$ or $
u(r)<
u(b)$. Euclidean domains are principal ideal domains, and hence, unique factorization domains.

title: Field
snippet: A field is a commutative ring $R$ such that $0_R\neq 1_R$ and every nonzero element of $R$ has an inverse in $R$ with respect to multiplication.

title: Field of fractions of an integral domain
snippet: If $R$ is an integral domain, then its field of fractions $F$ is the smallest field containing $R$. It can be constructed by mimicking the set of fractions $a/b$ where $a,b\in R$ with $b\neq 0$ following the usual rules for fraction arithmetic. It is unique, up to unique isomorphism.

title: Field of fractions (mathlib)
snippet: This is also defined in mathlib as Fraction Ring, which is the localisation at the non-zero divisors of your ring. When your ring $R$ is a commutative ring and an integral domain then gets the fact that this is a field.

title: Fractional ideal
snippet: If $R$ is an integral domain with field of fractions $K$, then a fractional ideal $I$ of $R$ is an $R$-submodule of $K$ such that there exists $d\in R-\{0\}$ with $$dI=\{da\mid a\in I\} \subseteq R\,.$$

title: Ideal of a ring
snippet: If $R$ is a ring, a subset $I\subseteq R$ is an **ideal** of $R$ if $I$ is a subgroup of $R$ for $+$ and for all $a\in I$ and all $r\in R$, $$ r\cdot a\in I \qquad\text{and}\qquad a\cdot r\in I.$$ In a polynomial ring $R[X_1,\dots,X_n]$, an ideal is **homogeneous** if it can be generated by homogeneous polynomials.

title: Integral element of a ring
snippet: If $R\subseteq S$ are commutative rings, an element $s\in S$ is integral over $R$ if there exists $n\in\Z^+$ and $a_i\in R$ such that $$ s^n+a_{n-1} s^{n-1}+\cdots + a_0 =0\,.$$ The integral closure of $R$ in $S$ is $\{s\in S\mid s \text{ is integral over } R\}$.

title: Integral domain
snippet: An integral domain is a commutative ring $R$ such that $1_R\neq 0_R$ and $R$ contains no zero divisors.

title: Integrally closed
snippet: Let $R$ be an integral domain and $F$ its field of fractions. Then $R$ is integrally closed if $R$ equals the integral closure of $R$ in $F$.

title: Irreducible element
snippet: An element $x \ne 0$ of a commutative ring $R$ is **irreducible** if it is not a unit and has the property that whenever $x=yz$ for some $y,z \in R$, either $y$ or $z$ is a unit.

title: Maximal ideal
snippet: In a ring $R$, an ideal $M$ is **maximal** if $M\neq R$ and for all ideals $I$ of $R$, $$ M\subseteq I \subseteq R\implies M=I\quad\text{or}\quad I=R.$$

title: Noetherian ring
snippet: A commutative ring $R$ is **Noetherian** if for every ascending chain of ideals $$ I_1\subseteq I_2\subseteq I_3\subseteq \cdots $$ there exists $N$ such that for all $n\geq N$, $I_n=I_N$.

title: Prime ideal
snippet: If $R$ is a commutative ring $R$, an ideal $I \ne R$ is prime if for all $a,b\in R$, $$ ab\in I \implies a\in I \quad \text{or}\quad b\in I.$$

title: Principal fractional ideal
snippet: Let $R$ be an integral domain with field of fractions $K$. If $a\in K^\times$, then the **principal fractional ideal** generated by $a$ is the set $$ \{ar\mid r\in R\}\,.$$

title: Principal ideal domain
snippet: A **principal ideal domain**, or PID, is an integral domain $R$ such that every ideal of $R$ is of the form $\langle a\rangle$ for some $a\in R$. Every such ring is a unique factorization domain.

title: Unique factorization domain
snippet: A **unique factorization domain**, or UFD, is an integral domain $R$ such that for every non-zero non-unit $a\in R$ there exist irreducibles $p_1, \ldots, p_n$ such that \[ a = p_1\cdots p_n,\] and this representation is unique up to the order of the factors and up to associates.

title: Unit in a ring
snippet: A **unit** in a commutative ring $R$ is an element $x \in R$ so that there exists $y \in R$ with $xy = 1$. The set of units in $R$ is denoted $R^*$ or $R^\times$ and forms a group under multiplication.

title: Zero divisor
snippet: An element $a$ in a ring $R$ is a **zero divisor** if $a\neq 0_R$ and there exists an element $b\in R-\{0_R\}$ such that $$ a\cdot b = 0_R \qquad \text{or}\qquad b\cdot a = 0_R.$$

title: Sage Mathematical Software System
snippet: [SageMath](http://www.sagemath.org) is a free open source mathematics software system originally created by William Stein.

title: Sage cell server
snippet: The **Sage Cell Server** is an open-source, scalable web interface to SageMath. It can be used to embed Sage computations into any webpage. Feel free to modify the Sage commands in the white text entry area and use the Sage Cell Server for short computations.

title: Sage cell knowl
snippet: This is a knowl which produces a Sage Cell Server. For technical reasons, it should always be called with at least one parameter such as n=1. One can specify sage code to be preloaded into the cell with an argument such as code='2^123'.

title: Discriminant of a Shimura curve
snippet: The discriminant of a Shimura curve is the discriminant of the associated quaternion algebra.

title: Level of a Shimura curve
snippet: The level of a Shimura curve is the level of the associated Eichler order.

title: Shimura curve
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$. Let $\mathcal{H}^\pm \colonequals \C \smallsetminus \R$ be the union of the complex upper and lower half planes. Since $B$ is indefinite, we may fix an embedding $B^\times \hookrightarrow \GL_2(\R)$, and so $B^\times$ acts on $\mathcal{H}^\pm$ by M\"obius transformations. We define \[ \widehat{B} \colonequals B\otimes_{\Z} \widehat{\Z}, \] \[ \widehat{O} \colonequals O\otimes_{\Z} \widehat{\Z}.

title: $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra over $\Q$. Let $N_{B^{\times}}(O)=\{\gamma\in B^{\times}:\gamma^{-1}O\gamma=O\}$ be the normalizer of $O$ in $B$. Then the **automorphisms of $O$ preserving the polarization $\mu$ (up to sign)** is defined by $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)=\{\gamma\in N_{B^{\times}}(O)/\Q^{\times}:\gamma^{-1}\mu\gamma=\pm\mu\}$.

title: $B\langle i,j\rangle$
snippet: A quaternion algebra $B$ over a field $F$ of characteristic not equal to 2 has elements $i,j \in B$ such that $\{1,i,j,ij\}$ is an $F$-basis for $B$, and such that there exists $a,b \in F$ with $i^2 = a$, $j^2 = b$, and $ji = -ij$. In this case, we write \[ B = B\langle i,j\rangle = \left(\frac{a,b}{F}\right). \]

title: Isogeny decomposition of the Jacobian of a Shimura curve
snippet: As with any abelian variety, the Jacobian $J_H$ of a Shimura curve $X_H$ can be decomposed into simple isogeny factors. For Shimura curves, these simple isogeny factors are modular abelian varieties corresponding to Galois orbits of weight 2 newforms. We list two types of information about the **isogeny decomposition** of $J_H$: - the multiset of dimensions of the simple isogeny factors: $1^3\cdot 2^2$ denotes $3$ factors of dimension $1$ and $2$ factors of dimension $2$; - the multiset of newforms corresponding to the modular abelian varieties in the isogeny decomposition, listed by label:…

title: Discriminant of a quaternion algebra
snippet: Let $B$ be a quaternion algebra over $\Q$. We say that $B$ is **ramified** at a prime $p$ if $B\otimes_{\Q} \Q_p$ is a division algebra over $\Q_p$; otherwise, we say that $B$ is **split** at $p$. The **discriminant** $\operatorname{disc}(B)$ of $B$ is the product of primes $p$ at which $B$ is ramified.

title: Discriminant of a quaternion order
snippet: Let $B$ be a quaternion algebra over $\Q$, and let $O\subset B$ be an order. Let $x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4 \in O$ be a $\Z$-basis of $O$. The **discriminant** of $O$ is the integer \[ \operatorname{disc}(O) \colonequals \left|\det(\operatorname{trd}(x_ix_j))_{i,j}\right|. \] where $\operatorname{trd} : B \to \Q$ is the reduced trace. The **reduced discriminant** of $O$ is the positive integer $\operatorname{discrd}(O)$ such that \[ \operatorname{discrd}(O)^2 = \operatorname{disc}(O). \]

title: Eichler Orders
snippet: An **Eichler order** $O$ inside a quaternion algebra $B$ is the intersection of two (not necessarily distinct) maximal orders. Over $\Q$, the reduced discriminant of an Eichler order $O$ is of the form $\mathrm{disc}(O)=D\cdot M$ with $\gcd(D,M)=1$, in which case we say $O$ is **Eichler of level $M$**.

title: Elliptic point on a Shimura curve
snippet: An **elliptic point** of a Shimura curve $X_H$ is a point with a nontrivial stabilizer group (that can be of order $2, 3, 4$, or $6$).

title: Enhanced group associated to a polarized quaternion order
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized quaternion order. We define $\widehat{O} \colonequals O\otimes_\Z \widehat{\Z}$. Then $\widehat{O}^\times$ is a profinite group with \[ \widehat{O}^\times = \lim_{\xleftarrow[N]{}} \modstar{O}{NO}, \] where $N$ ranges over all positive integers. We define \[ \Aut_{\pm\mu}(O) \colonequals \{\gamma \in N_{B^\times}(O)/\Q^\times : \gamma^{-1}\mu\gamma = \pm \mu\}. \] Then $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$ acts on $\widehat{O}^\times$. The **enhanced group (associated to $(O,\mu)$)** is the semidirect product $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$.

title: Genus of a Shimura curve
snippet: By the **genus** of a Shimura curve $X_H$, we mean the genus of any of its geometric components.

title: Genus-rank difference
snippet: The **genus-rank difference** of a Shimura curve $X_H$ of positive genus is the integer obtained by subtracting the analytic rank of $X_H$ from its genus. This difference is of interest because the methods available to compute the set of rational points on $X_H$ depend critically on whether the Mordell-Weil rank of its Jacobian is less than, equal to, or greater than its genus (one expects the Mordell-Weil rank to equal the analytic rank of $X_H$ and the later is much more feasible to compute).

title: $K$-gonality of a Shimura curve
snippet: The **$K$-gonality** $\gamma$ of a Shimura curve $X_H$ is the gonality of any of its geometric components over $K$. (The geometric gonality of that component might be smaller than the $K$-gonality.)

title: Index of a Shimura curve
snippet: The **index** of a Shimura curve $X_H$ is the index of the corresponding open compact subgroup $H \le \Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$ in the enhanced semidirect product.

title: Invariants of a Shimura curve
snippet: Invariants of a Shimura curve $X_H$ include its - underlying quaternion order - level - index - genus all of which can be derived group-theoretically as properties of $H$. Geometric invariants of the curve $X_H$ include - gonality

title: Is coarse
snippet: We say a subgroup $H\leq\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)\ltimes(O/NO)^{\times}$ is **coarse** if $H$ contains the projection of the kernel of the map $f: \Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)\ltimes O^{\times}\to N_{B^{\times}}(O)/\Q^{\times}$ defined by $(w,x)\mapsto w\cdot x$.

title: Label of a Shimura curve
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra $B$ over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$. Let $H \le \Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$ be an open compact subgroup of the enhanced semidirect product of level $N$. The **label** of the Shimura Curve $X_H$ has the form $\mathtt{D.d.\delta.N.i.g.c.n}$, where - $D$ is the discriminant of $B$, - $d$ is the reduced discriminant of $O$, which is omitted when $O$ is maximal, - $\delta$ is the degree of the polarized element $\mu$, - $N$ is the level of $H$, - $i$ is the index of $H$, - $g$ is the genus of $X_H$, - $c$…

title: Level of a Shimura curve
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized quaternion order, and let $G \colonequals \Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$ be the corresponding enhanced semidirect product. Let $H \le G$ be an open compact subgroup. The **level** of $H$ is the least positive integer $N$ such that $H$ contains \[ \ker(\widehat{O}^\times \to \modstar{O}{NO}). \] The **level** of a Shimura curve $X_H$ is the level of the corresponding open compact subgroup $H$.

title: Local obstruction
snippet: A Shimura curve $X_H/\Q$ has a **local obstruction** (to rational points) if it has no real points, or if it has no $\Q_p$-points for some prime $p$. When $X$ has genus $0$ the absence of local obstructions guarantees the existence of rational points, but this is not true in general.

title: Polarization degree of a polarized order
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized quaternion order. The **polarization degree** $\deg(\mu)$ is the square-free part of the integer $\operatorname{nrd}(\mu)/\operatorname{discrd}(O)$. Here, $\operatorname{nrd} : B \to \Q$ is the reduced norm, and $\operatorname{discrd}(O)$ is the reduced discriminant of $O$.

title: Quaternion order
snippet: Let $B$ be a quaternion algebra over $\Q$. An **order** $O\subset B$ is a $\Z$-lattice in $B$ that is also a subring of $B$ (where we require $1 \in O$). We say that $O$ is **maximal** if it is not properly contained in another order of $B$. Unlike orders in number fields, there is never a unique maximal order in a rational quaternion algebra; however, the number of $B^\times$-conjugacy classes is finite (and in many cases, there is exactly one $B^\times$-conjugacy class).

title: Polarized quaternion order
snippet: Let $O$ be an order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra $B$ over $\Q$. Let $\operatorname{trd} : B \to \Q$ be the reduced trace. A **polarization** of $O$ is an element $\mu \in B^\times$ such that $\mu^2 \in \Z_{<0}$ and for all $x \in O$, we have $\operatorname{trd}(\mu x) \in \Z$. A **polarized order** is a pair $(O,\mu)$ where $O$ is an order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra $B$ over $\Q$ and $\mu \in B^\times$ is a polarization of $O$.

title: Quaternion algebra
snippet: Let $F$ be a field. A **quaternion algebra** over $F$ is a central simple algebra of dimension 4 over $F$. A quaternion algebra $B$ over a field $F$ of characteristic not equal to 2 has elements $i,j \in B$ such that $\{1,i,j,ij\}$ is an $F$-basis for $B$, and such that there exists $a,b \in F^\times$ with $i^2 = a$, $j^2 = b$, and $ji = -ij$. In this case, we write \[ B = \left(\frac{a,b}{F}\right). \] We set $k = ij$. We say that a quaternion algebra $B$ over $F$ is **split** if $B$ is isomorphic to the matrix algebra $\operatorname{M}_2(F)$, and we say $B$ is **nonsplit** otherwise.

title: Analytic rank
snippet: The **analytic rank** of a Shimura curve is the order of vanishing of its L-function at its central point, which is equal to the sums of the analytic ranks of the L-functions of the simple modular abelian varieties corresponding to Galois orbits of modular forms that are the isogeny factors of its Jacobian. The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture for modular abelian varieties implies that the analytic rank is equal to the Mordell-Weil rank of the Jacobian.

title: Rational points on a Shimura curve
snippet: Genus zero curves with a rational point are isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ and therefore have infinitely many rational points. Genus one curves with a rational point whose Jacobian has positive Mordell-Weil rank are isomorphic to an elliptic curve with infinitely many rational points. In all other cases, the set of rational points is finite.

title: Simple Shimura curves
snippet: A Shimura curve $X_H$ is **simple** if its Jacobian is a simple abelian variety.

title: Standard Shimura curves
snippet: Let $D$ and $N$ denote positive integers with $D$ squarefree. Standard Shimura curves include: $X(D;1)$, $X(D;N)$, $X^*(D,1)$, $X^*(D,N)$, $X(D,M;1)$.

title: $X(D;1)$
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a maximal polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$. $X(D;1)$ is the Shimura curve corresponding to $H=\{1\}\leq$ $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$ $\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$.

title: $X^*(D;1)$
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a maximal polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$. $X^*(D;1)$ is the Shimura curve corresponding to $H=$$\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$$\leq$ $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$$\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$.

title: $X(D,M;1)$
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra that is Eichler of level $M$ over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$. $X(D,M;1)$ is the Shimura curve corresponding to $H=\{1\}\leq$ $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$ $\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$.

title: $X(D;N)$
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a maximal polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$ and $N$ be a positive integer. $X(D;N)$ is the Shimura curve corresponding to the subgroup $H\leq$ $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$$\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$ arising from the inverse image of $(O/NO)^{\times}$.

title: $X^*(D;N)$
snippet: Let $(O,\mu)$ be a maximal polarized quaternion order in an indefinite division quaternion algebra over $\Q$ of discriminant $D$. $X^*(D;N)$ is the Shimura curve corresponding to $H\leq$ $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$$\ltimes \widehat{O}^\times$ arising from the inverse image of $\Aut_{\pm\mu}(O)$$\ltimes(O/NO)^{\times}$.

title: Euler gamma function
snippet: The **(Euler) gamma function $\Gamma(z)$** is defined by the integral $$ \Gamma(z) = \int_0^{ \infty } e^{-t} t^{z} \frac{dt}{t} $$ for Re$(z) > 0$. It satisfies the functional equation $$ \Gamma(z+1) = z \Gamma(z), $$ and can thus be continued into a meromorphic function on the complex plane, whose poles are at the non-positive integers $\{0,-1,-2,\ldots\}$.

title: $K$-Bessel functions
snippet:
For $x\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}$ and $
u\in\mathbb{C}$ we define the **$K$-Bessel functions** \[ K_
u(x)= rac{1}{2}\int_0^\infty t^{
u-1}e^{-(x/2)(t+1/t)}dt. \]

title: Poincaré series
snippet: Let $G$ be a subgroup of $\Gamma$ of finite index $m$, $v$ a multiplier system on $G$ and $k\geq3\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $v(-I)=(-1)^k$. Let $s$ be a cusp representative of $G$ with width $w$ such that $s=\gamma_s(\infty)$ for some $\gamma_s\in\Gamma$ and let $G_s=G\cap\gamma_s\Gamma_\infty\gamma_s^{-1}$ be the *stabilizer* of the cusp $s$. Assume that $v$ is trivial on $G_s$.

title: Ambient group of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: A Sato-Tate group of weight $w$ and degree $d$ is a subgroup of either: - $\mathrm{USp}(d)$, if $w$ is odd (in which case $d$ must be even); - $\mathrm{O}(d)$, if $w$ is even (in which case $d$ may be even or odd). Here $\mathrm{USp}(d)\subseteq \mathrm{GL}_d(\mathbb{C})$ denotes the group of $d\times d$ complex matrices that are both unitary ($\bar A A^{\rm t}=I$) and symplectic ($A^{\rm t}\Omega A = \Omega$ for some fixed symplectic form $\Omega$), while $\mathrm{O}(d)\subseteq\mathrm{GL}_d(\mathbb{C})$ is the group of $d\times d$ orthogonal matrices ($AA^{\rm t}=I$).

title: Component group of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **component group** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ is the quotient $G/G^0$ of $G$ by its identity component $G^0$, which is a normal subgroup of finite index. If $G$ is the Sato-Tate group of a motive defined over a number field $K$, then the component group $G/G^0$ is canonically isomorphic to the Galois group of a finite extension $L/K$. For the motive attached to an abelian variety $A$ of dimension at most 3, $L$ is the smallest field over which all endomorphisms of $A_{\overline{K}}$ (the base change of $A$ to an algebraic closure of $K$) are defined.

title: Components of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: As a compact Lie group, a Sato-Tate group $G$ need not be connected, but it necessarily consists of a finite set of components, each of which is a coset of the identity component $G^0$, a normal subgroup. The number of components is equal to the cardinality of the component group $G/G^0$.

title: Connected Sato-Tate group
snippet: A Sato-Tate group $G$ is **connected** if it is connected as a topological group. In this case it is equal to its identity component $G^0$ and the component group $G/G^0$ is trivial.

title: Sato-Tate group data
snippet: This knowl can display data for a Sato-Tate group specified by its label.

title: Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **Sato-Tate group** of a motive $X$ is a compact Lie group $G$ containing (as a dense subset) the image of a representation that maps Frobenius elements to conjugacy classes. When $X$ is an Artin motive, $G$ corresponds to the image of the Artin representation; when $X$ is an abelian variety over a number field, one can define $G$ in terms of an $\ell$-adic Galois representation attached to $X$. For motives of even weight $w$ and degree $d$, the Sato-Tate group is a compact subgroup of the orthogonal group $\mathrm{O}(d)$.

title: Degree of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **degree** $d$ of a Sato-Tate group is the degree of the characteristic polynomials of its elements, equivalently, the dimension of the $d\times d$ matrices it contains. For an abelian variety $A$ over a number field, the degree $d$ of its Sato-Tate group is twice its dimension $g$ as an abelian variety (if $A=\mathrm{Jac}(C)$ is the Jacobian of a curve $C$, then $g$ is also the genus of $C$). The degree $d=2g$ is then also the degree of the characteristic polynomials of the Frobenius endomorphism of the reductions of $A$ modulo good primes.

title: Embedding of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: In order to explicitly compute moments and other invariants of a Sato-Tate group it is necessary to specify (up to conjugacy) exactly how it is embedded in its ambient group; for example, subgroups that are isomorphic as real Lie groups may have different distributions of characteristic polynomials.

title: First $a_2$ moment
snippet: The **first $a_2$ moment** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ is the expected value of the quadratic coefficient of the characteristic polynomial of a random element of $G$ (under its Haar measure). When $G$ is the Sato-Tate group of an abelian variety, the first $a_2$ moment is equal to the rank of its Néron–Severi group \cite{arxiv:1910.00518,mr:4038255}.

title: Fourth trace moment
snippet: The **fourth trace moment** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ is the expected value of the fourth power of the trace of a random element of $G$ (under the Haar measure). By a result known as "Larsen's alternative" \cite{mr:2058618}, if the fourth trace moment is less than or equal to 5 then $G$ is irreducible, and for $G$ of odd weight, the fourth trace moment is 3 when $G=\mathrm{USp}(2g)$ and greater than 3 otherwise.

title: Generators of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The generators of a Sato-Tate group $G$ are a set $S$ of matrices $g$ that together with the identity component $G^0$ generate $G$. The group $\langle S\rangle$ generated by $S$ necessarily contains representatives of every element of the component group but it will typically be larger (but not when $G^0$ is the trivial group, as in weight $0$).

title: Hodge circle
snippet: For a Sato-Tate group $G$ of degree $d$ and weight $w$ a **Hodge circle** is a continuous homomorphism \[ h\colon \mathrm{U}(1)\to G^0 \] for which there exist nonnegative integers $h^{p,q}$ satisfying $\sum h^{p,q}=d$ with $p,q$ varying over integers in $[0,w]$ such that - $h(u)$ has eigenvalues $u^{p-q}$ with multiplicity $h^{p,q}$ for all $u\in \mathrm{U}(1)$; - the conjugates of the image of $h$ generate a dense subgroup of $G^0$. When $G$ is the Sato-Tate group of a motive $X$ of weight $w$, the $h^{p,q}$ are the Hodge numbers of $X$.

title: Identity component of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **identity component** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ is the connected component $G^0$ of the identity element. As a compact Lie group, the identity component $G^0$ is a normal subgroup of finite index. The quotient $G/G^0$ is the component group of $G$.

title: Index of the identity component
snippet: The identity component $G^0$ of a compact Lie group $G$ is a normal subgroup of finite index; this index is equal to the cardinality of the component group $G/G^0$.

title: Sato-Tate group invariants
snippet: The invariants of a Sato-Tate group $G$ include: - the ambient group $\mathrm{USp}(d)$ or $\mathrm{O}(d)$ containing $G$; - the weight $w$ of $G$ (a nonnegative integer); - the degree $d$ of $G$ (a positive integer); - whether $G$ is connected or not.

title: Sato-Tate group labels
snippet: In general Sato-Tate group labels have the form $w.d.A.c.ns$, where * $w$ is the weight (nonnegative integer); * $d$ is the degree (positive integer, even if $w$ is odd); * $A$ is an uppercase letter that identifies the identity component among those of weight $w$ and degree $d$ (ordered by moment simplex); * $c$ is the number of components (positive integer); * $n$ is the second digit of the GAP id $[c,n]$ of the component group (positive integer); * $s$ is a lowercase letter used to distinguish groups for which all the preceding invariants coincide (ordered by moment simplex).

title: Moment matrix
snippet: The **moment matrix** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ is the matrix of moments $\mathrm{E}[\chi_i\chi_j]$ with $\chi_i$ and $\chi_j$ ranging over the irreducible characters of the ambient group that contains $G$; The irreducible characters are ordered according to their highest weight, which can be written as a monomial in the elementary symmetric functions; these are ordered by (unweighted) degree and then by reverse lexicographic order of exponent vectors. The **diagonal** of the moment matrix is the vector $(\mathrm{E}[\chi_i^2])$.

title: Moment simplex
snippet: For a Sato-Tate group $G$ of odd weight and degree $2g$ with $g>1$ it is natural to organize the mixed moments \[ \mathrm{E}[a_1^{e_1}\cdots a_g^{e_g}] \] by weighted degree $\sum ie_i$; here $a_i$ denotes the $i$th symmetric function of the eigenvalues of a random element of $G$ (equivalently, the $i$th coefficient of a random characteristic polynomial). We may restrict to cases where every term in the weighted degree sum is even, since symmetry constraints will force the expectation to be zero otherwise.

title: Moment statistics
snippet: **Moment statistics** refers to moments of random variables associated to a st_group.definition $G$, where the distribution of the random variable is defined using the pushforward of the Haar measure of $G$. Standard random variables one can associated to $G$ include the coefficients $a_i$ of the characteristic polynomial $\sum a_ix^i$ of a random matrix $g\in G$, the $i$th symmetric function $e_i$ of the eigenvalues $\lambda_j$ of $g$, and the powers sums $s_i:=\sum \lambda_j^i$ which can be expressed as polynomials in the $a_i$ or $e_i$ via the Newton identities.

title: Name of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The names of the Sato-Tate groups of weight 1 and degree 4 are taken from \cite{arXiv:1110.6638,doi:10.1112/S0010437X12000279}.

title: Event probabilities
snippet: If $a_i:=a_i(g)$ denotes the $i$th coefficient of the characteristic polynomial $\sum a_ix^i$ of a random matrix $g$ in a Sato-Tate group $G$ with Haar measure $\mu$, then \[ \mathrm{Pr}[a_i=n] := \mu(\{g\in G|a_i(g)=n\}) \] can be viewed as the **probability** that a random $g\in G$ satisfies $a_i(g)=n$. This is a rational number equal to the proportion of connected components of G on which $a_i$ is identically equal to $n$, by Proposition 8.4.3.3 of Serre's *Lecture's on $N_X(p)$* \cite{doi:10.1201/b11315}. This probability is necessarily zero unless $n$ is one of a finite set of integers.

title: Rational Sato-Tate group
snippet: A Sato-Tate group $G$ of degree $d$ is said to be **rational** if for every irreducible character $\chi$ of $\GL_d(\C)$ and every component $H$ of $G$ we have $$ \int_H \chi(g)\mu_H \in \Z, $$ where $\mu_H$ denotes the restriction to $H$ of the Haar measure $\mu$ on $G$, normalized so that $\mu_H(H)=1$. If $G$ is the Sato-Tate group of an arithmetic L-function $$ L(s)=\prod_{\mathfrak p}L_\mathfrak{p}(N(\mathfrak{p})^{-s})^{-1}, $$ this means that the polynomials $L_{\mathfrak p}(T)$ have integer coefficients, equivalently, the $L$-function $L(s)$ is rational.

title: Real dimension of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **real dimension** of a Sato-Tate group is its dimension as a real Lie group.

title: Find a Sato-Tate group
snippet: To find a specific Sato-Tate group, enter its label or name

title: Second trace moment
snippet: The **second trace moment** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ is the expected value of the square of the trace of a random element of $G$ (under its Haar measure). When $G$ is the Sato-Tate group of an abelian variety, this is equal to the dimension of its endomorphism algebra \cite{arxiv:1910.00518,mr:4038255}.

title: Maximal subgroups
snippet: For a Sato-Tate group $G$, its **maximal subgroups** are the proper subgroups $H$ of $G$ of finite index that are not contained in any other proper subgroup of $G$; such groups necessarily have the same identity component $H^0=G^0$ as $G$.

title: Sato-Tate subgroups and supergroups
snippet: For a fixed weight $w$ and degree $d$, the set of (conjugacy classes of) Sato-Tate groups $G$ with the same identity component $G^0$ form a partially ordered set under inclusion. This partially ordered set is finite if we restrict to rational Sato-Tate groups, but it need not contain a unique maximal element. The set of maximal subgroups of a Sato-Tate group is always finite, since component groups are finite, but the list of minimal supergroups is not finite in general.

title: Completeness of Sato-Tate group data
snippet: The database currently contains all rational Sato-Tate groups of weight 1 and degree up to 6, as well as all Sato-Tate groups of weight 0 and degree 1 with component group of order at most $10^{20}$.

title: Minimal supergroups
snippet: The **minimal supergroups** of a Sato-Tate group $G$ are the groups $H$ that properly contain $G$ with finite index and do not properly contain any other proper supergroup of $G$; they necessarily have the same identity component $H^0=G^0$ as $G$. For rational Sato-Tate groups in the LMFDB we list only rational minimal supergroups. A rational Sato-Tate group is **maximal** if it has no supergroups.

title: Symplectic form
snippet: A **symplectic form** on a vector space $V$ over a field $k$ is a non-degenerate alternating bilinear form $\omega\colon V\times V\to k$. This means that - if $\omega(u,v)=0$ for all $v\in V$ then $u=0$ (non-degenerate); - $\omega(v,v)=0$ for all $v\in V$ (alternating); - $\omega(\lambda u+v,w)=\lambda\omega(u,v)+\omega(v,w)$ and $\omega(u,\lambda v+w)=\omega(u)+\lambda\omega(v,w)$ for all $\lambda\in k$, $u,v,w\in V$ (bilinear).

title: Trace moments of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The trace $t$ of a random element of a Sato-Tate group $G$ can be viewed as a random variable whose distribution is given by the pushforward of the Haar measure on $G$ under the trace map. The $n$th **moment** $\mathrm{M}_n[t]:= \mathrm{E}[t^n]$ of $t$ is the expected value of the $n$th power of the trace, which is always an integer. For Sato-Tate groups of odd weight we have $t=-a_1$ and the moment sequences of $t$ and $a_1$ coincide because their distributions are symmetric about zero.

title: Trace zero density of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **trace zero density** of a Sato-Tate group is the probability that a randomly sampled element (chosen according to the Haar measure) has trace zero. On each component of a Sato-Tate group, the trace is either constant and equal to zero, or varies continuously taking each particular value with probability zero; so this probability is simply the ratio of the number of components on which the trace is identically zero to the total number of components.

title: Unitary symplectic group
snippet: For a positive even integer $d$ the **unitary symplectic group** $\mathrm{USp}(d)$ is the group of unitary transformations of a $d$-dimensional $\C$-vector space equipped with a symplectic form $\Omega$. In other words, the subgroup of $\GL_d(\mathbb C)$ whose elements $A$ satisfy: - $A^{-1} = \bar A^{\intercal}$ (unitary); - $A^\intercal \Omega A=\Omega$ (symplectic). It is a compact real Lie group that can also be viewed as the intersection of $\mathrm{U}(d)$ and $\mathrm{Sp}(d,\C)$ in $\GL_d(\C)$.

title: Weight of a Sato-Tate group
snippet: The **weight** $w$ of the Sato-Tate group $G$ of a motive $X$ is determined by the cohomology group $H^w(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$ used to define $G$. For a prime of norm $q$, the characteristic polynomial of Frobenius is a Weil $q^w$-polynomial.

title: Buckets for statistics
snippet: When creating a statistics display, you can specify buckets for each variable. Rather than creating a separate entry for every possible value of the variable, distinct values will be grouped.

title: Proportions for statistics
snippet: When creating a statistics display, you can choose how proportions are computed. For each box in the resulting display (ie a set of values for the variables), let $N_B$ be the number of objects satisfying the given constraint and in that box. * **Vs unconstrained**: each entry will show the ratio of $N_B$ out of all objects in the box. * **By row**: each entry will show the ratio of $N_B$ by the sum of all other $N_{B'}$ in the same row. * **By column**: each entry will show the ratio of $N_B$ by the sum of all other $N_{B'}$ in the same column. * **None**: No proportions will be shown.

title: Totals for statistics
snippet: When creating a statistics display, you can specify whether totals are shown. If two variables are selected, you can independently specify whether row and column totals are shown.

title: Table of first zeros of L-function
snippet: These tables contain the first positive zero of each Dirichlet L-function with level under 5000 and each elliptic curve L-function with conductor less than 200000. This data was computed using lcalc 1.23, and no specific claim is made about accuracy. Most of the printed digits should be correct, but the last few might be wrong in some cases.
